Though there are “other communities that might not be as supportive to their leadership” taking a confrontational stance, Elicker said he feels he has New Haven’s support. “ We have a good team and we’ll continue to respond to opportunities that come in the future,” Elicker said. “Things are so volatile and unpredictable right now, it’s hard to come up with a specific plan.” Elicker was first sworn into office in January 2020.

title: Unilever research facility set to harness New Haven biotech scene
subtitle: The multinational consumer goods company, whose portfolio includes Dove, Axe and Vaseline, announced plans last week to invest $270 million in an innovation center at 2 Church St. Local leaders touted the investment as a boon for the city.
author: Evelyn Ronan, Nellie Kenney
text: The consumer goods company Unilever will invest $270 million in a new research and development center in downtown New Haven, the company announced in a press conference last Thursday. The center is set to open at 2 Church St. in 2029. At the conference, which was livestreamed , Mayor Justin Elicker and Gov. Ned Lamont SOM ’80 heralded the investment as a sign of growth for New Haven and the state. Elicker lauded the city’s “booming” bioscience sector, which Unilever hopes to harness in studying “skin biology, the microbiome and the skin-brain axis,” according to a press release. In a statement emailed to the News, Leo Aquino, the company’s senior vice president of research and development, cited New Haven’s “ecosystem” of universities and quantum computing investment as a reason for the move. The center will apply “AI-powered, digitally integrated science” to product development and will house “formulation, fragrance, packaging, consumer insights, clinical testing” all “under one roof,” Aquino wrote. The Church Street location is set to employ around 300 workers. The Unilever development news arrives amid a steady flow of biotech developments in New Haven, funded by both Yale and the state. In September, Connecticut announced a $50 million grant to facilitate biotech growth in the city, alongside separate grants for startups QuantumCT and BioCT, which received $10 million and $3 million from the state, respectively. The building at 2 Church St. is set to be constructed by Winstanley Enterprises, a commercial real estate developer that has acquired properties across New Haven, CT Insider reported . In 2013, Winstanley Enterprises began construction on a tower at 100 College St. which now houses Yale’s psychology and neuroscience departments and Alexion Pharmaceuticals, a biopharmaceutical company. In 2024, the group finished construction on a building at 101 College St. which also houses Alexion, as well as biotech lab facilities. Both are located about a quarter of a mile from 2 Church St., where the building housing Unilever is set to stand. “New Haven places us at the center of some of the world’s leading medical schools and research institutes — including Yale — and we fully intend to build on those relationships as the center develops,” Aquino wrote. Becca Schelling GRD ’29, the director of content for the Yale Biotech Club, suggested that the Unilever investment could make the city a more attractive destination for recent graduates. The club supports student engagement with the local biotech sector by partnering with companies through externship and informational programs, Schelling said in a phone interview. Having more biotech companies in downtown New Haven “makes it easier” for the club to “facilitate those interactions with students,” she said. Although Schelling said that she had “not seen” members of the club staying in the city after graduating, she guessed that “if the opportunities presented themselves in New Haven, people would take them.” The Connecticut Department of Economic and Community Development will contribute a $10 million loan to support the construction of the 2 Church St. building, part of an effort to retain jobs for Connecticut residents, CT Insider reported. The new lab will replace Unilever’s longtime research and development lab in nearby Trumbull, Connecticut. David Schleicher, a Yale Law School professor who focuses on local government and property law, wrote in an email to the News that Unilever could have relocated out-of-state, like General Electric did in 2016 when it left Fairfield, Connecticut. A News article from the time described criticism that painted General Electric’s departure as an example of “the state’s chronic inability to grow its high-tech industry to full potential.” Ward 7 Alder Christine Kim ’99 wrote in a Monday email to the News that an “anchor tenant” of Unilever’s scale would mean “daytime population, foot traffic for our small businesses, and energy on blocks that have had vacancies.” Kim’s ward includes 2 Church St. as well as a cluster of nearby Winstanley bioscience developments. Kim added that she is keeping an eye on the “community impacts” of the center. “These are world-class scientists and engineers, many with families, who’ll need housing in an already-tight market. I don’t want our growth to price out the residents who built this city,” she wrote. Kim’s conversations with constituents about the future Unilever center have yielded both “genuine excitement about jobs and a livelier downtown” and “real questions about housing costs, parking, who gets hired, and what the public puts in versus gets back,” she wrote, noting that many are still “taking it in.” Aquino did not comment on which future roles would be filled by Unilever employees currently based in Trumbull and which would be filled through local hiring, but he noted that “pinpointing an area that worked for talent retention and attracting future talent” was one of the “key elements” in choosing the New Haven location. When asked about affordability concerns, Elicker wrote that New Haven is a “growing city” and is “committed to growing inclusively.” Elicker also pointed to the city’s goal of constructing 10,000 new housing units by 2035, a goal which New Haven is “well on our way” to achieving, he wrote. The 2 Church St. building will contribute millions of dollars in tax revenue to help support municipal services, Elicker wrote, adding that constructions at 100 College St. and 101 College St. contributed “more than the annual budget for the Parks Department or New Haven Free Public Library” in taxes to the city last year. Schleicher wrote that he did not think Unilever’s plans would substantially alter the city’s housing market, noting that Yale University and Yale New Haven Hospital each employ more than 10,000 employees, much more than Unilever’s expected staff of 300. Unilever will share the 2 Church St. space with biotech startup QuantumCT, according to the New Haven Independent .

title: Witnessing ICE arrests and nonprofit cuts, locals decry Trump policies
subtitle: Donald Trump’s second presidential term has jolted New Haven with arrests by Immigration and Customs Enforcement and slashed funding for and access to social services. Local activists and nonprofit leaders continue to protest.
author: Nellie Kenney
text: A group of New Haveners, clad in bright colors and carrying paper cranes, sang out advice for resisting Immigration and Customs Enforcement officers at a recent May Day rally on the New Haven Green: “Don’t say anything, don’t sign anything. Don’t let them trick you into opening the door.” Local activists — like these members of Singing Resistance New Haven, a chapter of the national organization — have mobilized in response to sweeping federal policy changes and their local effects since the beginning of Donald Trump’s second presidential term. New Haven advocates described an atmosphere of anxiety amid both the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown and federal cuts to social services, which have jeopardized local access to food. The “biggest thing, both for attorneys and for non-citizens living in the U.S.” is the “uncertainty and how quickly things are changing,” Maureen Abell, an immigration attorney at the New Haven Legal Assistance Association, said in a phone interview last Friday. Federal immigration officers have arrested at least five people in New Haven this year as part of the president’s ongoing mass deportation operation . ICE arrests increased by around 300 percent in New England in the first seven months of Trump’s presidency, according to data from the Immigration Research Initiative. Abell pointed to cases of federal immigration officers arresting people while leaving court or at green-card interviews . Although these arrests comprised “a small percentage of cases,” she said, the practice is “still having a huge effect through how afraid it is making people to engage with the system.” ICE detained a woman from the Hill neighborhood last June and a Wilbur Cross High School student the following month. The student, Esdrás Zabaleta-Ramirez, was later released following a ruling by a federal immigration judge. In January 2026, ICE arrested three people in downtown New Haven and another person in a New Haven courthouse . In March, federal agents arrested a nursing student at Southern Connecticut State University, Keyla Vasquez-Zuniga, who was granted bond last week. Those arrests have sparked an array of local protests. In July 2025, over a hundred people gathered at Wilbur Cross to protest Zabaleta-Ramirez’ detention. Teachers at the school led a letter-writing campaign attesting to the student’s schoolwork and character. Last month, students and activists rallied at Southern Connecticut State University, calling for Vasquez-Zuniga’s release. New Haven has also been the site of protests against ICE activity at the national level. In response to the deaths of Renée Good and Alex Pretti this January during the Trump administration’s crackdown in Minneapolis, locals formed the New Haven chapter of Singing Resistance and organized four demonstrations , each with at least a hundred attendees, in January and February. As organizers gather to protest immigration enforcement actions, nearby city organizations continue to grapple with federal funding cuts and locals describe increased food insecurity due to Trump-era policy. Integrated Refugee and Immigrant Services, a local nonprofit that aids refugee resettlement, closed its New Haven office in April 2025 after its funding was slashed by the federal government. The organization was also forced to lay off 30 percent of its staff, Maggie Mitchell Salem, the nonprofit’s executive director, told the News in February. In 2024, Integrated Refugee and Immigrant Services had a budget of over $20 million, most of which was provided by the federal government, Mitchell Salem said in a phone interview last Friday. In 2025, following federal funding cuts, its budget dropped to around $11 million, and this year, it hovers around $7 million, Mitchell Salem said — a number which will continue to decrease as funding to support Afghan arrivals “sunsets” in September. Mitchell Salem called the fallout of federal cuts “painful,” adding that for many immigrants, the organization’s New Haven location was one of the “first places they went to receive support and services” in the United States. “We’re having to make some very tough choices, and we have to figure out what the people we serve need most,” she said. “What, reasonably, can IRIS do through our food pantry, through our array of program services? And then what do others do, perhaps better than us? And how can we all work together to de-duplicate, to make sure that the most essential services remain available while having to cut back on others?” The Trump administration’s policy has altered the nonprofit’s partnerships as well as its funding, Mitchell Salem said. In October, the nonprofit announced that it would not receive refugees from the United States Refugee Admissions Program after the Trump administration moved to prioritize the resettlement of Afrikaners, a white minority group in South Africa. “We have never really questioned who the federal government was sending. And now we are,” Mitchell Salem said. Other local nonprofits have felt the impacts of federal actions through their effects on the populations that these organizations seek to help. Steve Werlin, the executive director of New Haven’s Downtown Evening Soup Kitchen, wrote in an email to the News that the greatest effects of federal policy “on the food assistance side” have been changes brought on by the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. The act, which Trump signed last July, tightened the work requirements around eligibility for the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, restricted eligibility for non-citizens, made changes to benefit calculations and shifted payment responsibilities onto states, according to the National Conference of State Legislatures. “Many of the people we serve have lost eligibility completely, or have seen their monthly allocations through SNAP decreased,” Werlin wrote. He also noted blocked funding to the Emergency Food and Shelter Program, which had been “pumping millions of dollars of food assistance funding directly to community providers across the country.” Werlin hypothesized that the “atmosphere of fear” created by immigration crackdowns has “made it more difficult for people to access food.” Kimberly Hart, a member of the board of directors for the Loaves and Fishes Food Pantry, has seen a “drop in migrants coming to the pantry,” she said. Hart said that changes to the SNAP program “disrupt” the lives of “have-nots.” SNAP was “working very well” before the changes, she said. 6,626 New Haveners — about 17.4 percent of the city’s total voters — voted for Trump in 2024, according to the News’ coverage at the time.

title: New Haven fights back against an antagonistic Trump administration
subtitle: Mayor Justin Elicker and state leaders have emphasized the disconnect between their offices’ priorities and those of an increasingly hostile federal government, which sued the city two months ago over its “sanctuary policies.”
author: Evelyn Ronan, Elijah Hurewitz-Ravitch
text: Since the beginning of President Donald Trump’s second term, New Haven has been targeted by the federal government as part of its crackdown against progressive and sanctuary cities. The city has responded with a series of coalition lawsuits and amicus briefs opposing the federal government, with local and state officials seeking to distance their political priorities with those of the Trump administration. In March, the federal government sued New Haven back, alleging that the city’s policies impede cooperation between local authorities and federal immigration enforcement. New Haven has joined five lawsuits and 24 amicus briefs since Trump took office, challenging policies related to immigration enforcement, federal funding, municipal authority and cuts to social welfare programs. In January 2025, the federal government released a memo which froze roughly $3 trillion in nonprofit and local government funds that provide the essential short-term cash flow allowing cities like New Haven to maintain regular operations. The motion was rescinded just as Mayor Justin Elicker was condemning the action at a Hartford press conference. A month later, the city signed an amicus brief alleging that the National Institutes of Health’s research funding cuts would destabilize the scientific research ecosystem and pose threats to early-career researchers, lamenting the “slash-and-burn philosophy” of the Trump administration regarding crucial funding, according to previous interviews with the News. Mayor Justin Elicker offered his first major commentary on the federal blows last February at his annual State of the City address, where he briefly alluded to the federal targeting of sanctuary cities like New Haven and disapproved of the Trump administration’s aims to defund diversity, equity and inclusion initiatives. Soon after, New Haven joined several Democrat-led municipalities in a lawsuit arguing that Trump’s crackdown on illegal immigration violated several clauses that protect municipal autonomy and unjustly targeted cities like New Haven for their “sanctuary” status. Last March, when announcing his proposed budget for fiscal year 2026, Elicker emphasized the difference between New Haven’s political posture and that of the Trump administration. Elicker also criticized the unpredictability of the Trump administration’s financial cutbacks, which he said makes it difficult for municipalities to draw up spending plans. He noted that New Haven is exercising “fiscal caution,” and that public entities like New Haven Public Schools rely on funding from the federal government. A month later, New Haven signed on to another lawsuit, which alleged that recent funding freezes to environment-related initiatives — including the termination of $20 billion in grant funding, $30 million of which was designated for New Haven — were illegal. That March, Elicker lamented the federal government “turning its back on cities” despite binding agreements in an interview with the News. Later that year, in October, New Haven joined another lawsuit which challenged the Department of Homeland Security’s updated terms and conditions for grant recipients. New regulations required recipients to agree not to “operate any programs that advance or promote DEI, DEIA, or discriminatory equity ideology .” The lawsuit coincided with last year’s New Haven mayoral race. Facing a Republican challenger , Elicker continued to emphasize New Haven’s “progressive values.” In a debate , he called the city the “most welcoming” in the world, alluding again to its sanctuary status. Allie Jacobs, the city’s primary legal counsel, said that New Haven weighs the benefits of joining the legal fight against the federal government against the fact that doing so could invite more scrutiny from Trump. “We assess whether the city, its interest, or its residents would be significantly adversely impacted by a particular law, policy, or judicial decision,” Jacobs wrote in an email to the News. “Sometimes that takes the form of joining a lawsuit if we have standing, sometimes that takes the form of an amicus brief, and sometimes that takes the form of other advocacy approaches.” When announcing New Haven’s participation in the lawsuit against the DHS, Elicker said that he was not concerned with drawing extra attention to New Haven. “I hear from some of my mayoral colleagues out there that they don’t want to make their cities a target,” Elicker said at a press conference at the time. “Now is a time that we should not be hiding. Now is a time that we should be standing up and fighting for the values of our community, both because at some point we’re all going to be a target anyway, but more importantly, because this is the right thing to do.” Days after New Haven joined the lawsuit against the DHS, Elicker contrasted the city’s balanced budget with the financial gridlock at the federal level that had resulted in a shutdown. “We in New Haven are continuing to do things differently by providing transparent, reliable, and stable budgeting,” he said at a press conference on the city’s fiscal outlook. “It’s what our residents deserve.” Two weeks later, New Haven joined a lawsuit filed by a coalition of municipalities, charities, religious organizations, business and unions against the Trump administration’s decision not to fund Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, or SNAP, benefits in the wake of the government shutdown. In response to the federal government’s cuts to SNAP funding — which Elicker condemned as “cruelty” that “blocked food from the table of 30,000 New Haveners” in a November interview with the News — Connecticut set aside $500 million for an emergency relief fund that subsidized food stamps during the government shutdown. In December 2025, New Haven joined a fifth lawsuit against the federal government, led by the Public Rights Project, over the federal government’s attempt to claw back over $12 billion in local funding designated for transit and public healthcare programs. The federal government declared that states would need to change policies on DEI initiatives, immigration, and abortion to obtain frozen funds. Aside from the slew of lawsuits, New Haven has joined in the national effort against the federal deputization of municipal governments and a national crackdown on immigration. This January, New Haven signed onto an amicus brief backing Minnesota in its lawsuit against the officials who authorized the federal government’s mass deportations. Elicker doubled down on his effort to distance New Haven ideologically from the Trump administration at the swearing-in ceremony for city officials this year, emphasizing New Haven’s values of “tolerance,” “respect for diversity” and “compassion for our most vulnerable.” He said that the Trump administration’s haphazard funding cuts force city officials to remain vigilant. Now, a year and a half since his first major action against the federal government, Elicker has continued to define his own ideological posture, as well as New Haven’s, as antithetical to Trump’s. In his State of the City address this February, the mayor doubled down on his previous criticism of the Trump administration, calling it an “endless dumpster fire of chaos and cruelty.” Following the dispatch of federal agents to carry out Operation Broken Trust, an Immigration and Customs Enforcement roundup that resulted in the arrest of 65 immigrants across Connecticut, the Trump administration last month filed its own lawsuit against New Haven. The Department of Justice complaint argued that New Haven’s policies illegally restrict local authorities’ collaboration with federal immigration enforcement, disregard federal law and threaten public safety. The suit, which also named Elicker, Connecticut Gov. Ned Lamont SOM ’80 and Attorney General William Tong as defendants, criticized New Haven’s enforcement of the Trust Act , which prevents the “deputization of local and state law enforcement for immigration enforcement.” A 2025 “Year in Review” message from Tong criticized the Trump administration’s “cruel, dangerous, and hurtful agenda,” adding that his office would not relent in its protection of Connecticut families. Rep. Rosa DeLauro, who represents New Haven in Congress, blasted Trump for “pursuing politically motivated lawsuits targeting our state for not carrying out ICE’s extreme enforcement agenda.” “President Trump’s actions are hurting working families, making it harder for them to afford basic necessities,” DeLauro said in a statement sent to the News by a spokesperson. “We must fight back, and New Haven and Connecticut are leading the way by challenging the Trump administration’s actions in court. Many of these cases have already been successful and restored funding to our communities.” In a phone interview with the News, Elicker said the city had been “prepared all along for a possible lawsuit” but emphasized New Haven’s compliance with federal law. “We can see that similar Trump lawsuits across the nation have already been thrown out,” the mayor said. “Trump is trying to force us to settle and carry out immigration policy, but that is not our job,” Elicker added. Several of the city’s legal challenges have already produced major rulings. One federal judge blocked the Trump administration from withholding federal funding from sanctuary jurisdictions, siding with New Haven, and another ordered the restoration of roughly $31 million in frozen environmental grants owed to the city, though the Trump administration has appealed portions of that ruling. Some legal disputes remain unresolved, including the litigation over DHS grant conditions and broader fights surrounding immigration enforcement. Elicker seems to have no intentions of backing down. He said that his approach differs from those of his colleagues across Connecticut. “Some have spoken out, but some have remained under the radar, and I don’t think that’s the right thing to do at this moment,” Elicker said. “We have to fight for what we believe in.” The Department of Justice does not currently have a permanent attorney general. Elijah Hurewitz-Ravitch contributed reporting.

title: Wooster Square and walkability encourage seniors to stay in New Haven
subtitle: Enticed by everything from fond memories to unique opportunities for research and music, seniors hailing from hometowns across the country say they will pursue career and educational opportunities in Yale’s home city.
author: Tajrian Khan
text: As seniors prepare to leave Yale’s campus and enter the workforce, a handful of them are choosing to remain in the Elm City next year. In a survey sent out to the class of 2026 by the News, 13 out of 115 respondents who said they will remain in the United States — or 11.3 percent — said they plan to live in Connecticut. According to data published by Yale’s Office of Career Strategy, around 12 percent of the class of 2025 stayed in Connecticut, compared to an average of 14 percent in the years since 2016. Rory Bricca ’26 says he hopes to stay in New Haven next year to teach music lessons at the Neighborhood Music School, as well as audition for choirs at the Yale Institute of Sacred Music and work on his own compositions. Bricca was born at the Yale New Haven Hospital and lived in Hamden until he was 10 years old, when his family moved to Tucson, Arizona. But he has “fond memories” of New Haven, he said, “especially East Rock and Wooster Square.” About a month ago, Bricca said he had a “gut feeling” that he wanted to stay in New Haven. “I’m hoping to just have a year where I can stay in this area and stay connected to the relationships I’ve built,” he said. Bricca is also excited by New Haven’s performing arts scene. “Nowhere else in the world could I be going to a friend’s musical or theater performance every week,” he said. “That’s just super fun.” Victor Nguyen ’26 plans to stay in New Haven next year to continue his research at a Yale School of Medicine neuroscience lab. “My current lab aligns with my research goals, and I didn’t want to have to start back at square one in a new location,” Nguyen said. At the same time, he thinks New Haven offers a “nice contrast” to his hometown of Fort Smith, Arkansas. When applying to colleges, he said he wanted to move to a city with a “fast living pace” he is excited to spend at least one more year in New Haven after four years at Yale. Jae-Hee Bae ’26 grew up in Dayton, Ohio, and first came to the east coast as a Yale student four years ago. But now that New Haven feels “comfortable and familiar,” she said, she is not ready to leave. “I think there’s a consensus that people want to leave New Haven after graduation,” Bae said. “But when you’re graduating, there’s a lot of change that happens and having some constant really relieves the pressure of all that change.” She loves that New Haven is “more pedestrian-friendly” than suburban Ohio and that residents are engaged in their communities. “I like that there are a lot of protests that happen on the Green, and there are a lot of events like the 5Ks and the Cherry Blossom Festival,” Bae said. “It shows that organizers really care about making it feel like home.” Like Nguyen, Bae is staying next year to work on mRNA research at the Yale School of Medicine’s Neugebauer Lab and apply to medical schools in the meantime. Many Yale students take a “gap year” before applying to medical school, and for those interested, research opportunities at Yale are “straightforward,” Bae said. “I feel comfortable knowing a handful of my friends are staying here,” Bae added. “I’m super excited to stay involved with a community that I’ve developed a relationship with.” Gia Cook ’26 will be working as a Woodbridge Fellow at Yale’s Office of Public Affairs and Communications. “I’m super-duper excited to be in New Haven,” Cook said. Originally from a suburb of Detroit, Cook said she lived in the “same little yellow house in the same street” her entire life until she came to Yale. “It’s been incredible living here, I don’t really have any dislikes,” Cook said, adding that she appreciated being “somewhere walkable” and having “access to such vibrant nightlife.” Yale University has been the largest employer of graduating students every year since 2016, when the Office of Career Strategy started publishing data on postgraduate plans.

title: After increased pledge, seniors split on whether Yale pays city enough
subtitle: The University announced that it would boost its yearly payments to New Haven in March. Some surveyed members of the class of 2026 said the contribution was too much, while others emphasized the importance of supporting the city they came to call home.
author: Chantal Eulenstein
text: Beginning next fiscal year, Yale will boost its annual payment to New Haven. Graduating seniors differ on whether they think the increased contribution is appropriate. Two months ago, University President Maurie McInnis and New Haven Mayor Justin Elicker announced an updated town-gown agreement that will see the University give its home city more than $230 million over the next seven years. A survey conducted by the News found that a plurality of graduating seniors — just under 50 percent of 147 respondents — feels that Yale’s new voluntary contribution is either “too little” or “somewhat little.” Elicker said in March that the deal “represents the largest voluntary payment” from any American university to a municipality. Yale will pay New Haven $29.9 million in the next fiscal year, and contributions will increase to $33.6 million by 2033. These sums represent a substantial increase from the previous agreement, under which Yale paid some $23 or $24 million annually. The new payment schedule followed months of negotiations between Yale and New Haven, and the agreement was announced as the class of 2026 looked ahead to graduation. Just over 44 percent of senior respondents said that the new commitment is “appropriate,” and nearly 11 percent said it was “too much” or “somewhat much.” “I think it’s important for Yale to give back to the city it is built in,” Estelle Balsirow ’26 wrote in an email to the News, adding that the survey results suggest that “a lot of seniors agree with that sentiment.” Other seniors were taken aback by the proportion of their classmates who felt that Yale’s contribution to New Haven was excessive. “I found it surprising that nearly 11 percent of people thought that Yale was giving too much money to New Haven,” Colin Kim ’26 wrote in a text to the News. “Yale as an academic institution siphons so many resources like land from the surrounding area, so I personally feel like the school should give more to rehabilitate the structural harms that it has caused to the city.” Yale owns over 850 acres in New Haven, according to CT Insider . Thanks to its status as a nonprofit, the University does not pay property taxes on any buildings that are used for academic purposes. That exemption is protected by an 1834 agreement between Yale and Connecticut. Efforts to push Yale to increase its payment ramped up in the months before the new deal was announced. In January, leaders of Yale’s Local 33, 34 and 35 unions joined city leaders and activists to demand that Yale up its annual contribution to $110 million. And in early March, nearly 500 people attended an online “solidarity summit” organized by New Haven Rising in which speakers denounced Yale for not contributing more. The University is New Haven’s second-largest taxpayer. The new town-gown agreement preserves an existing arrangement in which Yale makes payments equivalent to full property taxes for three years after purchasing a property for academic use, before reducing those payments by 10 percent each year. New initiatives announced alongside the updated agreement in March include a fund to support local nonprofits and a commitment to exploring the possibility of using vacant University land to address Connecticut’s affordable housing crisis . Some seniors believe that Yale’s volunteer contribution to New Haven could increase further. “I’m wondering what determines whether Yale is giving ‘too much,’” Kim wrote, “since I personally think that the quality of education that Yale provides would not suffer at all from the school giving more money to New Haven. In the end, isn’t that all that matters for answering the question of whether Yale pays New Haven ‘too much’ or ‘too little’?” For some seniors, attending Yale meant becoming a New Haven resident — which means that their alma mater needs to give back. “I hope that a stronger relationship from this agreement will form for both Yalies who call New Haven a temporary home and residents who will live here with their families for years to come,” Balsirow wrote. In the University’s first voluntary contribution agreement, signed in 1990, Yale pledged to give around $1.5 million to the city each year.

title: Even after monthslong scandal, ex-chief remembered for community ties
subtitle: Karl Jacobson resigned from his post as the chief of the New Haven Police Department in January after being confronted with allegations of larceny. But locals still remember him for his “comforting” leadership and focus on community engagement.
author: Evelyn Ronan
text: It hasn’t been a career-high year for Karl Jacobson. In January, Jacobson, then the chief of the New Haven Police Department, abruptly resigned from his post as chief and later turned himself in on an arrest warrant for two charges of larceny. He allegedly embezzled a combined $85,500 from two city funds, one intended for paying confidential informants in the narcotics unit and the other for the city’s Police Activity League. According to an affidavit, Jacobson bet more than $4 million on online gambling platforms during the time he was allegedly embezzling the funds. In April, the chief pleaded not guilty to the two larceny charges against him. But even as his department moves on from him — on May 5, the city swore in David Zannelli, who was among the three assistant chiefs who confronted Jacobson with allegations of embezzlement — New Haveners remembered his tenure as an era of progressive strides on community engagement. “He had the closest relationship to affected communities of any chief I’ve seen,” Paul Bass ’82, the founder of the New Haven Independent and longtime reporter on local policing, said in a phone interview. “I’ve been shocked at how little I’ve seen of that personal scandal reverberating at a larger scale.” Bass elaborated that the grievances about Jacobson allegedly stealing funds were limited to him and did not taint the department at large. In a phone interview with the News, Mayor Justin Elicker said he didn’t doubt that Jacobson “cared about New Haven.” But he struck a colder tone, emphasizing that the transparency-focused approach that Jacobson championed also extended to his removal. “It’s important to understand the fact that holding Jacobson accountable builds more trust in the community,” Elicker said. “When we talk about accountability, this is what we mean.” According to Bass, it wasn’t policy shifts that marked Jacobson’s term, but his overall “de-emphasization of arrest” and a focus on community ties. “A good policy doesn’t work if you don’t have trust of the community, if you haven’t been in people’s kitchens and living rooms during tough times and helped them navigate crisis,” Bass said. Zannelli, an 18-year veteran of the NHPD, has suggested he wants to continue Jacobson’s legacy of strengthening collaboration between the police and local community. Jacobson’s people-first approach took center stage in the wake of Randy Cox’s arrest . New Haven police arrested Cox on June 19, 2022. He was handcuffed and placed in a police car without a seatbelt . The officer driving the car, Oscar Diaz , made a hard stop, which caused Cox to slam into the front of the vehicle. In body cam footage released promptly after the incident, Cox could be heard saying that his neck felt broken. Diaz called for medical assistance but continued driving instead of waiting for paramedics to arrive and attend to Cox, who was then dragged face down into a holding cell, according to the police report . He could not move and repeatedly tried to get the officers to stop moving him. Officers in the footage can be heard saying Cox was “faking it” and doing “extra s—” before they dragged him out of the vehicle. The officers’ “reckless” treatment of Cox, a New Haven Police Department Internal Affairs report found, led to his ultimate paralysis. Cox is now in a long-term care facility . Jacobson moved to release bodycam footage of the incident and address the officers’ violation of conventional policy immediately after the incident. In March 2023, after the release of the Internal Affairs report, Jacobson called for the dismissal of the involved officers, who were fired in August of that year. “I think the community has asked us to be transparent and swift,” Jacobson said while announcing his choice to call for the officers’ dismissal, according to News coverage from the time. “We have put a lot of thought and investigation into this decision. We want to make sure that everything is done right, so that through this hearing, justice can be done.” Bass speculated that if Jacobson had handled the incident differently, “we would have the potential for riots.” “He had such a great connection with the people who were understandably upset,” Bass said. “He and the mayor got so much good information right away, rather than being defensive, which another chief would’ve been.” Michael Lawlor, a former state representative and a professor at the University of New Haven’s Henry C. Lee College of Criminal Justice and Forensic Sciences, said that Jacobson reached out to Cox’s family immediately after the incident. “That instinct to do something very uncomfortable immediately is a significant attribute of Karl,” Lawlor said. Jacobson later instituted a new policy in reaction to the Cox incident dubbed “ medical Miranda ,” where officers transporting prisoners are required to ask if they need medical attention. Jacobson also co-signed a judge’s decision not to expunge the records of the officers involved in Cox’s arrest. Diaz, the officer driving the police car during the arrest, was later reinstated under Jacobson’s leadership. “He was big on procedural justice,” Lawlor said, describing it as a central aspect of Jacobson’s policing philosophy. “If you’re a cop and you’re dealing with a citizen, it’s extremely important to explain what you’re doing. He really instilled among the rank-and-file cops the importance of this.” Lawlor said Jacobson also emphasized policing strategies that acquainted officers with particular neighborhoods — such as walking beats, in which officers patrol neighborhoods on foot rather than in a car. “When I came to New Haven, I became a better cop and a better person because I walked a beat,” Jacobson said in a WNHH Community Radio interview six months into his term as chief. 15 years on, he still maintained community ties from that time, Jacobson said in the interview. Lawlor said he considers Jacobson’s community-focused approach to be the crux of his term as police chief. “If you’re gonna be a police chief anywhere, you have to be sort of a politician,” Lawlor said.“A lot of cops have a tunnel vision approach to their job, but as a chief, you have to deal with the community, you have to explain things, and you have to be comforting. Karl really had that skillset.” Bass, the longtime reporter on local policing, contrasted Jacobson’s controversy to a 2007 NHPD scandal in which a group of top cops stole thousands of dollars of reserved police department funds , prompting the departure of then-chief Francisco Ortiz. The NHPD appointed James Lewis soon after in 2008, a chief who had never before worked in New Haven and took an “aggressive” approach to policing reminiscent of NHPD’s militaristic crackdown in the 1980s. Lewis was responsible for 20 arrests in his first week as chief. Bass said the fact that the city hired Zannelli “from within,” instead of hiring an outsider, “means this is so different from 2008,” adding that Zannelli maintained his predecessor’s community-focused approach to policing. “Zannelli has really good relationships with people from working in different neighborhoods,” Bass said. “He’s built up an extraordinary level of goodwill and trust in the community.” Karl Jacobson was sworn in as police chief on July 7, 2022.

title: After deportation flight controversy, an uncertain future for Avelo
subtitle: Local activists say that it was their pressure campaign that led to the suspension of Avelo Airlines’ partnership with the Department of Homeland Security. The airline has cited a cost-benefit analysis instead.
author: Qamar Al-Tameemi, Evelyn Ronan
text: 2025 was a tumultuous year for New Haven’s leading airline. After Avelo Airlines announced last April that it would begin chartering deportation flights for the Department of Homeland Security, activists in New Haven and across the country organized a boycott and put up billboards publicly decrying the company’s decision. Local organizations suspended their partnerships with the company. After nine months of pushback, Avelo announced in January that it would pause the charter flights. Avelo representatives and community activists presented different claims about why the company ultimately ended its contract with DHS — and what the episode means for Avelo’s future in New Haven. “They’ve got a lot of work ahead of them to earn back the trust of this state, and I hope that they can,” Connecticut Attorney General William Tong wrote in a statement after Avelo announced it would end its partnership with DHS in January. The Connecticut attorney general’s office declined to comment for this article, directing the News to the earlier statement. Avelo’s leadership initially described its partnership with DHS as a means of boosting revenue amid poor performance at Tweed-New Haven Airport, where the airline dominates traffic. In an email to Avelo employees announcing the contract, CEO Andrew Levy cited his company’s poor performance and noted that the first quarter of 2025 yielded its worst quarterly results since 2023, the News reported at the time. A petition created by the New Haven Immigrants Coalition entitled, “We Won’t fly Avelo until they stop ICE flights!” received over 40,000 signatures. Several protests occurred against the airline at Tweed Airport, with an April protest drawing a crowd of about 200, including Sen. Richard Blumenthal LAW ’73, three New Haven alders and state Rep. Juan Candelaria. Avelo announced in January that it would pause charter flights for DHS. In an internal email to Avelo staff obtained by the News, Levy said the partnership “provided short-term benefits” but acknowledged that the revenue was not high enough to “overcome its operational complexity and costs.” Courtney Goff, an Avelo spokesperson, wrote in an email to the News that the company’s partnership with DHS did not impact the number of customers that chose to fly Avelo, citing increased customers on commercial service in 2025 compared to 2024. “We stopped DHS flying because it was too infrequent to produce the anticipated revenue,” Goff wrote in an email to the News. “We chose to utilize the planes elsewhere and recapitalize our network and fleet.” But local activists said it was the local community’s pressure campaign that ended Avelo’s collaboration with the DHS. “They say it was about cost and complexity, but community pressure told the real story,” CT Students For A Dream, a local organization involved in protesting against the airline, wrote in a social media post in January. In a case study by the Coalition to Stop Avelo, a national activist group organized in light of the company’s contract with DHS, organizers attributed Avelo’s decision to a “sustained pressure campaign that Avelo could no longer ignore” and acknowledged the alliance of various activists involved in protesting against Avelo. “Even though we were the ones who started with the campaign, at the end, there was this national pressure that really changed everything around what happened with this company,” John Lugo, the community organizing director of the local immigrant activism group Unidad Latina en Accion, said in a phone interview. Protests against the airline occurred at several other airports across the country, including Wilmington Airport in Delaware and Mesa Gateway Airport in Arizona. New Hampshire state Rep. Seth Miller created several billboards that urged, “Don’t Fly Avelo.” Anne Watkins, an organizer with the New Haven Immigrants Coalition, highlighted the diverse alliance of community members and organizations that committed to the Avelo boycott, including groups like the New Haven Federation of Teachers and community organizing group New Haven Rising. Regarding perceptions of Avelo now, Watkins said she thinks “people have a lot of different opinions about it.” “Some people feel like, ‘Okay, they stopped the contract. We should fly them again.’ Some people feel, ‘Yeah, they stopped the contract, but they never apologized. They never acknowledged the harm that they caused.’” Watkins said. Several local organizations, including Faxon Law New Haven Road Race and Puerto Ricans United Inc. severed ties with Avelo in light of their decision to partner with DHS. The road race terminated their existing sponsorship agreement with the airline in July. Puerto Ricans United Inc., which had previously received support from the airline, preemptively cut ties with the airline ahead of its 2025 Puerto Rican Festival. In July 2025, Mayor Elicker prohibited the use of public funds to fly Avelo in a memo sent to city employees, as reported by NBC CT . In a phone interview on Tuesday, Elicker said the city had since lifted the policy. Puerto Ricans United Inc. board president Joe Rodriguez wrote in an email to the News that since first announcing it would not again partner with Avelo, the airline had not approached the organization for a new sponsorship, and that the non-profit did not “plan to proactively reach out.” According to data from the United States Department of Transportation, Avelo’s parent company, TEM Enterprises, carried out 88.35% of flights at Tweed Airport from March 2025 to February 2026. Evelyn Ronan contributed reporting.

title: New Haven board gets first regular report on Yale police use of force
subtitle: Under an agreement reached in December, New Haven’s Civilian Review Board is set to discuss its first quarterly report from the Yale Police Department. From January to March, the department recorded five use-of-force incidents and no complaints.
author: Adele Haeg, Evelyn Ronan
text: It’s official now — a New Haven oversight board will begin reviewing reports on the Yale Police Department’s use of force, starting with a report covering the first quarter of 2026. The two-page report , provided by the chair of New Haven’s Civilian Review Board, is the first one the board will review under a new agreement between Yale and the city. Board member Sam Fawcett said the board would discuss the report during their regularly scheduled monthly meeting on Monday. After months of negotiations, the New Haven review board and the University in December reached an agreement that authorizes the board to review use-of-force reports and civilian complaints against the Yale Police. The 2019 ordinance that created the Civilian Review Board requires such an agreement with the University, but the board had not exercised formal oversight of the Yale Police until now. “We appreciate our strengthened relationship with the New Haven Civilian Review Board and look forward to our continued partnership,” Duane Lovello, the head of Yale Public Safety, wrote in an emailed statement. Thursday’s report from the police department’s Professional Standards and Internal Affairs Unit included five incidents involving the use of force between Jan. 1 and March 31. The incidents are recorded when an officer uses physical force beyond routine handcuffing or verbal commands during an encounter. Each of the five incidents in the new report was “found to be reasonable and within policy” by the Yale Police, the report says. The review board’s chair, AnneMarie Rivera-Berrios, said the board has seen “huge changes” in how New Haven’s two departments have collaborated since the board was formed in 2019 . She said the Yale Police Department has been more responsive since the December agreement than it ever had since she became the board’s chair in 2022 . After the Yale Police Advisory Board dissolved last year, the YPD did not have a process for civilian review of complaints and use of force reports. A new Yale Public Safety Advisory Board saw its first members appointed in November 2025, but its charter lacked the specific authority to review such cases. Yale publishes its own annual use-of-force report . In 2025, there were 18 reportable use-of-force incidents, and the report claimed that force was only used to “safely gain compliance” from those resisting arrest. Under the new agreement, the Civilian Review Board will also review civilian complaints against the Yale Police Department, in addition to use-of-force reports. Between Jan. 1 and March 31, the department did not receive any such complaints, according to Thursday’s report. The Civilian Review Board will meet on Monday at 6 p.m. on the second floor of City Hall.

title: Effort to dedicate corner to Local 34 founding member moves forward
subtitle: Two former members of Local 34, which represents the University’s clerical and technical workers, are pushing to rename the corner of College and Elm streets after John Wilhelm ’67, a founding member of the union.
author: Brett Penk, Jingchu Sophia Zhang
text: New Haveners and Yale student leaders have collected about 320 signatures on a petition to name the corner of College and Elm streets in honor of a man who helped establish Yale’s first employee union. The petition required 250 signatures to qualify for review by the president of the Board of Alders, Tyisha Walker-Myers, who may assign it to one of the board’s committees, according to Ward 1 Alder Elias Theodore ’27. Theodore said he recently submitted the petition to City Hall for review. Under the proposal, the corner would be dedicated to John Wilhelm ’67, who helped found Local 34, which represents the University’s clerical and technical workers. Last year, the union celebrated the 40-year anniversary of its first contract with Yale — a milestone that motivated locals Aldo Cupo and Cheryl Bergman to spearhead the renaming efforts, they said. Cupo and Bergman were also founding members of Local 34 and are now members of the Yale Unions Retirees Association. The pair said in a phone interview that they wanted to honor Wilhelm’s contributions to the union. According to Cupo and Bergman, Wilhelm led Local 34’s first contract negotiation processes and pushed for pay equity for male and female workers. Later in his career, Wilhelm was elected president of UNITE HERE, a national labor union that encompasses Yale’s Locals 33, 34 and 35. He retired in 2012. Cupo and Bergman started the renaming process by reaching out to Theodore, whose district includes the corner where College Street meets Elm Street. “I was really excited when Aldo and Cheryl reached out,” Theodore said. “We all recognize this as an opportunity to help educate on this history.” In order to gain city approval to rename the street corner, Cupo and Bergman had to collect 250 signatures, at least 166 of which were mandated to come from downtown residents. Theodore assisted them by partnering with the undergraduate organizations Students Unite Now and the Yale College Democrats to collect signatures from Yalies, many of whom live in the downtown area. Audrey Landeros ’27, a community engagement fellow for the Yale College Democrats, described Yale students as being “incredibly positive, super enthusiastic to sign and give someone that honor” of having a street corner named for them. Cupo and Bergman said that the corner of College and Elm is symbolic for the union. For the past four decades, they said, Local 34 members have met at First and Summerfield United Methodist Church, which is nested in the northeast corner of the intersection. The pair described the street corner as the dividing line between New Haven and Yale’s campus. Wilhelm said in a phone interview that he was humbled to learn of the renaming effort. “If you had asked me if that was okay, I would have said no because I don’t like glorifying myself,” he said of the renaming effort. If approved, the sign would also commemorate the work of all Yale union members in the past 40 years, Wilhelm said. “I’m humbled and I’m proud of the work that I and others did when I was there,” he said. “I am also really proud of the amazing progress that the people who have led the union of Yale have made since I left.” Now that the petition has surpassed the 250-signature threshold, Theodore said, several steps are in order. If Walker-Myers assigns the renaming effort to a committee, it would then hold a public hearing in which locals can weigh in on the petition, he said. “Hopefully, we can get this done in the next four to six months, so we’re hoping for a ceremony at the corner of College and Elm Street in the fall, and we would love to have many people there,” Theodore said. Wilhelm said he would attend the ceremony. Local 34 signed its first contract with Yale in 1985. Jingchu Sophia Zhang contributed reporting.

title: Union influence dominates New Haven alder elections. Is it faltering?
subtitle: The Board of Alders has long been controlled by representatives whose campaigns were backed by local unions. A fall race between two Yalies for a downtown alder seat marked a rare loss for one of New Haven’s most influential political forces.
author: Elijah Hurewitz-Ravitch
text: Early last September, days before the Democratic primary for Ward 1 alder, Elias Theodore ’27 gathered his supporters at La Cocinita Mexican Cafe. As they wolfed down rice and fajitas, the floppy-haired New Havener gave an impromptu campaign speech . His opponent, Norah Laughter ’26, had the backing of the powerful Yale unions and New Haven Rising, an adjacent community advocacy organization. Laughter’s supporters, often identifying themselves as union workers, had taken to following would-be Theodore voters on walks to the polls, trying to persuade them to change their minds. “It has been intimidating at times these past few days,” Theodore told the crowd at La Cocinita. “I know that there are a lot of people with a lot of power and a lot of money who are against us, which is funny to say, because these are traditionally, I think, the groups that get seen as grassroots. We are the ones running the grassroots campaign.” In the last days of the race, Theodore bumped up against one of the most influential forces in New Haven politics: the coalition of Yale’s UNITE HERE unions and New Haven Rising. “The union really wants this seat, and that has become abundantly clear this past week,” Theodore said in September. “I want to be an alder who solely acts and votes on behalf of my constituents.” Three days after the event at La Cocinita, Theodore beat Laughter by 125 votes . The unusually competitive race to represent the downtown ward that encompasses part of Yale’s campus on the Board of Alders offered a window into the unions’ role in New Haven’s electoral politics. In a rare setback since their rise to political dominance, the unions’ organizing efforts fell short in swaying a local election. There are four unions headquartered in New Haven that belong to the UNITE HERE national labor organizing network. Local 217 represents hospitality workers across Connecticut. Local 34 represents Yale’s clerical and technical workers. Local 35 represents Yale’s service and maintenance staff, including dining hall workers. And Local 33, which officially unionized in 2023 after a protracted battle with Yale, represents the University’s graduate students. Together, the UNITE HERE unions represent thousands of Yale workers. And since 2012 — after the unions organized a slate of candidates that won seats on the Board of Alders — their candidates have maintained a majority on the board, the legislative arm of New Haven’s government. “We have 23 out of 30 allies on the Board of Aldermen,” Local 35 president Bob Proto said at a 2017 meeting of Locals 34 and 35, according to the New Haven Independent . Local 35’s chief steward, Ward 23 Alder Tyisha Walker-Myers is the board’s president. Elijah Hurewitz-Ravitch, Staff Photographer A machine Before their candidates won a majority on the Board of Alders in 2011, Yale’s unions spent decades building out their grassroots organizing power, according to Zachary Schwartz-Weinstein ’04, a historian of university labor. Then they made their push for City Hall. “You had regular, everyday, run-of-the-mill people running for political office to be able to make changes that people can feel,” Ward 22 Alder Jeanette Morrison, who was first elected as part of that 2011 slate, said. Since then, she has represented a district that includes a significant portion of Yale’s campus, including six residential colleges. Together, the unions spent around $200,000 on the 2011 effort, the New Haven Independent reported at the time. Union candidates won 14 of the 15 races against establishment Democratic candidates. In election cycles since then, political action committees associated with Locals 34 and 35 have contributed money to elect and reelect candidates to the Board of Alders. Many candidates have also received money from the Connecticut arm of the UNITE HERE TIP State and Local Fund, which is a “nonfederal” PAC, according to its website . Nonfederal PACs are not subject to federal campaign finance regulations. In fall 2025, the Connecticut TIP State and Local Fund spent $9,125 on local races. Much of that money went to political committees like “Eastside Dems United” that do not disclose the candidates they support. The Local 34 PAC spent $9,875 that fall. Both the Local 34 and the TIP PACs gave $1,500 to New Haven’s Democratic Town Committee, according to state campaign finance disclosures . Vincent Mauro Jr. , who chairs the committee, said that it solicits contributions from “all kinds of PACs” but that it gets a “significant portion” of its funding from UNITE HERE. That money goes towards registering voters and turning them out on election day, Mauro said. He added that the Democratic Town Committee is “politically aligned” with the local unions. “There’s a symmetry between good jobs, fair work, fair wages, a coalition built around not just the short term, but the long term future of the city of New Haven, its residents, its workers,” Mauro said. There are currently six first-term alders on the board, and three of them — Ward 8 Alder Mandy Martinelli, Ward 16 Alder Magda Natal and Ward 18 Alder Leland Moore — received money from union PACs in their campaigns, disclosures show. Of the 24 other alders, at least 16 have received financial support or endorsements from UNITE HERE unions. Two alders — Ward 2 Alder Frank Douglass Jr. and Ward 29 Alder Brian Wingate — are vice presidents of Local 35. That union’s chief steward is Walker-Myers, the ward 23 alder who is the president of the Board of Alders. Ward 25 is represented by Adam Marchand GRD ’99, the chief steward of Local 34. Over the years, union issues have come to dominate local politics, local experts say. David Schleicher, a Yale Law School professor and an expert on urban politics, said that “on the core things 35 and 34 care about” — the relationship with Yale — union-backed alders don’t have “too much range to do anything else.” Yale’s unions have long pushed for the University to give more to New Haven, and Mayor Justin Elicker — who beat a UNITE HERE-backed incumbent in 2019 but has since forged a sometimes uneasy alliance with organizers — consistently thanks the unions for their efforts on that front. In January, Walker-Myers joined other union leaders in front of Sheffield-Sterling-Strathcona Hall, which houses Yale President Maurie McInnis’ office, to call for Yale to pay $110 million to New Haven each year. In March, Elicker and McInnis stood on the second floor of City Hall to announce a new voluntary contribution deal in which Yale will pay New Haven around $230 million over the next seven years. Walker-Myers, who did not respond to repeated requests for comment, did not attend the announcement. The UNITE HERE-New Haven Rising coalition is “somewhere between” a local political party and an interest group, Schleicher said. The unions make endorsements and develop a platform on a variety of issues — some of which are directly related to their interests and some of which are not, he added. A major source of the unions’ power in aldermanic elections is simply the fact that there are not other organized groups that get involved, according to Schleicher, “which makes sense, because it’s a very small city.” In a Democratic-dominated urban context, the unions fill a void that competing political parties would otherwise occupy, Schleicher said. New Haven Rising and the unions largely operate in tandem, often hosting rallies together. The group is also financially tied to the UNITE HERE unions. The 501(c)(4) organization’s 990 filing from 2024 — the most recent year available — shows that it spent $378,078, most of which went toward compensation for officers and directors. Data from the United States Department of Labor shows that New Haven Rising received $326,950 from Local 34 that year, plus $348,985 from Local 217. As of publication, the Rev. Scott Marks — the director of New Haven Rising, vice chair of the Democratic Town Committee and a UNITE HERE union employee — has not agreed to an interview or responded to repeated requests for comment. The resistance Efforts to disrupt union dominance emerged not long after 2011. A slate called Take Back New Haven — spearheaded by then-Ward 7 Alder Doug Hausladen ’04, who now heads the city’s parking authority — coalesced in 2013, though it quickly fizzled out . Hausladen and several other alders in 2014 formed a coalition called the People’s Caucus that aimed to counter union influence on the board. The effort appears not to have gotten very far. In 2023, Frank Redente Jr. won the Democratic primary in Ward 15 against a UNITE HERE-backed incumbent. “Obviously, it’s public knowledge that they campaigned against me during my election,” Redente said, referring to the unions. He added that before that election, he had felt frustrated by the unions’ “blanket endorsement ” of candidates. Ward 10 Alder Anna Festa, who was elected in 2013 after an uncontested Democratic primary, was part of both the Take Back New Haven slate and the People’s Caucus, and she is the longest-serving member of the Board of Alders who was elected without union backing. “Every institution in the city, whether it’s labor or large employers or developers or government itself, should have a seat at the table, but no one should control the table,” Festa said. “At the end of the day, our responsibility is to the public, to make decisions that are fair, fiscally responsible, and in the best interest of the entire city.” Festa supports labor organizing but is skeptical of union hegemony, she said. “Their influence has to be balanced,” she said. “And our job is to make sure that no single group has so much influence that it limits our transparency and our accountability and our good decision making.” Leslie Radcliffe, a longtime civic leader and the former chair of the City Plan Commission, has been a frequent critic of the Yale unions. She is also a former steward of Local 34. “Every citizen in New Haven is not a Yale employee,” she added. “And when the focus of those alders are Yale, Yale, Yale, for me, it’s like, you’re ignoring the non-Yale employees.” There’s been another battle brewing in the Hill’s Ward 3, a district that includes Yale-New Haven Hospital and the Schools of Medicine and Public Health. Miguel Pittman, a businessman and restauranteur , has spent the past few years trying to disrupt UNITE HERE’s hold on Ward 3. After Ron Hurt, a close ally of the Yale unions and of New Haven Rising, resigned in 2024, Pittman faced off in a special election against Angel Hubbard, a community organizer backed by several local elected officials. Pittman lost by just 16 votes. He lost again to Hubbard in the Democratic primary in the fall and, running on both the Independent and Republican lines, in the general election . Each of those races has been highly competitive, driving up voter turnout in a ward that has had low political engagement in recent history. Hubbard said that the Yale unions have a strong influence on the board, particularly “because a lot of us are union workers. And you know, for any job, you will want a union.” She added that the UNITE HERE unions stay engaged in local politics at all times — not just during campaign season — and hold alders accountable. In Ward 3, Hubbard said, “when I hold community meetings, they are there. When the CMT has meetings, they are there. So they’re not just around when it’s campaigning time. No, they are always present.” In that sense, Hubbard said, the Yale unions are involved in “basically” all realms of city governance. Reflecting back on his primary, Theodore feels he underestimated how much it was shaped by larger forces. “I hadn’t realized that this race could be so imbued with city-wide dynamics and union dynamics,” he said in an interview this month. “There were a bunch of people in Local 34 and Local 35 out canvassing. And I think I was naive, but I didn’t think of this race much beyond the few hundred stakeholders who live in these downtown apartments and Yale undergrads.” After his win, Theodore said he had to work hard to repair ties the election had strained. Theodore said his campaign was not meant to affront union power, but he acknowledged that his outsider status created tension with one of New Haven’s strongest political forces. “I don’t want my campaign to seem — it was not one that was anti-union, and it was never meant to be one to degrade their power. But, yeah, I mean, I think, I think that dynamic was at play.” Now, seven months after that election, Theodore is a member of a board built in large part by the unions, and he is navigating relationships with the same people whose volunteers followed his supporters to the polls. “I’m just still, like, trying to be very conscious of — like, union relationships are good, but I think I still feel very, kind of, anxious about saying something that will be misconstrued,” he said. When pressed, Theodore declined to say why he is anxious about what he says. Schleicher thinks union power may be waning. “There was a period when everyone understood them to be the most powerful force in local politics,” Schleicher said. “It’s not clear that’s true anymore, and it’s also not clear that the place where they’re powerful, which is in the board, is not the center of politics, right? ” Factions? Or fantasy? John DeStefano, New Haven’s mayor from 1994 to 2013, pushed back on a narrative of union dominance. For DeStefano, it is inaccurate to define alders elected to the board with union support as “purely UNITE HERE alders.” The former mayor said that the unions have had a much larger impact on the local Democratic party than they have had on city governance. “For sure, UNITE HERE in 2011 decided to, in quotes, take control of the Board of Alders. But I don’t think it was to some purpose,” DeStefano said. “Maybe we all imagined this to be a bigger thing than it was.” Morrison, the Ward 22 alder who is a steward at a state employees union, agrees. She said that UNITE HERE-affiliated alders check their union affiliation at the door. But she said they never stop thinking “about their role as an advocate, regardless if they’re a union steward or a legislator.” “It’s about making sure that the needs of the people are met,” Morrison added. “That’s why I say it’s kind of blurred. It’s the same goal.” Mauro, the chair of the Democratic Town Committee, referring to the unions, said: “I think sometimes people make them out to be the big bad wolf. And I think that’s an unfair criticism.” For Douglass, the Ward 2 alder and Local 35 vice president, concerns about tension between union interests and constituent interests are unwarranted. “No, we’re gonna shut that down right there,” he said. “They can keep their opinion. I know why I’m here. I’m here for my community.” Ian Dunn, the UNITE HERE unions’ spokesperson, said that he was “a little reluctant to engage overall.” “We don’t want to engage in this story,” Dunn said, clarifying that he was referring to the Locals 33, 34, 35 and 217 and New Haven Rising coalition. He questioned whether speaking about that coalition’s approach to politics was part of its current “message” or “program.” “The zeitgeist in New Haven has often been, like, ‘The unions are bad. What the unions are doing in politics is bad.’ We’re like, ‘Why? Why? Why?’ It’s working people representing working people. What’s wrong with that?” Dunn added. Dunn framed the Yale unions’ political involvement as self-evident rather than strategic. He rejects the premise that unions need a formal rationale for political engagement. The mayor and the Board of Alders before 2011 were much more closely aligned with Yale than they are now, Dunn said. “Is that good for New Haven to have the billion dollar company — the company town controlling everything?” he asked. Dunn said that he could not answer questions about the political engagement of Locals 34 and 35 without approval. He said that Marks, the New Haven Rising director, was “totally the right person” to answer questions about the Yale unions. Marks did not respond to repeated requests for comment. “The beauty of a union, right, is it’s not any individual’s choice to do anything,” Dunn said. “We collectively decide what we’re going to do. It makes things really slow and painful sometimes.” When it comes to the Board of Alders, some say the split between alders that are aligned with Yale’s unions and those that are not is overstated. For Redente, walking into his first board meeting with a president who had actively campaigned against him was “weird, to say the least” — but he said that those dynamics quickly evaporated. “I don’t think there’s camps. No, I would have thought that going in, but being there, no, I think everybody’s been very welcoming,” Redente added. Ward 7 alder Christine Kim ’99, who was sworn in just last month, agreed that there are not distinct camps and said she has not observed “this kind of conspiratorial type of work going on.” “I had heard that there’s like a UNITE HERE and non-UNITE HERE,” Theodore said. “And I just — I think it’s not that black and white.” Theodore, who beat the unions’ candidate, is now backing an effort to name a street corner after John Wilhelm ’67, formerly an influential Local 35 employee, a founding organizer of Local 34 and a longtime president of UNITE HERE itself. The signpost would stand at the corner of Elm and College streets. That’s where Old Campus faces First and Summerfield United Methodist Church, the headquarters of New Haven Rising and the Yale unions.

title: Police cameras keep eyes on New Haven. Will city scrutinize them back?
subtitle: In the past five years, the New Haven Police Department has expanded a surveillance system that lets crime analysts see what’s happening across the city. Questions about the system’s effectiveness and privacy implications remain unanswered.
author: Adele Haeg
text: At first, they are hard to notice. But start to see them, and they seem to be everywhere. In downtown New Haven, there are two on the public library across from the Green, another on City Hall, four more between the district attorney’s office and the courthouse: surveillance cameras. Each sends footage directly to New Haven’s Real Time Crime Center, a surveillance hub in the city police department where analysts monitor citywide cameras 16 hours a day, responding to officers’ calls. Cameras are always recording, and footage is stored for at least two weeks, according to a spokesperson for the New Haven Police Department. Alexandra Kenealy, one of the center’s crime analysts, first saw a Real Time Crime Center while interning at the Bridgeport Police Department in 2023. She remembers experiencing a “wow factor” when she stepped in, she said, gesturing at several TV-size screens in front of her desk in New Haven’s Real Time Crime Center. “I didn’t realize that you can monitor the city like this,” Kenealy said as she clicked through camera feeds, touring the city from her computer. “It’s insane. But I felt a sense of safety.” In the past five years, the New Haven Police Department has expanded its surveillance system, installing hundreds of cameras, license plate readers and ShotSpotter microphones — which detect gunshots — across the city. The police have asked residents to connect their Ring cameras to a department registry and deployed drones with video capability. In 2022, the department also started using StarChase, a technology that allows officers to shoot rubber GPS trackers at cars instead of pursuing them at high speed. Officers don’t need warrants to deploy StarChase, which Officer Christian Bruckhart, the department spokesperson, said is generally considered an exception to Fourth Amendment privacy rights. This surveillance system grants the police department “a lot of power,” Ward 29 Alder Brian Wingate, who chairs the aldermanic Public Safety Committee, said. Alders held public hearings before purchasing cameras, but Wingate said that most residents still are not aware of how extensive New Haven’s surveillance system is. And it is continuing to expand. Last month, the city received a $750,000 grant from the state to expand StarChase and purchase more automated license plate readers and tire deflation devices. Kenealy said last month that the police department was looking to hire another crime analyst. “The biggest question I have is, where do you cross the line with privacy?” AnneMarie Rivera-Berrios, chair of the city’s Civilian Review Board, which handles civilian complaints against the police, said. “Is it okay to put these cameras in place if we don’t have these answers yet?” Martin Peralta, Head Photography Editor A surveillance hub When a shot is fired in New Haven, all of the city’s police officers are instantly alerted on their department-issued smartphones. Each time Kenealy gets a ShotSpotter notification, she immediately pulls up camera feeds in the area of the gunshot as dispatchers send officers to the scene. Kenealy’s task is to give officers what experts call a “tactical advantage” when they arrive at the scene. That could mean describing a suspect’s car, clothing and direction of travel over the radio, or sending photos or videos to cruiser computers, so officers know exactly who they are looking for and where they are going. Stationary and police-car cameras can also automatically identify license plate data, which officers can access and send to Kenealy. “Ten, fifteen years ago, when we didn’t have all these cameras, these people could have been like a ghost,” Kenealy said. According to Kenealy, the police department doesn’t have cameras “in every single intersection, but for the most part, our areas are pretty well covered” — particularly high-traffic areas or crime “hotspots.” There are cameras in each of the city’s 10 policing districts, but the city does not disclose their locations. When she’s not actively responding to a crime, Kenealy keeps cameras around District 1 — which covers downtown — on the main screens, she said, “since there's a lot of traffic” there. Kenealy said she most frequently responds to car thefts and other larcenies. She mentioned a recent case in which Real Time Crime Center technology helped the department apprehend a woman who had stolen $100 from a CVS pharmacy. “A crime is a crime, and if you can get a record of it, it definitely just helps for closing a case out,” she said. “No crime is too small.” The department stores camera footage for 15 to 30 days, and the footage can be searched as needed by anyone in the department, according to Kenealy. Andrew Guthrie Ferguson, an expert on privacy and policing and a law professor at George Washington University, said a Real Time Crime Center gives officers an “impressive level of observational power.” New Haven Mayor Justin Elicker said in a phone interview that “reasonable people” can disagree about whether cameras make cities safer, or just give that illusion. Even without the guarantee that they will deter crime, or proof that they allow the department to solve crimes faster, Elicker said that giving up a measure of anonymity is “a sacrifice that most people are willing to make.” ‘We needed every tool possible’ In 2001, the New York Police Department became the first department in the United States to set up a Real Time Crime Center, according to Kimberly Przeszlowski, an assistant professor of criminal justice at Quinnipiac University. New Haven started to develop its own in 2020, which Przeszlowski called a year of “peak growth” in Real Time Crime Centers nationwide. There was “peer pressure” on New Haven to level up, she added. Wingate also attributed New Haven’s increased spending on surveillance technology to the police department’s retention issues in the early 2020s. “We needed every tool possible to keep New Haven safe,” he said, emphasizing that the department “didn’t have a lot of manpower.” Bridgeport and Hartford both have Real Time Crime Centers. According to a press release from Milestone — a company from which both Hartford and New Haven buy cameras — Hartford had a “staggering 442% increase in case solvability for firearm assault incidents with video evidence” during a two-year study starting in January 2022. The company appears not to have conducted such a study in New Haven. Since 2021, the city has committed about $13 million to contracts with Axon, a police and military technology company, and Fusus, a surveillance company Axon acquired in 2024. That money has come from the city budget and funds from the American Rescue Plan Act, a 2021 pandemic relief bill, according to public records of the agreements. In 2021, the NHPD signed its first five-year contract with Axon for tasers, improvements to interview room equipment, dashboard cameras for police cruisers and body-worn cameras, which the department began using in 2017. The city bought 500 surveillance cameras that year, 300 of which have been installed so far, according to Elicker. Axon provides storage for camera footage on a website called evidence.com, according to Ferguson, the privacy expert. Elicker said the companies “have made written commitments to us that we own the data.” Ferguson said Axon has been “more transparent” than other surveillance companies, conducting bias checks on their technology. Still, he said, “the technology is moving faster than law and regulation.” “I think the cameras and the money that we spent on the surveillance has been beneficial,” Wingate said.
