Wingate said surveillance is one aspect of a “holistic” approach to policing, and that a recent downturn in crime has come as a result of “communication with the community.” Acting Police Chief David Zannelli, who has been with the police department for 18 years and whom Elicker nominated last month to become the permanent chief, emphasized that the new technology, particularly the citywide cameras, has increased the department’s efficiency. “The expansion of the cameras has really helped us in the last couple of years to recover vehicles and people involved in gun violence rather quickly,” Zannelli said in a phone interview. “Now that we have equipment that is set up there, we're able to respond quickly, and not just respond quickly, find the right person and the right car.” Data as to whether the Real Time Crime Center is helping the department solves crime faster is “pending,” Zannelli said. Martin Peralta, Head Photography Editor Concerns about expansion Axon and Milestone, two surveillance companies that the department has partnered with, both sell artificial intelligence technology. Other Real Time Crime Centers employ two types of surveillance cameras: closed-circuit television, or CCTV, and cameras with the capacity for video analytics, which can turn a city into “a trackable object tracing system,” according to Ferguson. Officers can use video analytics to search footage for specific objects. Bruckhart said the department does not use video analytics. He declined to specify the make or model of its cameras. “We don’t provide specifics for the cameras as we don’t want the exact capabilities of them made public,” Bruckhart wrote in an email. He added that the department uses “a variety of vendors depending on who is cheaper.” “One can have video without video analytics. It is just super time consuming to find or use anything for court,” Ferguson wrote in an email to the News. According to Bruckhart, right now, the New Haven police’s crime analysts and detectives manually search footage themselves, although it’s inconvenient. When the city reupped its first contract with Axon in 2024, it signed on to the possibility of implementing more advanced Axon technology, including Axon Investigate Pro and Axon Redaction Assistant, which Bruckhart said the department is not currently using. “We have a contract with Axon in which various products are negotiated,” like tasers, body cameras and data storage, according to Bruckhart, but the department does not “provide details on those negotiations because it could affect future contracts.” Under the 2024 contract, the department also purchased Draft One — an AI-assisted police report drafting tool — and Axon video drones with the ability to play pre-recorded messages. The chief state’s attorney and the state’s attorneys for Connecticut’s 13 judicial districts this month issued a moratorium “on the use of AI tools for drafting or narrating police reports,” according to Alaine Griffin, a spokesperson for the chief state’s attorney. The department had been “experimenting” with Draft One, according to Bruckhart. ‘I wouldn’t want Big Brother’ The New Haven Independent reported in 2024 that the city operates 190 “regular cameras” and 183 automated license plate readers. Przeszlowski, the Quinnipiac University professor, said the New Haven Police Department has access to footage from over a thousand cameras, some of which are businesses’ or private Ring cameras. Chelsea Infinity-Gonzalez, the director of public policy and advocacy of the American Civil Liberties Union of Connecticut, said that when a city expands its surveillance system like New Haven has, “the question isn’t just about the number of cameras, but what rules are actually in place to govern that data.” The ACLU of Connecticut has advocated for limits on the police’s retention of license plate reader data, meaning that data would be deleted after seven days if not saved. With automated license plate readers, “you can query your plate and see where it’s been across multiple locations,” Infinity-Gonzalez said, “so from a privacy perspective there’s a strong argument and we support having stricter retention limits.” Zannelli proposed that the department should be able to keep license plate data for six months. There is no current Connecticut law limiting license plate data retention. “How do we know that they’re not just going to hide their vehicle used in the drive-by shooting in a garage somewhere for a week and then just start driving around?” Zannelli said. “I wouldn’t want Big Brother, but that’s not the intent of it.” In 2017, before New Haven had a Real Time Crime Center, Sean O’Brien, the founder of the Privacy Lab at Yale Law School’s Information Society Project, decided he would try to map the city’s surveillance cameras. When O’Brien posted pictures of cameras on State Street that year to a website called Open Street Map, he said he “would come back the next day and there would be a traffic cone over one of them.” Others, he said, “would look like they’d been hit with baseball bats, or would be spray painted black.” He hasn’t mapped the new cameras — the Open Street Map of New Haven’s surveillance cameras was last updated by the Privacy Lab in 2017. For O’Brien, the police department’s surveillance has gone too far. “If your neighbor had a camera pointing into your window and you knew that they were watching it on their monitor or on their smart TV in their living room, watching a feed of your living room,” he said, it would be “very visceral and very creepy.” But, he said, “when we have cameras everywhere outside all the time, even if they’re effectively doing this at a massive scale, we don't have the same feelings because we can externalize it and not have to think about it as strongly.” O’Brien believes New Haveners should be paying attention to surveillance. “People deeply care about privacy,” O’Brien said, “yet feel powerless to do anything about it.” O’Brien has seen what he calls “privacy nihilism” take over. “Every once in a while, you know, perhaps we need to take a peek in to see what’s going on with that kind of level of power,” Wingate said of police surveillance. New Haven’s Real Time Crime Center is in the police department’s headquarters at 1 Union Ave.

title: 91 percent of senior class has used AI for schoolwork, News survey finds
subtitle: Results from a survey of the class of 2026 indicate that the use of artificial intelligence is widespread among graduating seniors. According to the survey, 48.5 percent of respondents said they used AI in writing their senior theses.
author: Hari Viswanathan
text: This year’s graduating seniors were first years when OpenAI released ChatGPT. As the first class with the opportunity to use large language models like ChatGPT during every year of their college career, most seniors have now incorporated artificial intelligence into their lives to assist them for various purposes, from aiding with problem sets to researching theses. According to an anonymous survey conducted by the News and filled out by 172 seniors, 91 percent of the class of 2026 have used AI for schoolwork. Only 9.1 percent of students surveyed said they had never used AI for schoolwork. A majority of students — 67.5 percent — reported using AI sometimes, often or very often. More than 75 percent of respondents said they have used AI in a problem set. About 64 percent of respondents said they have used AI to write a paper, while 48.5 percent of the surveyed seniors said they used AI to write their senior theses — slightly less than the 51.5 percent that said they did not. Joey Tan ’26, who is a computer science major, described AI as a “supplementary tool” that she utilizes alongside other forms of technology. “I’ve used it to look up things that I want, like a more in-depth answer compared to Google, for instance,” Tan said in a phone interview. “Or I’ll do a mix of Google searching and AI.” She said that she thinks one of the most evident impacts of AI is on office hours, adding that students can now use AI, instead of tutors and undergraduate learning assistants, to teach them content and help them through problems. “It’s kind of unfortunate, because I made so many of my computer science friends in office hours,” Tan said. “It was, like, a really important community for me. It was how I found people that take upper class electives with me later on.” Aviv Melamud ’26, another computer science major, said that using Claude — the large language model developed by Anthropic — to work through problems is like having a virtual teaching assistant. “It basically killed office hours,” Melamud said in a phone interview. Who is using AI? Male respondents were more likely to report that they used AI for schoolwork than female respondents. While 16.7 percent of male respondents said they used AI “very often” for schoolwork, only 1.6 percent of female respondents said they did. According to the survey results, students majoring in the sciences were most likely to report AI use for schoolwork, followed by those in the social sciences, those in interdisciplinary majors and those in the humanities. Brian Moore ’26 noted that in the humanities, AI can serve a similar function as writing partners in the Poorvu Center for Teaching and Learning, improving things like grammar, syntax and diction. “I tend to see this as polarizing. There’s a lot of folks who won’t touch AI with a 10-foot pole, and others who will readily adopt it,” Moore, who is majoring in humanities, said in a phone interview. Melamud said he thinks AI will not render STEM classes obsolete. He compared the experience of learning computer science with AI to that of learning math with the aid of a calculator. Professors will have to grapple seriously with the advancement of AI technology, Moore said, but he added that there are creative ways in which professors can weave AI into class assignments and pedagogy. “I guess, in some ways, AI is prompting us to re-examine what the philosophy of education is, right?” Moore said. ChatGPT was released on Nov. 30, 2022.

title: With high hopes, Yale continues to invest in Upper Science Hill
subtitle: The University has poured hundreds of millions of dollars into the development of Upper Science Hill, which University leaders say will significantly improve research opportunities at Yale. The first phase is set to be completed this fall.
author: Sara Agrawal
text: One corner on campus has experienced a particularly expensive transformation over the past four years. The University had poured hundreds of millions into the development of Upper Science Hill, with the aim of setting Yale apart in the fields of physics, the applied sciences and engineering. University leaders have consistently emphasized their hopes to place Yale at the forefront of cutting-edge scientific fields like quantum computing. “The USHD reflects a long-term commitment to securing Yale’s leadership in scientific discovery and innovation in order to contribute meaningfully to addressing the world’s present and future challenges,” Michael Crair, Yale’s vice provost for research, wrote in an email to the News, referring to the Upper Science Hill Development project. In a February interview with the News, Yale President Maurie McInnis identified investing in the physical sciences and engineering as the “top priority” of the University, and in her end-of-year message to the Yale community, she singled out the University’s “strides in reimagining Science Hill.” When Linda McMahon, the United States’ secretary of education, toured Yale in April, she called the construction on Upper Science Hill Construction “incredible.” Yale’s 2023–24 financial report stated that out of the University’s $614 million in capital spending, which includes “renovations” and “acquisitions and new construction,” 12 percent — around $75 million — went into biological and physical sciences. That figure rose to over $190 million — 29 percent of $661 million — in the 2025 fiscal year . When asked why he thought Yale was investing so heavily in engineering and physical sciences, physics professor Karsten Heeger emphasized the importance of Yale’s frontier scholarship in those fields as well as the humanities. “Yale has always been leading in various fields, including the humanities, for example,” Heeger, who is also the director of the Yale Wright Laboratory for experimental nuclear physics, particle physics and astrophysics, said. “Science and engineering and technology is absolutely essential for society and our modern world. If we want to be a leading research university, we have to be cutting-edge in those fields.” The development of Upper Science Hill is a decade-long project that has been planned in two phases. According to Heeger, the first phase, which began in 2022 and broke ground in 2024, is set to be completed this fall. This phase involves the construction of a new Advanced Instrumentation Development Center and replacing an old part of the Wright Lab. Heeger said that the renovation will bolster the Wright Lab with “state-of-the-art laboratories that will give us the vibration isolation, the environmental control, all of the things that we need, the infrastructure for cutting-edge research.” That transformation will provide new research opportunities for both undergraduate and graduate students, Heeger added. The second phase of the Upper Science Hill development project will see the completion of a new Physical Sciences and Engineering Building, or PSEB, Heeger said. “The idea was to build a building complex that brings together research groups from physics, physical sciences, and the engineering departments and mainly with the focus on quantum science and material science,” he said. Crair wrote that the building complex will foster “cross-disciplinary collaboration.” In total, the building will house around 50 research groups, according to Heeger. Heeger also said that the physics department has made new appointments in quantum science and atomic, molecular and optical physics, but that exact final number “remains to be seen.” The development of Upper Science Hill follows a 2018 report of the University Science Strategy Committee, or USSC, which states that “tomorrow’s leaders need a solid grounding in STEM so they can understand the world and shape its future” and outlines investments the University might make to improve STEM at Yale. The report suggested that the University invest in graduate students, advance instrumentation, update core facilities and promote diversity in the STEM pipeline. The report also emphasized that the School of Engineering and Applied Sciences, or SEAS, would play a vital role in the future of science at Yale and “must be fully integrated into the fabric of the University.” In 2022, following another report from a SEAS strategic planning committee in 2021, the University announced major investments in applied sciences and engineering and the separation of SEAS faculty from the Faculty of Arts and Science. A focus on quantum Quantum science has been one of the focuses of Yale’s investments in engineering and applied science. The field, which studies the smallest units within atoms that act both as particles and as waves, is used both in physics and in computing and was one of the “five ideas of priority investment” highlighted in the 2018 USSC report. Speaking about the research in Wright Lab, Heeger said that “quantum science is intrinsic to all what we do.” Shaun Pexton ’26, the co-president of Yale Undergraduate Quantum Computing, called quantum the “shining beacon of Yale engineering” in an interview with the News. Pexton said that he’s noticed growing attention around quantum during his time at Yale as the “race” of quantum technology has made its way into “political conversations” and as Yale has gained recognition as a place where “a lot of cool stuff is happening.” In her end-of-year message, President McInnis said that the new facilities on Science Hill will “supercharge quantum science at Yale.” The Yale Quantum Institute will be relocated from Hillhouse Avenue to the PSEB, according to its website . In the message, President McInnis also mentioned professor Michel Devoret, who won a Nobel Prize in 2025 for his work in quantum physics , and QuantumCT , a nonprofit launched in 2022 by Crair and Pamir Alpay, the interim provost of the University of Connecticut. McInnis’ wrote in the end-of-year message that through quantum technology, “we will make our home state more attractive to start-ups” and “create a talented workforce in this area.” When asked why he thought the University was heavily investing in and promoting quantum, Pexton said that technological development, especially in AI and quantum, have become indicators of a university’s advancement. “Yale wants to be at the forefront, and they are at the forefront,” Pexton said. The construction on Upper Science Hill is located at 268 to 280 Whitney Ave.

title: After admin banner ban, YSE grads don anti-ICE t-shirts in class photo
subtitle: In a student-organized unofficial class photo, members of the Yale School of the Environment’s class of 2026 wore shirts depicting melting ice, a double entendre intended to highlight both the changing political and global climates.
author: Michelle So
text: Since the school’s founding in 1900, graduates of the Yale School of the Environment have taken an annual class photo behind Marsh Hall, where the original Yale Forest School was located . For over a century, these photos have captured the school through class size increases, the emergence of color photography and shifts in student body demographics. In recent years, the class photos have become a platform for School of the Environment students to make broader political statements. Members of the class of 2024 posed with pro-Palestinian protest materials, according to members of class of 2026, before the school updated its class photo protocol to prohibit signs and banners. This year, some members of the class of 2026 organized an unofficial class photo in which students donned t-shirts that depicted melting ice cubes. “The shirts were meant to be symbolic and hold two meanings: climate change is melting ice, and abolish ICE,” Riki Bertoldi ENV ’26, one of the photo’s organizers, wrote in a text to the News. On April 29, after the graduating students lined up for their official photo, nearly all of them stayed behind to change into the shirts and pose for a new photo, according to Bertoldi. She said that the idea for the unofficial photo had been conceived “months before.” Emily Kaufman ENV ’26 wrote in an email to the News that past students have presented more “explicit messages” in their class photos. “I really loved the class of 2024 photo when we saw a lot of protest signs, and I believe there was a big banner that said, ‘War is an environmental issue,’” Adrian Huq ENV ’26 said in a Zoom interview. “That really resonated with me as a peace advocate and anti-militarist sort of climate activist.” Huq said that administrators discouraged protest-related photoshoots after the 2024 display. Paige Stein, the school’s chief communications and marketing officer, wrote in an email to the News that in order to “emphasize the collective, inclusive nature of our school community,” the School of the Environment updated its “class photo protocol.” “Official photos are now taken without banners or signs,” Stein added. “The School of the Environment adheres to the university’s wider policy on freedom of expression.” According to the website of the office of the secretary and vice president for university life, the University prohibits large “banners held by multiple individuals” at outdoor events, but does not specify a policy prohibiting signs or banners in official photos. This year, as the annual class photo neared, Huq and Bertoldi said they worked to navigate around this updated policy change. “We decided to get creative and use textiles instead as a medium to express a collective statement in a way that still felt visible and meaningful to our community,” Bertoldi said. According to Bertoldi, Kaufman led much of the planning and coordination, while Brandon Hoak ENV ’26 created the initial design, produced the stencil and led the screen-printing process. “We made sure to communicate our plans with both international students and the broader class and asked whether people would feel comfortable with the shirts being worn during the official class photo,” Bertoldi wrote in an email. “Some students expressed discomfort, so we decided to take the shirt photo separately after the official class photo.” Bertoldi said she and other organizers invited other students to join the unofficial photoshoot through a WhatsApp group chat called “YSE Class of 2026,” which included every member of the graduating class. Students who wished to opt out were given the option to. According to Kaufman, the photo’s organizers “felt it was important to continue the tradition in a more subtle yet still powerful way” after they had elected not to wear the shirts during the official photo. “Through the drawing of melting ice, we wanted to show that environmentalism cannot be separated from the injustices currently facing immigrant communities and that YSE will continue our longstanding tradition of speaking out despite administrative pushback,” Kaufman said. Marsh Hall is located at 360 Prospect St.

title: Yale is building 263 new wells for geothermal energy
subtitle: Geothermal energy offers a cleaner alternative to traditional carbon-based fuels. As Yale invests in hundreds of geothermal wells, the technology may be the University’s ticket to fulfilling its zero-emissions commitment.
author: Michelle So
text: Natural gas provides roughly half of Connecticut’s energy, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration. While burning the fuel produces half the emissions as does burning coal, natural gas is not as green as one might think. Any carbon-based form of energy produces carbon dioxide and other greenhouse gases when combusted for energy. Greenhouse gas emissions are responsible for trapping heat in Earth’s atmosphere, which contributes to global warming. Yale has committed itself to achieving zero emissions by 2050, a goal towards which the University has slowly been advancing. Alternative forms of energy, including geothermal technologies, may be the ticket there. “Yale is moving toward geothermal energy as part of the university’s commitment to achieve a zero‐emissions (zero‐carbon) campus by 2050,” Andy Bromage, the sustainability communications officer for the Yale Office of Facilities, wrote in an email to the News. In order to achieve that goal, Bromage said, drilling is underway to install 263 geothermal wells on Upper Science Hill. The new plant will support buildings including the Yale Science Building, the Sloane Physics Laboratory, the Sterling Chemistry Laboratory, the Kline Chemistry Laboratory, the Chemistry Research Building and the Bass Center. Electricity is generated when water is heated to turn turbines that produce steam. Yale currently employs several means of energy generation, including natural gas power plants on Tower Parkway and Congress Avenue, wind turbines on the roof of the Becton Engineering and Applied Sciences building and solar panels on Kroon Hall, Fisher Hall and West Campus. The core change in Yale’s energy-generating system involves the replacement of steam-based heating with low‐temperature water — heated to around 120 to 140 degrees Fahrenheit — generated by an electric heat pump that will draw heat from deep underground. “Any move away from using fossil resources (carbon) and toward using renewable resources (i.e., those that don’t move carbon from underground storage to the atmosphere) is a step in the right direction and is a good thing!” Hanno Erythropel, a researcher at the Yale University Center for Green Chemistry and Green Engineering, wrote in an email to the News. “That is certainly the case for the Yale geothermal energy project.” Erythropel added that more specificity regarding the amount of emitted carbon dioxide that could be offset by the project would be helpful. Yale’s move toward geothermal energy follows a trend of universities moving towards cleaner energy. In 2024, The New York Times reported that other universities and colleges, including Princeton, Carleton College and Ball State University, were also tapping into the technology. “I think it’s a good push for Yale as a university and as a whole to make good on their carbon capture initiative while being more sustainable,” Naomi Orcel ’27, the president of Club Geo, an Earth and planetary science student organization, said. Orcel said she was initially irked that the construction on Upper Science Hill took away parking spaces for faculty, but that she changed her mind on the project after hearing its mission. “I think it’s a really good thing that we’re using geothermal energy,” she added, “because it’s not an energy source people usually think to use; people usually think like solar or wind.” The shift toward geothermal energy extends beyond Yale. New Haven received around $9.5 million from the Environmental Protection Agency to build a networked geothermal system to provide clean heating and cooling to the New Haven Union Station. The funding would also support the construction of a mixed-income housing development adjacent to the station. Yale’s Upper Science Hill Development’s new facilities for quantum science, engineering and materials science are designed to hopefully support both new construction and existing Science Hill laboratory buildings by providing electrified thermal energy and reducing the need to add capacity to Yale’s existing power plants and steam distribution system. The Upper Science Hill Development is scheduled to be completed in 2030.

title: New student-built app aims to make dining hall menus more accessible
subtitle: YaleMeals, an app that was recently developed by students in the Yale Computer Society, allows users to view the menus at various dining halls, as well as input their dietary preferences and coordinate meals with friends.
author: Anya Geist
text: When Yassin Mudawi ’28, a transfer student, arrived on campus last fall, he said he found Yale’s dining system confusing: There were so many dining facilities, all with varying closures, transfer restrictions and menu changes. Mudawi recently led a team at the Yale Computer Society to launch YaleMeals, an app that aims to offer an intuitive, easy-to-use menu guide to Yale’s dining options. The app was downloaded over 700 times by April 7, according to Mudawi. “For a new student coming here, it seemed to be a pain point for me personally, and how I approach software is I find a pain point in my life and then try to fix it,” Mudawi said in an interview, referring to Yale’s dining system. Mudawi wanted to create an easily navigable app. The Yale Hospitality website, which uses Nutrislice to display menus, has a lot of “nested pages,” Mudawi said. Nutrislice requires users to visit new pages to view a dining hall’s menu for each meal on a given day. Mudawi said he brought his idea to Yale Computer Society co-President Ryan Fernandes ’28 last fall, and the club decided to take on YaleMeals as a project. On YaleMeals, Mudawi tried to make it simple to switch between locations, meals and days. The app also allows students to customize its settings, including for their dietary preferences. The app features a “Friends” tab, where users can invite and schedule meals with their peers. “I noticed there’s a big meal culture here at Yale, where everyone wants to grab, like, lunch together or dinner together, and it’s really wholesome. So why not try to promote that and encourage that?” Mudawi said. Mudawi said he and his team hope students can use YaleMeals even in the case that Yale Hospitality’s database changes. He added that he built the YaleMeals code to make it easy to switch to a new interface for a changed database. Yale Hospitality spokesperson Alexa Gotthardt wrote in an email to the News: “We value student innovation and recognize the creativity students bring in developing new tools and platforms. At the same time, we encourage students to rely on Yale Hospitality’s official menus page for the most accurate and up-to-date menu information.” Other students have created similar apps to YaleMeals in the past, including Bulldog Menus, which was founded by Edmundo Chavez Ramirez ’28. Regarding similar apps created outside of the Yale Computer Society, Lily Lin ’27, the club’s co-president, said, “Our goal is not to monopolize the market.” YaleMeals builds on the work of Yale Menus, a previously popular app that also listed each dining hall’s menus. Yale Menus was originally launched by Eric Foster ’20 and his brother David Foster ’23 in 2017, and it was relaunched by David Foster and Erik Boesen ’24 in 2020, Boesen wrote to the News. After Boesen graduated, the app became challenging to maintain. “We negotiated a license to sell Menus to Yale Hospitality over a period of roughly 4-5 years,” Boesen wrote to the News. Ultimately, the sale fell through. Boesen, realizing the app was out of date due to changes to Yale Hospitality’s databases and programming interfaces, passed it on to the Yale Computer Society. While Yale Menus was not a project created through the club, Boesen wrote, he had been a club member as an undergraduate. “Yale Hospitality has previously explored partnership opportunities related to student-developed dining apps, including the ‘Yale Menus’ app,” Gotthardt wrote to the News. “At that time, we determined it was not the right fit, largely due to the operational complexity involved in maintaining a separate platform alongside our existing systems, which serve the same purpose.” Ruby Lytton-Hirsch ’29 frequently uses Yale’s Nutrislice app to look at lunch menus around campus. Looking at the menu in advance allows her to see whether she prefers the Greens and Grains option or the regular offering. However, Lytton-Hirsch said the Nutrislice app can be challenging to use, particularly because she has to scroll to the bottom of the page to see the main options for each meal. Lytton-Hirsch said she thought the YaleMeals app looked “prettier and easier to navigate” than Nutrislice. Similarly, Hrishita Shah ’28 said she found YaleMeals more “intuitive” to use than Nutrislice, though she added that she does not mind using Nutrislice because she has gotten used to it. Shah, however, noted that the YaleMeals app lacks reviews of the menu items. “The old Yale Menus app, it had a lot of reviews, like the Commons cookies had, like, 50 hearts,” Shah said, adding that “not a lot of people” use YaleMeals. “So, like, the s’mores brownies have zero ratings.” Rishi Sankhe ’28 sometimes uses YaleMeals, though he does not always feel the need to check dining hall menus before visiting. He finds the app easy to use, he said. Sankhe noted that he thought the YaleMeals team was continuing to make good changes to the app, including adding pictures of the dishes. Some students have never used YaleMeals. Zach Pan ’27 said he had not heard of YaleMeals or even the Nutrislice website. “I show up at the dining hall and eat what’s there,” Pan said. “I know I’m not going to spend additional funds on food from other sources. So I enter the dining hall, and I eat the food in the dining hall.” The dining halls serve chicken tenders for lunch every other Thursday.

title: Yale study finds link between cancer and living near factory farms
subtitle: A recent Yale study published in the journal Environmental Research found that living near concentrated animal feedlots or factory farms may be linked to higher cancer rates.
author: Michelle So
text: Earlier this year, Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. announced a revamp of the familiar food pyramid, with protein sources like red meat and full-fat dairy at the top of the list of recommended foods. Yet, certain studies have found consumption of red meat to be linked to cardiovascular diseases and cancers of the colon . Now, simply living near sites of red meat production may be linked to cancers as well. A Yale study published in the journal Environmental Research examined whether living in areas with higher densities of animal feeding operations and concentrated animal feeding operations is associated with cancer incidence. The feeding operations, commonly known as factory farms, often contain livestock in cramped conditions. Concentrated operations exceed a threshold population of 1,000 beef cattle, 700 dairy cows or 2,500 hogs. “These facilities are known sources of environmental pollution, including air pollutants (e.g., ammonia, particulate matter, hydrogen sulfide) and water contaminants such as nitrates, which may have implications for human health,” Jiyoung Son, a researcher at the School of the Environment and the paper’s first author, wrote in an email to the News. Using county-level data from California, Iowa and Texas between 2000 and 2021, the researchers found that counties with higher factory farm density had higher overall cancer incidence. The increased cancer incidence prevailed despite variations in factory farm makeup; for example, dairy operations are more common in California, swine in Iowa and beef cattle in Texas. Cancer isn’t the only risk associated with living near a factory farm. When animals live in close proximity with one another, disease transmission becomes nearly unavoidable. Farmers often add antibiotics to animal feed to prevent disease-related deaths and bolster livestock growth. Overuse of antibiotics can cause bacteria to develop antibiotic resistance. When runoff from these feedlots enters the environment, these superbugs may infect humans and other animals and remain unresponsive to treatment — a scenario that is dangerous to both livestock and human health. Cole Haynes ’27, the co-president of Plant Forward Yalies, a student group that promotes sustainable food systems, said he was not shocked by the study’s results. “While the findings of this Yale study are deeply concerning, they are unfortunately not surprising. The public health impacts of animal agriculture are widespread and well documented,” Haynes wrote in an email to the News. The study’s researchers still say more detailed research is needed. Son suggested implementing environmental monitoring, especially of air quality and drinking water contamination, and improving waste management and reducing emissions. Consuming red and processed meats is also associated with “pro-inflammatory mechanisms” that increase gastrointestinal and colon cancers and cardiovascular disease, John Dinkler, a cardiologist and the associate head of Timothy Dwight College, said. Instead, Dinkler recommends that people switch to a more plant-based diet. Changing one’s eating habits has marked benefits on cardiovascular disease and reduces cancer risk. A plant-based diet tends to lower blood pressure, improve cholesterol levels, maintain healthy blood sugar levels and reduce inflammation, he wrote in an email to the News. All of those changes improve the health of blood vessels throughout the cardiovascular system, reducing the risk of heart attacks and strokes. “I’ve actually had many patients lower doses of cholesterol, blood pressure, and diabetes medications (or come off medications completely) after changing to a plant-based diet,” Dinkler wrote. “It’s impressive to see!” According to Haynes, a plant-based diet has been shown to be one of the most effective ways to reduce an individual’s carbon footprint by 75 percent, while also decreasing reliance on systems that harm human health, animals and the environment. “Even small changes, like replacing meat with plant-based alternatives, opting for plant-based desserts, or swapping dairy milk for oat or soy, can have a significant impact,” Haynes said. An estimated 99 percent of the United States’ farmed animals live on factory farms, according to the Sentience Institute, a think tank.

title: Students host Claude hackathon, promoting the product on campus
subtitle: The event aimed to introduce students without significant experience using artificial intelligence to Anthropic’s model. At the hackathon, participants built applications using the AI tools.
author: Melissa Adamantidi
text: Yale students gathered for the first Claude hackathon on Yale’s campus on Sunday. They competed for various cash prizes, and their participation guaranteed six months of free access to the artificial intelligence assistant Claude Pro. The hackathon served as the capstone event for the Yale Claude Builder Club’s first semester on campus. The group has run weekly programming since the start of the spring term. Nicholas Gertler ’27, who co-leads the club alongside Lucas Santos ’27, described the event as the culmination of a semester-long effort to lower the barrier to entry for building with AI. Gertler and Santos are both campus ambassadors for Anthropic, a role they obtained by applying through the company’s ambassador program. “This is the first Claude hackathon on Yale’s campus,” Gertler said. “One of the things we’re really committed to is supporting students who haven’t built applications or technology before and helping them get started.” The competition invited participants to build applications using Anthropic’s artificial intelligence tools. Santos said the hackathon’s five tracks were drawn directly from an essay by Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei titled “ Machines of Loving Grace ,” which outlines a vision for how AI might benefit humanity. The tracks, covering biology and physical health, neuroscience and mental health, economic empowerment and education, governance and collaboration and creative flourishing, were intended to push participants toward projects with real-world impact, Santos said. “It’s basically AI for humanity,” Santos said. “How do we create meaningful lives? How do we live meaningfully? How do we be creative?” Claude’s ambassador program, which operates across hundreds of universities globally, aims to expand student awareness and use of Claude, Santos said. According to Santos, their work this semester has included demonstration sessions, social media outreach and the Claude Builder Club meetings, where attendees can receive free Claude Pro access and learn foundational skills like building personal websites and getting started with Claude Code. The ambassador program is structured on a semester-by-semester basis. Gertler described his role as a liaison between Yale student organizations and Anthropic, responsible for promoting the company’s tools on campus. Santos said the club’s programming has been deliberately designed to serve students who are curious about AI but do not know where to begin. “The big thing was introducing it to people who are less technical, who really want to get interested but don’t quite know where to start,” Santos said. “So it was a very accessible demonstration.” The event drew participants from a wide range of backgrounds. Among those present were students from the School of Management, humanities majors and first-year undergraduates with no prior coding experience, as well as STEM students. Justin Jin ’29, a computer science and psychology student, said he was drawn to the event by the chance to do something actionable and to see what was possible with Claude Code in a compressed timeframe. “It’s a really good way to learn,” Jin said. “Just for yourself and also just having something that you can showcase to others as well.” Jin added that he sees particular promise for AI tools in fields where technology and the humanities intersect. He said that for students interested in game development, for instance, Claude could handle scripting, programming and animation work that might otherwise be out of reach. “I think it really helps beginners to really get into those processes a lot easier,” he said. Sophie Gal SOM ’26 said she had experience with AI and is currently exploring a new startup focused on mental healthcare. She said the prospect of building something tangible drew her to the event. Duurenzaya Bukhbat SOM ’26 said AI has become a fixture of his daily routine. “At this point, it has become a part of my life, it’s like a day-to-day assistant, so it’s important for me to get to know it better,” Bukhbat said, adding that while he finds it an exciting time to be a student, the same cannot be said of the job market. Claude was developed by Anthropic, an AI company that was founded in 2021.

title: Tech startup scene on the rise at Yale, student founders say
subtitle: Student groups have been organizing a number of events focused on promoting students’ startups, as well as connecting them with professionals and potential investors.
author: Anya Geist
text: Across campus, students are noticing a growing entrepreneurial scene as more Yalies turn toward tech-driven innovation. Student organizations are expanding programming, creating networks with investors and arranging opportunities for founders. Over the last two weekends, students have organized events inviting investors and other student entrepreneurs to campus, and participants at those events pointed towards a proliferation of similar events at Yale. “The Yale builder community has been growing a lot in recent years,” Filippo Fonseca ’28, a student who participated in at least one of the recent events, said. At YPitch, an event run by Yale Undergraduate Capital Partners on Friday, undergraduates, graduate students and alumni representing 10 startups pitched their projects to an audience of peers and venture capitalists, most of whom were Yale alumni, according to Mae Chew ’27, a co-president of the group. YPitch was the “centerpiece” of a week of panels and events with founders and venture capitalists, Chew wrote to the News. The weekend before, Major League Hacking, an organization that runs student hackathons worldwide, hosted YHack, a two-day hackathon that drew more than 600 students from around the world to build code and hardware and network with sponsors. Some Yale students said events like YHack increase Yale’s visibility in the startup world. YHack and YPitch are only a few events in a series of efforts by various Yale clubs to promote student entrepreneurship. The Yale Entrepreneurial Society will be hosting 12 student founders in its first student hacker house in San Francisco this summer. The Yale Computer Society hosted a hackathon centered on agentic AI last month and hopes to run further hackathons in the fall, its co-president, Ryan Fernandes ’28, told the News. Startups “still need a lot of talent,” Steven Zhou ’27, the co-director of YHack, said. “These tech companies, and a lot of startups, have a lot of trouble finding them, so I thought it’s just like a perfect opportunity to reinvigorate a club,” he added, referring to YHack. Before the COVID-19 pandemic, YHack drew more than 1,400 hackers to Payne Whitney Gymnasium, according to a News article from 2015. In 2024, though, the event only drew around 250 participants , a 2024 News story reported. This year, from the 600 participants, 244 projects were submitted, and around 40 winners were chosen from categories that included hardware, personal artificial intelligence agents and best solo hack. Though most students were undergraduates from the United States, some participants came from graduate programs or schools abroad. Prizes were worth upwards of $1,000 for the winning teams. At YHack, Fonseca and his co-founder Emir Ahmed ’28 created PosturePoke, a wearable posture-correcting device. For Fonseca, YHack was just one of an increasing number of startup events at Yale. He mentioned that more investors visit campus for events and that Y Combinator, a startup accelerator and venture capital firm, has visited campus twice within the last year to meet with students. So, when he and Ahmed saw YHack announced on LinkedIn, they were excited to participate in a meaningful building event on campus, Fonseca said. At YHack, students also had the opportunity to meet representatives from some of YHack’s sponsors, learn more about their products and network with them. Kyle McVeigh, a founding engineer at LavaAI, an AI-based marketing tool, said that his goal by attending YHack was to encourage students to use LavaAI and gather feedback from them. “Being where the engineers are, being where the programmers are,” McVeigh said, “it’s exactly where we want to be.” In addition to providing an interesting and meaningful experience to participants, Zhou and his co-directors, Michael Gao ’27 and Perryn Chang ’27, said they hope that Yale’s reputation in the startup world will improve due to events like the hackathon. “You don’t think of us as a tech powerhouse,” Zhou said, referring to Yale. Zoya Hussain ’29, who helped organize YHack, said she believes the event could expand in future years. This year, for example, the hackathon largely took place on select floors of Kline Tower, 17 Hillhouse, and in Davies Auditorium and O.C. Marsh Lecture Hall. Hussein noted that it would have been nice to have a larger space for participants, such as Payne Whitney Gymnasium, which YHack used in past years. “I think it was just us having to redevelop our like ethos and like our reputation in the eyes of Yale so that hopefully in future years, we can provide a better space for our participants,” Hussain said. Hussain, who is also a member of Yale Undergraduate Capital Partners, and was working at the welcome table for YPitch, emphasized the importance of events like YPitch, where many students had built projects over the course of months. “I think it’s really important to be able to have a space where students have the opportunity and agency to explore their ideas for the first time, and people who are genuinely putting faith in that and willing to back and support those students,” Hussain said. Though Hussain is only a first year, she has already noticed the energy around startups at Yale. She mentioned that at the beginning of the year, a presentation by the Yale Entrepreneurial Society said that more Yalies than ever are taking gap semesters to work on building projects. “As a computer science major, every other conversation I’ve had is with students who are cultivating and fostering their ideas for the next big thing or some solution that they finally feel empowered to start pursuing because of the wealth of resources and support at Yale, which is really amazing to see,” Hussain said. Chew emphasized that the goal of YPitch was to expose students to investors and support them as they seek to grow their projects, while also signaling to venture capitalists that “there is serious founder talent coming out of Yale.” Chew attributed some of the growth of the Yale startup scene to the increasing accessibility of the tech world. “The barrier to building has dropped dramatically: AI tools, no-code platforms, and cloud infrastructure mean you can prototype something meaningful as an undergrad,” Chew wrote. “Students are increasingly thinking of building as a viable and exciting path, not just something that happens after you’ve done two years in consulting or banking, and we want to help support it the best way possible.” Many YHack participants worked on their projects in Steep Cafe.

title: Hacking helpfully, Yale senior co-founds a startup and wins rewards
subtitle: A practice called ethical hacking helps identify software vulnerabilities so they can be fixed. Alex Schapiro ’26 has won over 2 million airline miles from United and co-founded a company using AI tools to pick out cybersecurity weaknesses.
author: Melissa Adamantidi
text: A Yale senior who earned 2 million United Airlines miles for identifying a significant vulnerability in the airline’s computer systems has co-founded an artificial intelligence cybersecurity startup to help companies fix software weaknesses before they can be exploited. The startup, Strix, founded by Alex Schapiro ’26 and Ahmed Allam, develops automated tools that scan code and applications for security flaws — a practice known as ethical hacking. According to Allam, Strix is currently in the seed stage and working with enterprise clients as it expands its platform and product capabilities. The idea for the company grew out of the founders’ interest in identifying how systems break. Allam wrote to the News that his background in AI and early experience exploring software vulnerabilities led him to build tools that could scale that process. “I was always curious about how systems worked, where they broke, and how they could be improved,” he wrote. Schapiro’s path into cybersecurity followed a similar trajectory, beginning with hands-on experimentation while building applications in high school. “In high school I would be building apps where I would need to scrape data from websites, and that data wasn’t always easily accessible. So I would find ways around it to get the data I needed, and often when doing that I would find much more data was public than the website was expecting,” Schapiro wrote to the News. Over time, that curiosity developed into a sustained focus on identifying vulnerabilities and reporting them responsibly. “My mom also is in the cybersecurity field so I have always been exposed to it,” he wrote. “Beyond that, I love helping people out, so hacking a company and telling them how to fix it is a great way to protect the users because otherwise their user data could be exposed.” Earlier this year, Schapiro identified vulnerabilities in United Airlines’ systems, earning a total of 2.6 million airline miles through the company’s bug bounty program — including one reward of 2 million miles for a particularly major vulnerability — he told the News. “This recent experience with United also was super memorable — my first ever official bug bounty was from United my freshman year of college, for 250k miles, but this time I was able to get the maximum reward possible,” he wrote. Schapiro wrote that he has seen a rise in vulnerabilities as artificial intelligence tools make it easier to build and deploy software quickly, often without strong security protections. “Especially in the age of AI I have seen way more vulnerabilities than ever before, which is why I decided to co-found this company that can help companies who are building fast using AI to ensure the code they are pushing is secure,” he wrote. Strix aims to address that issue by automating the process of identifying vulnerabilities, allowing developers to continuously test their applications rather than relying on periodic audits. “Normal penetration tests, what it is called when you pay someone to proactively hack your app, cost anywhere from $10-20,000, so this is a much more affordable way to make sure your app is secure, and it does it every day on every code change so you can be confident about the code you are pushing,” Schapiro wrote. For Schapiro, the distinction between ethical and unethical hacking is defined by how vulnerabilities are handled. “It is not ethical to hack an app and exploit it, but it is ethical to hack an app without changing any information on it and just disclosing it right away to the company so they can fix it before a bad guy targets them,” he wrote. He added that companies’ responses to vulnerability disclosures can vary. Some get angry or defensive, he wrote, but most react positively. Beyond the company itself, ethical hacking raises broader legal questions about how cybersecurity is regulated. Yale Law School professor Scott Shapiro said in an interview with the News that while ethical hacking is intended to improve security, it exists within complex legal boundaries. “Ethical hacking is understood to be a practice whereby a hacker tries to find and or exploit vulnerabilities for the purposes of making products safer,” Shapiro said.“So it’s ethical hacking because you're doing it because you're trying to harden the system.” However, he emphasized that intent alone does not determine legality. Shapiro also questioned whether current legal frameworks adequately incentivize companies to prioritize security and warned that artificial intelligence may intensify existing vulnerabilities. “So I think it’s just like spewing bugs into cyberspace,” he said. Yale’s Digital Ethics Center, founded in 2023, focuses on the ethical and societal implications of emerging technologies.

title: Recycling plant turns glass to cement that supports Yale construction
subtitle: Raw building materials are highly carbon-intensive. The News joined a tour of a Connecticut facility that is helping Yale lower its carbon emissions by making a cement substitute out of recycled glass.
author: Michelle So
text: 3.5 million glass bottles on the wall. 3.5 million glass bottles. Take one down, pass it around, and it might end up as cement in Yale’s Physical Sciences and Engineering Building under construction. Yale’s ongoing construction on upper Science Hill will feature 600,000 gross square feet for the School of Engineering and Applied Science and more. In accordance with Yale’s promise to achieve net-zero carbon emissions on campus by 2035 and zero actual carbon emissions by 2050, the development will include a thermal utilities plant that will produce energy for the facilities. But even if operations are sustained by on-site energy production, creating a building from raw materials is quite carbon-intensive. Instead, some of Yale’s building materials will use Pozzotive, an industrial filler made from recycled glass such as drink bottles — the equivalent of 3.5 million of them, to be precise, according to a slideshow used during a recent tour of the glass recycling facility. At the tour last month, organized by several students at Yale’s School of the Environment, a group of Yale affiliates explored Urban Mining Industries’s facility in Beacon Falls, Connecticut, where recycled glass is processed and turned into a powdery building material known as pozzolan. Concrete is an essential building material for modern-day structures. Standard concrete consists of aggregate like pebbles or gravel, sand, water and cement — the powdered binding agent, often confused with concrete, that gives concrete its integrity. “This isn’t chemistry that we’re inventing,” Chris Cotulio, a material sustainability specialist at O&G Industries — a construction company that has worked with Urban Mining Industries — said during the tour. “The ancient Romans and Greeks used volcanic ash as a pozzolan in their structures over 2,000 years ago, and a lot of them are still standing because it makes more durable concrete. So this is just a modern twist, using a post-consumer recyclable as a vehicle for that silicon dioxide.” Michelle So, Contributing Photographer Chris Cotulio leads the Yale group through the plant, pointing to machinery that cleans and filters glass particles. During the tour, Cotulio and Urban Mining Industries CEO Louis Grasso Jr., who invented Pozzotive, walked Yale members through the process of creating the finished product. The facility receives post-consumer glass from nearby material recovery facilities that collect refuse from ordinary recycling bins. Upon arrival, the material is put outside in foul-smelling mounds of muddy grit. Given the imperfect recycling systems, contamination from organic matter, plastics and metals isn’t uncommon. The raw recycled glass is cleaned mechanically in a tumbler that Grasso called a “dryer” — which he said differs from most plants’ chemically intensive cleaning processes. “Everything is really done correctly and transparently, so no chemicals or water goes through the dryer,” Grasso told the News. “The dryer tumbles the glass on itself, and it breaks off the paper labels, and it breaks off the dried organics as it goes through the tumbler or dryer.” Grasso founded the company over two decades ago and has since taken on large projects like the more-than-$3 billion JPMorgan Chase high-rise in Manhattan, which used 52,000 cubic yards of pozzolan-substituted concrete to build floors, according to the Pozzotive website . This project and more were covered in a tour led by Cotulio and Grasso. In the factory control room, Cotulio showed the visitors the systems and operations screen, where a schematic of the factory's operations is constantly monitored for changes in operations and efficiency. “What we’re seeing is an example of where the sustainable option is the better option,” Ayushi Khan ENV ’24, a postgraduate associate at the Yale Center for Industrial Ecology, said. “So this is a great, amazing example of why recycling can be so lucrative sometimes, and the better option sometimes. And this is easy to scale.” Natural forces like motion, gravity and density are utilized by the plant to process this raw material. Harmful batteries are then removed by giant magnets. Powerful fans blow out the lightweight plastics while leaving the denser glass pieces behind. Michelle So, Contributing Photographer Metal objects, such as batteries, can be dangerous if left in the filtered glass. After a high-powered magnet removed them, they were collected in large bins for transport to special waste facilities. Mechanical grinding, akin to a geode tumbler, breaks down the glass pieces into the plant’s final product: pozzolan. Pozzolan can be substituted for new cement at rates of 15 to 50 percent, according to Cotulio, though Grasso has experimented with blends containing up to 70 percent substitution. Due to the chemical composition of cement, a baseline proportion of cement is required to mix with water and make concrete, so full replacement with pozzolan is not possible. The remainder of the cement is usually supplemented with fly ash, which is a coal-burning byproduct, or slag, a remnant of iron smelting. However, a partial replacement of traditional cement already significantly reduces carbon emissions, Cotulio said. Recycling a ton of glass back into glass bottles saves roughly 166 kilograms of carbon dioxide, Cotulio said in his tour presentation. By comparison, recycling glass into concrete saves 980 kilograms — nearly a ton of carbon emissions. According to Cotulio, benefits of Pozzotive’s cement include increased resistance to chloride — useful for when sidewalks are salted in the winter — and that it shrinks less due to temperature fluctuations compared to traditional concrete, which can lead to structural cracks. “It was also pretty amazing that the product itself is cost parity with regular cement, which means it’s the same cost,” Alfonso Panis SOM ’26, who was also on the tour, said. “It’s really just the supply chain, at least to my mind, that's the bottleneck, like the raw material sourcing.” The Urban Mining Industries facility is located at 105 Breault Road in Beacon Falls. Correction, March 23: An earlier version of this articlemisstated the name of the concrete alternative made from recycled glass. It is Pozzotive, not Pozzitive.

title: Hackathon AI projects include Fizz newsletter, CourseTable assistant
subtitle: Teams of undergraduates submitted new projects to a Yale Computer Society hackathon last week focused on agentic artificial intelligence. The first-place project was an AI-powered tutor.
author: Anya Geist
text: Student projects tackled agentic artificial intelligence at a hackathon run by the Yale Computer Society last week. The projects ranged from an AI tutoring agent to a weekly Fizz newsletter to a CourseTable AI assistant. While language models like ChatGPT are best known for providing written responses to user prompts, AI companies have recently developed agentic models, which can take a user’s prompt and execute a complex multi-step product as a result, according to Ryan Fernandes ’28, a co-president of the computer society. “This is a new technology in the space,” Fernandes said. “So let's all experiment with, one, how to use AI for coding, and two, how to use it as a part of the actual project itself, and then go experiment, come back and collaboratively share.” The hackathon ran in a hybrid format for eight days last month, ending on Friday, which let the 28 participating teams create projects on their time, while attending hackathon workshops and office hours. Only eight teams finished, with the top three receiving a prize of $500 per team. Richard Kim ’27 and Ian Tinney ’27 won first place with Web, an AI-powered tutor. It allows users — independent learners, not necessarily students studying for an exam — to propose a topic, whether related to math, history or another subject. Then, Web creates a personalized curriculum. Unlike ChatGPT, which provides quick responses to questions, Kim and Tinney hope that Web helps users meet long-term learning goals. “You can continuously explore different facets and go down different routes within that general idea that you're studying,” Kim said. “You could start studying, I don't know, linear algebra, and end up in machine learning several nodes later.” Second place in the hackathon went to Sean Donahue ’29 and Evan Boyle ’29 for their project FizzBuzz, a weekly AI-generated newsletter that summarizes recent information on the anonymous campus social media app Fizz and sends it to students’ email inboxes. “It really gets an understanding of the parties that are going on, the rumors that are spreading or anything that you wouldn't hear from other news sources,” Boyle said. Donahue and Boyle noted that Fizz posts are not verified for accuracy and that AI can make mistakes. Throughout the newsletter, they said, they have disclaimers to take its content with “a grain of salt,” Boyle said. Balancing midterms throughout the hackathon, Donahue and Boyle completed most of their project within a few days of the due date. It was also challenging to find the Fizz data needed for the newsletter, because Fizz has “really good network security,” Boyle added. The project CourseTable Assistant received third place in the hackathon. Created by Jaden Cohen ’29, Raymon Zhang ’29 and Danny Zheng ’29, the application allows users to ask an AI assistant for information about classes or professors, recommend certain classes or make a sample worksheet directly on CourseTable. In addition to minimizing the stress of course selection, the team wanted to help students choose classes that would fulfill major requirements efficiently or connect them with professors whose research they might want to participate in. To do that, the application scrapes data available about majors, professors and classes from Yale websites. “But getting high-quality data that way is really difficult,” Cohen said. “All you really want is that one paragraph saying this professor just researches this, this and this, and he runs this lab, and here’s how you get into it. You know, that paragraph is lost among so many other random things.” The team adapted their scraper program to filter data by keywords and then scored webpages by relevance to find important information. They hope to see CourseTable Assistant available across campus, but they still have bugs to work out. For Fernandes, the hackathon demonstrated the Yale Computer Society’s commitment to making coding accessible and collaborative. It was sponsored by the start-up MiniMax. Users could see and borrow its code and programs and use them off the MiniMax server, making the creation of agentic models more accessible and collaborative. “We're really trying to promote open source development and letting people have access directly to the code base and be able to contribute to it,” Fernandes said. Fernandes said he hopes that the computer society will run another hackathon in the fall. Many teams from the hackathon plan to participate in Y-Hack, an annual hackathon at Yale in April that is sponsored by the organization Major League Hacking. MiniMax, a Chinese startup, was founded in 2021.

title: For three Yale researchers with federal grants intact, snags remain
subtitle: Research continues to rely heavily on federal support after a year of volatility for grants. Three Yale professors described their experiences with federal funding and some complications affecting their work.
author: Anna Koontz
text: Nikhil Malvankar has dedicated over 20 years to studying how a common soil bacteria survives without oxygen. His research depends on the support of American taxpayers. Despite the volatility of federal research funding during President Donald Trump’s second term, it continues to support researchers whose grants have remained fully or partially intact. Three such professors at Yale — who are currently receiving federal funds for studies ranging from molecular biophysics to reproductive sciences to cell biology — told the News that the funding has enabled their work, even as grant policy changes cause complications. “I’m just really fascinated by this country and how much faith they have shown in basic science,” said Malvankar, an associate professor of molecular biophysics and biochemistry who is from India. “I just hope that going forward, we can continue keeping the faith.” The first year of Trump’s second term impacted about 120 grants at Yale , including 50 terminated grants that were later reinstated, according to Michael Crair, Yale’s vice provost of research. Labs currently receiving federal funding still experience the effects of recent policy changes — such as a longer application process for future grants and difficulties with international collaborations. Bacterial breathing Malvankar and his longtime colleague, researcher Sibel Ebru Yalcin, use federal funding from the National Science Foundation and Department of Energy to research how Geobacter bacteria survive without oxygen. For bacteria living deep within the ground and ocean, protein nanowires act as “snorkels” that exhale electrons, Malvankar said in a Zoom interview. Understanding how the bacteria breathe has implications for environmental and human health . “We were able to find something which is not just new from basic science, it also has a lot of applications to cure diseases and maintain our environment,” Malvankar said. However, Malvankar said that Trump administration policy changes have “greatly affected” his lab’s work. Some of his funding programs were cancelled or substantially reduced, he said, and program managers that he worked with for the past decade have now left federal agencies. Malvankar said that research projects, even those without “immediate use in everyday life,” are opportunities to train younger scientists and provide a foundation for future discoveries. He cited the decades of mRNA research that enabled the rapid development of COVID-19 vaccines. In addition to federal funding, Malvankar noted that philanthropic foundations and private funding have been important in developing his research. “They helped us to get necessary preliminary data, which allowed us to build a much stronger proposal to be competitive for federal research,” he said. Reproduction lab relying on slower processes Binyam Mogessie — an assistant professor of molecular, cellular and developmental biology and of obstetrics, gynecology and reproductive sciences — has dedicated his career to studying reproductive health. “These are really timely problems,” Mogessie said in a phone interview. “They just don’t wait even if you choose not to do the research.” The Mogessie Lab receives federal funding from the National Institute of Health, or NIH, to research female meiosis and reproductive longevity. The lab studies the processes and failures of egg formation with the goal of improving assisted human reproduction technologies. Beyond deepening our understanding of biology, Mogessie’s research has a “large consequential impact at the socioeconomic level,” he said. Extending female reproductive life would provide women with more flexibility when deciding when to pursue a career or start a family. Federal funding currently enables the lab to maintain an average workforce of about six to ten people. Since grants only last for a few years, Mogessie is continually applying for new funding. He estimated that he wrote 12 or 13 applications in 2025 alone, half of which were for federal funding. Complications with policy changes and government shutdowns slow the application and approval process, limiting researchers’ ability to secure funds, Mogessie said. But federal grants remain unparalleled, he said. “No one funds at the level of the NIH,” said Mogessie. “The loss of federal funding would be completely catastrophic to the American research landscape.” Split from Irish collaborators One of the least understood aspects of physiological regulation is how forces from gravity, muscle contraction and blood flow affect our tissues, Martin Schwartz said. The professor of medicine, biomedical engineering and cell biology researches the processes by which those forces affect the functions and behaviors of cells and tissues. Schwartz’s research, largely funded by the NIH, contributes to the development of new treatments for disease. “You have to understand the basic mechanisms in order to develop therapeutics. And working across that whole spectrum, from very fundamental to applied, is what I love about it,” Schwartz said over Zoom. Although Schwartz’s lab is well-funded, he said policy changes under the second Trump presidency have limited his ability to work with international researchers. Schwartz had been working with a lab in Ireland until it got “kicked off” the grant and lacked funding to continue, he said. “The Trump administration simply decided that no NIH money was going to go outside the country.” An increasing number of foreign researchers have also lost interest in coming to the United States, Schwartz said. Those that do wish to work in America can experience difficulties with visas. One of Schwartz’s postdoctoral researchers recently visited his sick father in China and was not allowed to return despite having a J1 visa, he said. Schwartz also expressed concerns about “political meddling that will distort who gets funded.” He referenced the recent discontinuation of vaccine research and closure of departments based on ideology. At the same time, Schwartz is optimistic that “the political tide is turning.” “After Trump’s attempt to drastically slash NIH and NSF budgets, Congress reinstated their budgets,” Schwartz said. “So resistance is building.” The Malvankar Lab has offices on Science Hill and on Yale’s West Campus in West Haven. Correction, March 5: Due to an editing error, an earlier version of this article misrepresented Binyam Mogessie ’ s position as an assistant professor. He is primarily affiliated with the Department of Molecular, Cellular and Developmental Biology, not with the School of Medicine, where he holds a secondary appointment.

title: Dramat makes ‘fetch’ happen in riotous rendition of ‘Mean Girls’
subtitle: The Yale Dramatic Association’s Commencement musical, which opened Friday night at the University Theatre, delivers a cautionary tale of lust, pride and personal discovery that is fitting for graduating seniors’ entrance into a period of change.
author: Cameron Nye
text: Ah, Commencement! The time when graduating Yalies gaze upon the sea of white folding chairs on Old Campus and look back on four prosperous academic years. Friends and family fly in from across the globe to celebrate the students who are ready to break out of their blue and white chrysalides and spread their beautiful, corporate wings. It’s a time of rejoicing and reflection for most, but for the Yale University Dramatic Association, Commencement means one thing and one thing only — a fast, frantic and full-throttle musical production. In the downtime between finals and Commencement weekend, an ambitious group of thespians work for ten days to produce a show in celebration of the year’s theater season and the seniors that will go on to bigger, brighter stages. This year, director Will Sussbauer ’27, producer Jack Dozier ’27, music producer Lyndon Chen ’28 and stage manager Rena Kawasaki ’27 teamed up to present a musical based on one of this generation’s cultural pillars — “Mean Girls.” If you haven’t seen Tina Fey’s original 2004 satiric film, close out of this tab and go watch it. It’s 97 minutes of unadulterated Y2K drama and witty dialogue that remains quotable and iconic even 22 years later. After receiving critical acclaim, “Mean Girls” was turned into a musical, hitting Broadway in 2017 and bringing Fey’s beloved characters into the age of social media. The musical includes all the quips and digs that made the original iconic, and stays true to the film’s plot: 16-year-old Cady Heron moves from Kenya to the United States and, after years of being homeschooled, realizes the lions of her youth did not prepare her for the beastly monsters of high school. She’s initially befriended by two social outcasts but is quickly noticed by the Plastics, a gaggle of popular girls who are North Shore High’s apex predators. Cady is thrown into the maws of popularity, and quickly turns from naive newcomer to manipulative mean girl. The musical gained mostly positive reviews and then gained a frighteningly dull film adaptation in 2024. The production, which I saw Friday night, opened to a friendly audience of supportive suitemates, weepy parents and reminiscent peers. The next day, Dozier informed me via text message that the production had become the “highest grossing show in recent history” staged by the Dramat. A very fetch development. Kudos to them! Staying true to the 2017 production, the University Theatre has been transformed into a minimalistic high school — desks come and go as classes change and cafeteria tables are wheeled off once the lunch bell has rung. The back of the stage remains mostly illuminated a fittingly vibrant shade of pink, backlighting slits of drywall decoupaged in a postered collage of song lyrics, inspirational quotes and Uno reverse cards. The rendition incorporated some changes to the show’s book. Lyrics that contained homophobic slurs and problematic language were tweaked, creating a more inclusive environment without taking away from the show’s intended cruelty. Sussbauer did incorporate some more campy choices that leaned into the melodramatic undertones of the comedic musical. Damian Leigh, the show’s resident flamboyant homosexual, cheekily directs his “sexually active band geeks” line to the pit orchestra, most of whose members give toothy grins and wave. Meanwhile, during Regina George’s fiery revenge tune “World Burn,” her notorious hot pink burn book — which contains The Plastics’ nasty notes about their peers — is lowered down like a clunky weapon of mass destruction. Papers then begin cascading down from the rafters onto the stage, picked up by an anxious and enraged ensemble who has learned that their business has been aired to the entire school. It’s choices like this that make “Mean Girls” a gleeful riot, leaning into the absurdity of stringent social hierarchies in which gossip literally rains from the sky while remaining grounded in the lockered halls of a midwestern high school. That balance is further maintained by the performances of the shows’ cast. The Plastics are brought to life by Hadley Murphey ’29 as the tyrannical Regina George, Sasha Fedderly ’27 as the anxious, antsy Gretchen Weiners and Nneka Moweta ’27 as the delightfully dimwitted Karen Smith. It’s clear Murphey studied at the “Reneé Rapp School of Regina George Portrayals,” because her mix of conceited “cursive” singing — heard in “Geeoorggggeeeeehhhh” — and piercing vibrato is a ferocious ode to Rapp’s performance both in the 2024 movie and on Broadway. Meanwhile, Moweta’s impeccable comedic timing elevates Karen from just another “dumb blonde” to a girl who understands the unspoken workings of high school social hierarchies. The social rejects-turned-narrators — comprised of the jaded Janis Sarkisian, played by Olivia Ridley ’26, and the showy “too gay to function” Damian brought by Sebastian Gervase ’27 — drive the show with grungey “F YOU” anthems and electric numbers reminiscent of classic Broadway showtunes. Gervase’s numbers, in particular, feature showstopping choreography — fluttering jazz hands and showy kicks that infuse an intoxicating intricacy to the production, all courtesy of choreographer Sakura Grashow ’28. Caught in the middle of these two worlds is Cady Heron, brought to life by Amelia Gallegos ’29. She captures Cady excellently, portraying the bright eyed, naive math nerd with remarkable earnestness before embodying her character’s curdle into a conniving, calculated bully. Her bright soprano lends itself well to festive, ingenuous numbers like “It Roars” and “Stupid With Love,” in which her developing crush on hunk Aaron Samuels, played by Abram Knott ’27, fills her with “calcu-lust.” With only ten days to become a fully fledged musical, “Mean Girls” accomplished a great deal. Many of the characters were fully realized with tics and quirks that captured the itchy awkwardness of teenage angst. The production, however, was hampered by its technical inconsistency. Even from a seat relatively close to the stage, lines and runs were lost as microphones crunched and sputtered. The lighting wavered, too, with spotlights often scrambling to catch up to actors already speaking in shadow. With more time, undoubtedly, these wrinkles could be ironed out, but for the rehearsal period it was afforded, “Mean Girls” is practically a triumph. What this cast and crew was able to pull off in a short amount of time celebrates the commitment thespians give to their craft — which in turn honors the seniors who have given their all to the University Theatre they’ve called home for the past four years. “Mean Girls” makes sense as a Commencement musical because its story has already cemented itself in the cultural imagination as funny and sharply quotable. But beneath the pink plastic surface is a tale about insecurity and the exhausting performance of trying to be liked. Those questions do not disappear after high school — or even college. We still navigate cliques, self-comparison, friendship fallout and the pressure to package ourselves into something more impressive than we feel. At a moment meant to celebrate moving forward, “Mean Girls” offers a reminder that growing up is about learning how to be kinder and more honest — a fitting sendoff for Yale’s seniors before they contemplate acquiescing to the Regina Georges of the world. “Mean Girls” will run until May 17.

title: Seniors reflect on their experience within Yale’s music scene
subtitle: Yale has an incredibly vast music scene, from student bands to a cappella to orchestra. Here’s how four graduating seniors took advantage of it.
author: Gemard Guery
text: Every fall, many first years at Yale weigh how they’ll take part in one of the University’s largest selling points, the music scene, through everything from a capella rush to Yale Symphony Orchestra auditions. Now on their way out, four seniors spoke to the News about their participation in Yale’s music scene, its impact on their Yale experience and what’s next for them. The singer-songwriter Zaida Rio Polanco ’26 has been passionate about music for as long as she can remember. An environmental studies major from White Plains, New York, Polanco grew up with a dad and brother who were both singer-songwriters, contributing to Polanco’s decision to do the same.
