Polanco grew up watching her dad play for weddings and her brother, who is 11 years older than her, play drums for her dad’s band. “There was always music playing in the house, and they let me find my own genre that I liked,” Polanco said in an interview with the News. Polanco was writing songs when she was as young as three years old. Her dad ran the songwriters club at her high school, and Polanco would perform at open mics as early as third grade alongside high school aged students. By the time she entered Yale, Polanco had already released an album with 10 songs. Polanco was drawn to Yale’s theater and acting scene when she first arrived at Yale but knew she wanted to continue singing and songwriting. “I just knew I wanted to keep going, no matter where I ended up,” said Polanco. “I just got lucky I was able to go to a school that had a student record label that had a bunch of people willing to help bring this music to life.” Polanco was a member of the Shades of Yale for her first three years at Yale and a frequent participant in theater on campus . She was also an active part of 17o1 Records, the student record label that helped Polanco release many of her own projects. For a while, Polanco was reluctant to pursue a career as an artist, but her time at Yale showed her that it is possible. “I’ve met people that turned this pipe dream of going into this career as an artist into something that could actually happen,” Polanco said. “I love the fact that I can hear my best friends performing at Chi Psi from my bedroom window. There are not many places you can do that, be able to have that experience where you’re literally living in a small little bubble of performing artists. I’ll miss that a lot.” While she enjoyed her experience as an environmental studies major, it is not what she hopes to pursue professionally. Following commencement, Zaida plans to pursue music in Los Angeles while working in marketing with a company that has a creative entry point. She also hopes to continue auditioning for theater and film. The bassist For Thara Joseph ’26, music has always been an important part of her identity. A bassist from Jacksonville, Florida, Joseph began playing the piano regularly in elementary school. “Every day, I knew I had at last ninety minutes of playing per day,” said Joseph in an interview. “That really started in sixth grade, and it stayed consistently until I graduated, so it was very intensive in that way.” Courtesy of Thara Joseph Joseph attended a magnet arts program for both middle school and high school, where her passion for music intensified. She was a part of her city symphony orchestra and youth symphony orchestra, where she played the French horn, and in her senior year of high school, she was accepted into a jazz combo through the Jazz Education Network, which connects young musicians with professionals in the field. But as Joseph was preparing to come to Yale, she anticipated putting music on the back burner, focusing on academics instead and seeing where Yale would take her. “I didn’t know if I was going to be able to play and practice at the same frequencies that I had been for many, many years,” Joseph said. But when Joseph found herself unable to practice playing music every day during the First-Year Scholars program, she said she realized how little she enjoyed that experience. In fact, during the program, Joseph went to a local pawn shop and bought a bass bundled with an amp so she could practice. “Looking back, even that moment should have clued me into how important music was as a core part of my daily routine and how hard it would have been to just drop it,” Joseph said. Joseph has been a bassist for the Yale Jazz Ensemble during the entirety of her undergraduate experience, playing in musical productions like “The Wiz” and “For Colored Girls.” She has regularly gigged professionally with graduate students at the Yale School of Music, and has performed for events held through Yale’s Schwarzman Center and opened for celebrities such as Cécile McLorin Salvant and John Legend. Originally, Joseph came to Yale planning to double-major in both global affairs and music. Instead, she tested out classes across many different fields and departments until she landed on Ethics, Politics and Economics. She said her interdisciplinary major, alongside her music experience, opened her eyes to the nature of Yale’s liberal arts education. “I realized I don’t have to choose one path here. I feel like I’m the type of person where I want to have my hands in many different pockets,” Joseph said. “Because of all that time I was able to still invest in my craft, it really had me making that shift into considering pursuing music in a more serious and intentional way.” Joseph says she will miss Yale’s vibrant community the most from her music experience as she leaves Yale. “You get to meet so many different people, and a lot of people, I don’t think I would have ever crossed paths otherwise,” said Joseph. “There’s such an emphasis on collaboration here when it comes to the arts, where it’s music that feels good, it sounds good, and it’s enjoyable. I’ll miss that a lot.” Joseph hopes to someday work in the more executive side of the music industry. “There’s a need for more artists as industry leaders, so that we can increase artist advocacy and bridge what can sometimes be misunderstandings between creatives and executives,” Joseph said. Following graduation, Joseph will be staying in New Haven as a Chauncey Fellow, where she will be developing a service-based arts project. She plans to continue gigging throughout her time as a fellow. The Whiffenpoof For John Colbert ’26, the Yale journey was unlike what he originally anticipated. A humanities major, he originally matriculated in fall 2020, as a member of the class of 2024, but took a gap year due to the pandemic to get more out of his Yale experience. Colbert played the drums often in his hometown of Montclair, New Jersey, gigging with jazz bands through his time in middle school and high school. He also participated in an after school and summer program where local musicians help youth create their own ensembles. Colbert’s older sister attended Yale and recommended that he try out for Yale’s Tangled Up in Blue — or TUIB — a folk music group. While Colbert’s sister was not a part of the group, he said she always enjoyed watching them perform. “It was a big event for her and her friends to go to every year. She was like, ‘You should do this.’ Colbert said in an interview with the News. “So then I auditioned, and that’s been a big part of my time at Yale. That’s really what made me take singing more seriously as something that I did here.” Colbert eventually served as the musical director of TUIB during his junior year, in addition to participating in bands and theater on campus. He also became a member of the Whiffenpoof class of 2025, where Yale seniors take gap years to perform worldwide, something he said he had no intention of being a part of originally. It wasn’t until a friend from TUIB encouraged Colbert to audition after sharing her own experience that Colbert considered becoming a Whiffenpoof. “The idea of being a professional amateur musician or an amateur professional was kind of appealing to me,” Colbert said. “I spent so much time doing music stuff here that I was like, I really wouldn’t mind doing essentially just that for a whole year.” Colbert enjoyed his time as a Whiffenpoof, which allowed him not only to have a fun experience but also gave him the opportunity to reflect on what he wanted his life after Yale to look like. For Colbert, music was also a means of finding his community at Yale. “Being involved in music here was a wonderful way to be engaged with campus life,” he said. “I think playing music together is a really interesting and unique way to form bonds with people and it means a lot to me.” As Colbert prepares to leave Yale, he said he already feels nostalgic about his time in the music scene and how it became a serious endeavor. “In some ways, the things that I’ll miss about playing music here are the things that I’ll miss about not being as young as I am now,” he said. “I’ll miss the strange, infinite possibility of college that is so, so alluring when you’re here.” Following commencement, Colbert hopes to move to New York and continue playing with his band, Dog Boy, which he began during his time at Yale. There, he hopes to engage in the city’s art scene while he continues searching for a job separate from his creative endeavors that he finds fulfilling on a personal level. The concert pianist Paul Ji ’26 has been a well known and distinguished musician for years. A philosophy and music double major from France, Ji has been playing the piano since he was 4 years old. Ji participated in many international competitions when he was younger, winning almost all of them, which further fueled his passion for music, specifically the piano. “I did the Steinway competition for seven years, consecutively, and fortunately, I won all of them and that motivated me to continue,” he said in an interview. “But at one point, I decided that music was much more than competitions, and there was so much joy to be found in connecting with people through music.” Courtesy of Paul Ji When Ji was 15, he recorded his first album after winning a national competition in France. Ji’s second album, the one he recorded while at Yale, will be coming out in early 2027. Much of Ji’s Yale experience was finding the perfect balance between his professional piano career and his academic interests. “I very much appreciate the liberal arts, and I really wanted to delve deep into philosophy,” he said. “I also felt that it would help my music, that it would give me more insights and more intellectual experience.” The majority of his sophomore year was devoted to learning the work of Polish composer Frédéric Chopin. Ji’s next album, recorded in Sprague Hall, consists of the full 24 preludes by Chopin, accompanied by other pieces. This year, Ji had the opportunity to perform with the Yale Symphony Orchestra as the winner of the William Waite competition , where he played Chopin’s second concerto, a romantic piece he performed on Valentine’s Day. Ji’s talents have brought him all over the world throughout his time at Yale. During his sophomore year, he performed his debut recital in Paris at the Théâtre des Champs-Élysées. Since then, he’s played in Vienna in spaces where famous musicians such as Beethoven have performed. While Ji wasn’t a consistent part of any music group, his experience continuously demonstrated to him how strong the musical community at Yale is. He often participated in graduate piano classes in Sprague Hall and would play alongside experts in the field. “Meeting musicians who are also very intellectually driven and invested in many, many different fields has been a gift,” Ji said. Ji hopes to continue to build his career as a concert pianist while simultaneously finding solutions and strategies to make classical music more accessible. “It’s really such a great art that can help people transcend, and I think more people deserve to know it,” he said. Following graduation, Ji will be pursuing his master’s of music in piano at the Juilliard School.

title: Under Bundy, drama school went tuition-free and saw a cultural shift
subtitle: James Bundy DRA ’95, the longest-serving dean of the David Geffen School of Drama, oversaw the historic change to the school’s financial aid policy that eliminated tuition costs. Bundy’s colleagues described his leadership as “remarkable.”
author: Kiva Bank
text: The office of James Bundy DRA ’95, who is retiring from his posts at the David Geffen School of Drama and the Yale Repertory Theatre, is set to be cleared out by the end of June. But for now, the room’s blue walls are still covered with framed posters from student thesis productions Bundy has seen throughout his 24-year-long tenure — the longest of any Drama School dean to date. “The theater is filled with mementos,” Bundy, who has served in his role as dean and as the Yale Rep’s artistic director since 2002, said in an interview with the News. His office is laden with keepsakes, each object hinting at a piece of his life and work at Yale. Beneath his coffee table sits a “Shakespeare Lexicon and Quotation Dictionary,” a nod to the “Acting Shakespeare” undergraduate course he will continue to teach after stepping down this summer. Nearby is a gold-painted leg signed by an Estonian clown who performed at the Yale Rep this past season, and on Bundy’s desk sits a Funko Pop gifted to him by Nancy Yao, the assistant dean of student life at the drama school. The figurine is styled in his likeness — bald, wearing clear-framed glasses — and the diploma it holds echoes Bundy himself, who earned his MFA in directing from the School of Drama in 1995. “The school completely changed the direction of my career, and opened up a world of opportunities,” he said. Prior to his appointment to the Drama School, Bundy served as the artistic director of Great Lakes Theater Festival, as well as the associate producing director of the Acting Company in New York City and as managing director of Cornerstone Theater Company. Posters for two different student productions of “Hedda Gabler” — the last play that Bundy himself directed at the Yale Rep while serving as its artistic director — hang in his office. During his tenure at the Rep, he also directed productions of Shakespeare’s “Hamlet” and “All’s Well That Ends Well.” Liz Diamond, the chair of the directing program at the David Geffen School of Drama, told the News she is “a little bit in denial” about the change in leadership at the school. She said was glad to see Bundy “pour his heart and soul” into the Yale Rep’s production of “Hedda Gabler.” “I was really moved by the work,” she said in a phone interview with the News. “I’m sure that was partly because I knew it was his last production as dean and artistic director.” Diamond said she first met Bundy in fall 1992, when he was one of four students in her directing seminar. She described him as “surpassingly conscientious,” an “indefatigable researcher” and a “wonderful thinker.” Catherine Sheehy DRA ’92 ’99, who is currently the chair of dramaturgy and dramatic criticism at the Drama School and a dramaturg at the Yale Rep, was a first-year faculty member when Bundy was in his final year as a student. She said she can still recall his 1995 thesis production of “Three Sisters.” “He created these images that were really strong,” Sheehy said. “I can still see Protopopov in his white suit, sitting and smoking.” She said that Bundy’s training as an actor enabled him to understand players better as a director, and that in her mind, “he was always sort of an old soul.” Sheehy recalled that Bundy balanced his studies at the drama school with his responsibilities as a husband and a young father. “I remember him wheeling around his kids at the time,” she said. Bundy said that his time as a student at the Drama School aided him when he took his place at its helm, helping inform his understanding of the school’s potential. When he stepped into the deanship, Bundy said that both the search committee that had appointed him and then-University president Richard Levin ’72 ’74 were clear in communicating their priorities for the school. He said there was a general agreement to rank financial aid as the top priority, followed by faculty compensation and new facilities. “24 years later, we still haven’t broken ground on that building, but we're close,” Bundy joked, referring to the Dramatic Arts building set to be completed in 2029. The seven-floor structure will house the Yale Repertory Theater, the David Geffen School of Drama and the undergraduate Theater, Dance and Performance Studies department in one space. “James willed this building into being,” Sheehy said. “James has had to have extraordinary persistence. The administration has changed. The president has changed, the provost has changed more than once.” At the same time, Bundy has been driven by his desire to decrease student indebtedness, which he said was a major problem when he first began his tenure. According to Bundy, debt for School of Drama students in 1982 averaged $10,000, or roughly $34,000 in today’s dollars, and the average debt for the class of 2005 was around $45,000, or $75,000 today. The average student today graduates from the school with about $16,000 in debt — around a 50 percent net reduction from 1982 and an 80 percent net reduction from 2005. Sheehy said that when she graduated from the drama school in 1992, her student loan payment was $575 per month, which she characterized as “enormous.” She said that the financial aid program did not offer much grant aid at the time, but that when Bundy arrived as dean, he immediately started working to increase financial aid. Diamond said that the first full faculty meeting under Bundy’s leadership marked a change from the previous dean’s style. “We really didn’t have very lively faculty meetings under the previous dean,” Diamond said, but under Bundy, “we began with a physical warm-up led by the acting teacher who taught movement.” “For the first time in history, faculty and staff of the drama school were jumping up and down together and sweating,” Diamond said. She said that when Bundy led a discussion to determine the school’s priorities during the first five years of his tenure, the desire to increase financial aid for students was unanimous amongst faculty. “He pursued that goal assiduously, and it took the better part of nearly 20 years,” Diamond said. She described Bundy’s skills as those of “remarkable systems analyst,” which enabled him to establish a plan to go tuition-free. In 2021, nearly twenty years into Bundy’s deanship, entertainment mogul David Geffen donated $150 million to the Drama School. The gift allowed the school to become tuition-free for all full-time students in perpetuity, and the school was renamed in Geffen’s honor. Before the donation, Bundy oversaw a need-based student aid program, according to Sheehy, and also played a key role in establishing 70 of the school’s 91 named scholarships. In 2008, Bundy helped facilitate funding for living stipends and summer support for playwriting students through a bequest from playwright Eugene O’Neill and Carlotta Monterey, which he said made Yale’s playwriting program competitive with other fully-funded MFA programs. As a result, all students in the playwriting program — of which there are three per year — receive stipends to cover their living expenses during both the academic year and summer months, in addition to full-tuition scholarships. Chantal Rodriguez, associate dean of the Drama School, said in a phone interview with the News that Bundy’s dedication “to decreasing the overall indebtedness of alumni” has formed part of his legacy. During the first year of the pandemic, the Drama School saved roughly $3 million in production costs because it did not put on any plays. The school then used the excess funds toward tuition and living stipends to offer a fourth year for students in the classes of 2021, 2022 and 2023 free of charge, Bundy said. Diamond also praised Bundy for his dedication to establishing a shared leadership model among the associate and assistant deans, which promotes collective decision-making. “You cannot make theater if you are a person who insists on flying solo,” Diamond said. “It’s a form of practicing for civilization, and I think that the fact that James, in his own practice as a leader of our school and theater, strove to lead collaboratively, was remarkable.” Bundy also brought about a cultural shift at the school, according to Sheehy. For years, the acting program followed a longstanding tradition of admitting 10 men and six women each year, based on the belief that classical repertory, particularly Shakespeare, required more male actors, Sheehy said. But students argued that the policy was both discriminatory and no longer beneficial to their training, and Bundy removed the quota. Evan Yionoulis ’82 DRA ’85 was recently announced as Bundy’s successor. The outgoing dean characterized Yionoulis as his former mentor, as he worked as her teaching assistant during the summers of 1993 and 1995 when she taught acting for a Yale program. Yionoulis, who previously served as the chair of acting at the drama school, also served on the search committee when Bundy was hired, he said. “She asked me, to the effect of, ‘How would you want people to describe your leadership style?’” he said, recalling the time when Yionoulis was involved in his selection. “I said that I would want to lead with my heart.” Diamond said Bundy is known to read the application of every single incoming student as well as memorize their names and proper pronunciation before orientation. As of last fall, the school enrolled 240 students, according to the Office of Institutional Research. “That is just an example of his deep commitment to this community, and it makes everybody step up,” Diamond said. Bundy also hosts an annual barbecue in the backyard of his house in Westville, a tradition he said started when he moved into the home with his family around the time his deanship began. He said he recalled thinking, “Oh, the whole school could fit in this backyard.” “It’s a nice way for people to be together at the outset,” he said. “We’ve had 10,000 guests at our house over the course of the decades, and that’s felt like a really meaningful experience.” In April, Diamond said, the school was able to return the favor and host a farewell party in celebration of Bundy with all students, faculty and staff. “There were a gazillion balloons that were shaped like hearts, which has extra meaning for a dean who had at least two open heart surgeries during his tenure,” she said. Sheehy said that Bundy’s “open-heartedness” and “open door” policy as a dean will be hardest to replace. “I always think that it’s a sign of good character if you’re willing to be a fool for love,” Sheehy said. “James is willing to be a fool for his love of this institution, and he’s willing to be humble in that way and enjoy a joke and see the funny side.” Bundy graduated from Harvard College in 1981.

title: Student performers to seek light and truth in the ordinary on Class Day
subtitle: This year’s class day performance will feature five musicians, a comedy trio and an orator. Each senior taking the stage aims to reveal a different aspect of the Yale experience: the light carried forward, the joy in the mundane and the order in chaos.
author: Hannah Liu
text: This year’s student orator and eight Class Day performers — composed of five musicians and a comedy trio — will combine an array of cultures, mediums and thoughts to reflect on the class of 2026’s time at Yale. Class Day is Yale’s annual student-led ceremony, held on Old Campus on the Sunday before Commencement. This year, the event will take place on May 17, and is set to feature keynote speaker Min Jin Lee ’90 alongside student performances and a prize ceremony. Seniors in attendance traditionally don headgear of their choice along with their graduation gowns. “Our whole focus has been, ‘How do we celebrate this warmth and excitement about the world that our class has been showing?’” Maya Moseley ’26, one of this year’s four Class Day co-chairs, said in a phone interview with the News. “There’s an aspect of joy in all the pieces that they’ll be able to see and an enthusiasm for what’s next. We’re looking up and looking ahead, and I think that’s a really cool aspect of it.” Auditions for this year’s student performances consisted of an online application and a live performance, a process run by this year’s four Class Day co-chairs. According to Moseley, this year’s Class Day performance order will allow for a larger, more cohesive block of student section performances rather than the previously more interspersed performances throughout the ceremony. Here is a quick look into the three Class Day performances. The Ivy Ode: “The Light We Take Forward” Inspired by Yale’s “Lux et Veritas” motto, this year’s Class Day musicians have composed an intercultural musical experience exploring what it means to carry forward light and truth. “Even though we’re leaving all of the people and places at Yale behind, the light of friendship and love and familiarity and the way that Yale has taught us to think and grow as intellectuals are the things we will carry with us,” Grace Halak ’26, who will be playing guitar, said in a phone interview with the News. The seniors’ performance weaves together spoken word with music from various styles into one cohesive piece, inspired by the western pop tradition, Indian Classical music and Arabic melodies. “Each segment of our story is a different musical form,” Ayush Iyer ’26, who will play piano and sing for the set, said in a phone interview. “I grew up with one culture in the house and one culture outside the house. When I came to Yale, it was the first time I actually got to combine those things. It’s beautiful to me because it’s sort of a culmination of a lot of things I’ve done throughout my life in one.” Iyer and Anjal Jain ’26 are members of the Indian Classical group Yale Dhvani and were inspired by the recent Muziko concert they played in collaboration with the Yale Undergraduate Jazz Collective and Yale Undergraduate Chamber Orchestra. The two, along with Yara Chami ’25+1, are also members of the Yale Glee Club and have performed in previous Class Days ceremonies in the choir. Iyer, Jain and Chami will perform as vocalists in their set. “I’m really excited because throughout my time at Yale, whenever people ask me what I’m doing, I’ll be at rehearsal through the different musical groups I’ve been able to engage in,” Jain said in a phone interview. “So I think it means a lot for my friends and my classmates to see what types of things we’ve been doing at rehearsal and to see my musical side.” For Chami, who was originally part of the class of 2025 but took a gap year to perform with the Yale Whiffenpoofs, being a Class Day performer for this year’s class is particularly meaningful. “I truly do feel like a member of both the class of ’25 and the class of ’26,” Chami said in a phone interview. “It’s very much an honor to graduate with this group of people who I’ve come to get to know very well,” she said, referring to the class of 2026. Halak said that one of her original songs will be featured in the Class Day performance. “I feel like it’ll be a really special and symbolic way to end my time here, especially since music has been such a big part of my time here, and it’s probably the biggest thing I do outside of the classroom.” Naomi-Jeanne Main ’26 will also be performing on violin. Comedic Reflection: “Yale is Magical” Following the Ivy Ode, three students will take the stage to perform a comedic piece called “Yale is Magical,” which centers around a hectic group project at Yale that goes awry. “We’ll be tripping over each other and making a bunch of jokes about Yale along the way, which is sort of what it feels like to put together a sketch comedy show sometimes,” Josh Bock ’25+1 said in a phone interview. “Yale is a space where you can run into challenges, disagree and work with people that you don’t expect to, and make it through despite how tough it is.” Bock, Prentiss Patrick-Carter ’26 and Amara Neal ’26 make up the comedy trio that will perform on Sunday. The three are all members of Yale’s Red Hot Poker sketch comedy group, and Bock said their time collaborating in the group allowed them to learn how to write and make jokes together. According to Patrick-Carter, the group has been excited about the prospect of performing in a setting like Class Day since their first year at Yale. They came together this February to write a script for their audition to perform at Class Day and, after finding out they were selected, have been honing their exhibition ever since. “Comedy is a valid means of connection with people,” Neal said in a phone interview with the News. “You can laugh and also be sad at the same time.” The group hopes to connect with people through comedy and laughter, both at Class Day and post-graduation. Class Oration: “Tuesday” To close out the student portion of Sunday’s ceremony, Shubh Gupta ’26’s oration will highlight the everyday moments that make Yale special. Focused on the idea of “Tuesdays,” which is intended to symbolize the smaller, mundane parts of the Yale experience, Gupta hopes his audience will realize that it is not always the grand milestones that shape a student’s time at Yale. “We all look forward to these big moments, these highlight reel grand moments like Harvard-Yale and Spring Fling and the senior thesis, and the senior society,” Gupta said in a phone interview with the News. “But the moments that change us don’t really feel important while they’re happening, they feel super ordinary.” When Gupta found out that another student from his high school in Kentucky had given the graduation speech a few years ago, he was inspired to apply. His experience starting the speech and debate club at his high school helped him develop his love for speaking, he added. “It’s such an honor and so humbling to know that I’m going to have this opportunity,” Gupta said. “There is definitely some weight I felt in, ‘How do I do this class justice?’ Class day and graduation feels so much bigger than one person. The real challenge is how to say something that feels true to everyone, and that everyone can relate to and carry something out of for the rest of their lives.” The Yale Glee Club was founded in 1861.

title: ‘Furlough’s Paradise’ dives into dissonance to paint family narrative
subtitle: In what was originally their thesis production for the David Geffen School of Drama, a.k. payne’s two-person play closes the Yale Repertory Theatre’s season on a powerful note, with rich reflections on familial identity and elite education.
author: Zoe Frost
text: “Furlough’s Paradise” begins in a nightmare. The lights dim, and as spots of blue swirl across the modest on-stage living room to a washy, enveloping soundscape, two figures emerge, one after the other. The first wears a bodysuit covered in bulbous growths, like those on a toad or humpback whale, while the other dons tight silver clothing and a lampshade as a hat. The two figures writhe, grunt and scream as they try to tear these strange costumes off. Apparently at war with their own skin, they take what feels like eternity to wriggle out of their entrapment. Once they do, the scene that follows is another nightmare of its own, this time all too real: bizarre costumes fall away to reveal funeral black attire, blue projections fade and leave a modest and nondescript living room and the pain previously expressed by the actors’ bodies hides in pregnant silences between lines of terse dialogue. This fluctuation — between expression and suppression, nightmare and reality — is a persistent theme in “Furlough’s Paradise,” written by a.k. payne DRA ’23 and directed by abigail jean-baptiste. Dim lights, dreamy music and anguished physical expression continue to interrupt the play’s events, leaving audience members to reconcile with the gap between life and dream themselves. A pair of cousins — Mina, played by Tiffany McLarty, and Sade, played by Lauren F. Walker DRA ’25 — straddle mundanity and profundity as they attempt to process grief, meet each other as adults and resolve the feeling of dissonance that neither of them can seem to shake. Granted a three-day furlough from a high-security prison 100 miles north of the “Great Migration City” where she grew up, Sade has come home to attend her mother’s funeral. But the Dayton Road to which she returns feels different: following the earlier death of her uncle, Mina’s father, the house that served as the center of their childhood has been sold. The two cousins, having lost the parents who connected them, now only have each other. Despite growing up on the same block, Sade and Mina lead very different adulthoods. Mina’s Ivy League education, as well as the high expectations of her father, have pushed her into a comfortable life in San Francisco, where she works for Google and dates a white vegan yoga instructor from Nevada named Chelsea. Conversely, Sade has a felony charge — and a child, whose whereabouts have been unknown since Sade’s arrest. The play asks — interrogates, genuinely and curiously — what is privilege, really? How can we tell who has it and who doesn’t? Can the gap that it creates ever be closed, and if so, how? Sade tells Mina about her dreams for after prison: she and her found community of incarcerated Black women will build a utopia, buying house after house until their entire neighborhood is “the United States of Free Formerly Incarcerated Black Girls.” At the prison library, she reads Sir Thomas More’s “Utopia” and Ntozake Shange’s “For Colored Girls Who Have Considered Suicide / When the Rainbow Is Enuf,” looking forward to the day when she is free and can “lead with” her “chest.” Although Dayton Road’s dreams were pinned onto a young Mina — her career, her education, her geographical gap from home — Sade emerges as the real dreamer here, herself the same kind of “radical-ass bitch” that she describes her cousin to be. At Yale, the cousins’ discourse about elite educational institutions feels particularly pointed. Sade mourns the fact that she never had the opportunity to exist freely, with her only responsibilities for herself being to read, write and learn. In her eyes, Mina’s college years are washed in a golden light — afternoons spent on picnic blankets and classrooms with vaulted ceilings, as schools advertise in their brochures. But for Mina, Sade’s idyllic vision of intelligentsia is far from reality. She reveals to Sade that a mental breakdown, fueled by the pressure of the university environment and the alienation she felt as a Black woman, prompted her to take a medical leave of absence from school. The Ivy League, with its whispers of upward economic mobility, isolates Mina from her home community, characterizing her as spoiled and too good for her father’s house on Dayton Road. Furthermore, her education fails to deliver on its utopian promises — she fails to find a new community at college and, despite her corporate job, feels yoked to the trauma of her childhood and hometown. payne’s script, beyond its thematic maturity, is rich at the level of sound and syntax. As the two cousins confess their fears and desires to one another in Mina’s living room, all of the pain visually expressed in the play’s interludes of night-terrors explodes into vulnerable poetry that demands the audience’s attention. At one point, Sade recalls fondly the rainbow twists that her mother gave her for her fourteenth birthday. Arms stretched out to her sides, Sade declares, “I felt like my body was extending from the beginning to the end of the sky.” Beyond the rhythms created by the spoken-word musicality of the cousins’ dialogue, the play is filled with and conscious of a rich soundscape composed by payne themself. From the John Coltrane album Mina plays to lighten the mood after the funeral to the “Cheetah Girls” soundtrack blasting as they build an elaborate pillow fort, the noises of their childhood weave in and out of Mina’s living room, dissonant with the pain of their adulthood. Under jean-baptiste’s direction, McLarty and Walker form a tight and convincingly-paced ensemble, while they also have chances to step away and shine on their own. Both women move with grace between shy, catching-up conversations, screaming fights, frantic night terrors and joyful moments of play. The tenderness they display toward one another — Mina timidly holding Sade’s head as she breaks down into tears — evokes real feelings of familial love, and so do their moments of unbridled rage, with voices cracking as they express fears that only a cousin and best friend could understand. The play ends with Mina and Sade hugging goodbye, the carcass of their pillow fort on the floor behind them as a Corrections Officer knocks on the door. After the lights fell at the performance I attended, the entire theater was on its feet in an instant; the palpable love and vulnerability involved in the production seemed to have drawn the audience into its embrace. Upon exiting, each audience member was invited to reach into a basket with “words of wisdom” from previous nights’ attendees and then leave wisdom of their own. I drew out an orange slip of paper with a quote from “For Colored Girls.” It reads, “somebody / anybody sing a black girl’s song to bring her out to know herself / to know you.”

title: Whiff and Whim classes of 2026 to take a final bow at Woolsey Hall
subtitle: Ahead of their commencement concert, which will take place this Sunday at 8 p.m., members of the Whiffenpoof and Whim ’n Rhythm classes of 2026 said they are glad to have grown close to their respective groups after months of rehearsals and concerts.
author: Simran Randhawa
text: The Whiffenpoof and Whim ’n Rhythm classes of 2026 will sing in one final show this Sunday at 8 p.m. to commemorate their musical experiences at Yale. At this year’s Woolsey Hall concert, the two all-senior a cappella groups will come together in a performance that represents months spent rehearsing, refining arrangements and singing in harmony. It also marks the culmination of months spent in unusually close company with their respective groups as they prepared for gigs and tours. “The concerts are some of the most exciting things we get to do,” Charlie Calkins ’26+1, the Whiffenpoofs’ business manager, told the News in a phone interview. He said that what he will miss most, however, are the quieter moments: “Getting a drink at a bar, playing cards, exploring new places together.” Founded in 1909, the Whiffenpoofs are Yale’s oldest senior tenor-bass a cappella ensemble and one of Yale’s most prominent student groups. The group is known for their spiffy tuxedos, annual international touring and “The Whiffenpoof Song.” Calkins said that after months of touring and near-constant travel, the Whiffenpoofs have come to know one another so well that at restaurants, if one member were to step away, the others could order food for them without asking their preferences. Sophia Lahik ’26, a member of Whim ’n Rhythm, also said that she has become close to the other members of her group and looks forward to singing with them at Woolsey Hall on Sunday. “I hope I can look around on that stage at all the faces that I have grown to love and have a full circle moment,” Lahik wrote in a text message to the News. Whim ’n Rhythm was founded in 1981 as Yale’s first senior women’s a cappella group. The ensemble is now all-gender, similarly to the Whiffenpoofs, though Whim ’n Rhythm is composed of soprano and alto singers. The group, often referred to simply as “Whim,” blends contemporary singing arrangements with longstanding traditions. Though the groups differ in history and repertoire, members of both said they were approaching Sunday’s performance as something of a coda to their musical journeys at Yale. “As performers, I don’t know if there’s ever a moment that I feel like we’re completely done working on a song in rehearsals,” the Whiffenpoof’s musical director, Eunice Oh ’26+1, wrote in a text message to the News. “Going into this last on-campus performance, I’m so proud of the process of rehearsals and performances we’ve worked hard towards, and I’m excited to showcase that to our original graduating class.” For Whim ’n Rhythm’s Lahik, the venue for Sunday’s performance reminds her of a performance she witnessed before ever joining an a cappella group. “I remember attending Woolsey Jam my first year and thinking about how lucky I’d be to ever step foot on it with Proof of the Pudding,” Lahik said, referring to the a cappella group she sang with before being tapped for Whim ’n Rhythm. “Only a year later, I was soloing Lullaby of Birdland on that very stage in my red dress.” Since both ensembles are composed of entirely new members every year, the Whiffenpoof and Whim ’n Rhythm classes of 2026 inherited longstanding traditions while reshaping their own. The final concert offers seniors an opportunity to reflect on what their class leaves behind. For many, the experience of being in an all-senior a cappella group transformed not only their relationship with singing but also their understanding of community. “I look on this past year with so much gratitude,” Oh said. “So many people have poured their efforts into this absurd opportunity from each of our group members to our hosts along the way to our friends and family cheering us on.” Lahik similarly described the experience of being a member of Whim ’n Rhythm as one she could not have imagined when she first arrived at Yale. “I wouldn’t have seen myself doing a cappella when I came to Yale,” Lahik wrote. “But that one show in freshman year led me to this moment right before I travel the world with a talented group of women I never would have met, but I’ve grown to adore before we all go our separate ways in life.” As the Whiffenpoof and Whim ’n Rhythm classes of 2026 take the Woolsey Hall stage for the final time, the concert will mark a passing of the torch to the next senior class, before the classes of 2027 begin composing memories of their own. Woolsey Hall is located in the Schwarzman Center on 500 College St.

title: ‘Mean Girls’ production seeks to offer seniors a ‘cautionary tale’
subtitle: The Yale Dramatic Association’s show will play from Friday to Sunday. The production boasts one of the largest teams to ever work on a Yale commencement musical and aims to combine Y2K nostalgia with insights into personal identity.
author: Greta Garrison
text: The Yale Dramatic Association’s 2026 Commencement musical, “Mean Girls,” will run this Friday through Sunday at the University Theatre. This show, which director Will Sussbauer ’27 says will pay tribute to the 2004 movie of the same name, was produced over the 10 days following the end of finals period and involves 102 team members. Last year’s commencement musical, “The 25th Annual Putnam County Spelling Bee,” had 14 actors — significantly fewer than this year’s 22. “One of the special things about this edition is it is quite possibly one of the largest casts and pits that we have ever had in a commencement musical,” producer Jack Dozier ’27 said in a voice memo to the News. Based on the 2004 film of the same name, the musical “Mean Girls” centers around 17-year-old Cady Heron as she transitions from being homeschooled in South Africa to attending a clique-ridden American high school run by a trio known as the “Plastics.” The show satirizes social hierarchy structures as Cady navigates culture shock and embarks on a quest for popularity. Sussbauer hopes the show will resonate with graduating seniors because of its themes surrounding identity, as characters learn to overcome concerns about how others perceive them. “I think that’s a lesson for all of us, but especially for folks stepping out into a brand new space, going off into a new job somewhere,” Sussbauer said in a phone interview with the News. “I wouldn’t be surprised if a lot of folks will enter next year feeling a little bit like Cady Heron.” In 2024, actress and comedian Tina Fey helped turn her beloved screenplay into a musical movie by incorporating TikTok and social media trends into the plot. According to Sussbauer, the goal of their production of the Dramat’s commencement musical was to bring back the “Y2K 2000’s energy” essential to the original film while still making it their own. He said he hopes the stage edition will pay proper homage to what he called a “nostalgic cultural staple,” with nods to the influences of MySpace, YouTube and Facebook. “People who come into this who are fans of the original movie are going to very, very much enjoy it because it’s got all the same plot beats,” he said. According to Aabi Whyte-Spence ’27, the show’s costume designer, some of the costumes were modeled after the movie, but most were original designs. “I tried to take inspiration from the early 2000s, when the events of the show are taking place,” she wrote in an email to the News. Whyte-Spence also sought to incorporate character development into the costumes, especially those that will be worn by Cady. While Cady begins dressing with little sense of style, she evolves throughout the musical, fashioning herself like the Plastics before ultimately finding her own unique aesthetic. “Her costuming journey is parallel to her personal journey; she doesn't quite know herself at the very beginning of the show, gets caught up in trying to emulate other people, and finally ends the show by trying to be a better version of herself, rather than someone else entirely,” Whyte-Spence wrote. Dozier said that the long workdays demanded by the show’s short production timeline have not impeded the positivity of his experience. “At the end of the day, it’s just proven to be so fun with such a lovely and delightful group of people,” he said. Whyte-Spence said there have been some challenges in working with such a large group. “The cast contains 22 wonderful actors, all with multiple costumes, which means that lots of quick changes are happening backstage,” she wrote. “Making sure that all of the actors have assistance to change into their costumes on time involves the cooperation of multiple departments within the production team.” Sussbauer also noted how his connection to graduating seniors informed his rehearsal process. He said that many others involved in the production are also taking inspiration from members in the class of 2026 who were involved in theater during their time at Yale. “There has been a tremendous group of seniors, specifically in theater, who all of us who are a part of this commencement show have worked with and really admire and really enjoy working with,” Sussbauer said. “I’m basing so much of the way that I run a rehearsal room and the way that I think about what doing this show means off of the team that did it last year and did it so well.” Sussabuer encourages audience members to engage with performers. “Laugh and clap and cheer and jeer and participate as much as you can,” he said. “That is what this show is for. It’s for massive, massive audience energy.” He also hopes it might leave audience members with some important life lessons. “It’s almost a what-not-to-do,” Sussbauer said. “Don’t be mean. Don’t be manipulative to the people you call your friends.” He continued: “As the opening words of the first song say, this is a cautionary tale.” The Yale University Theatre is located at 222 York St.

title: Why the Lipstick, now tucked away, should return to Beinecke Plaza
subtitle: The sculpture, which served as a symbol against war and consumerism after its 1969 installation on Beinecke Plaza and was recently moved from Morse College, does not best embody its artistic purpose in the Yale University Art Gallery sculpture garden.
author: Ellie Koo
text: If you wander into the Yale University Art Gallery’s sculpture garden and glance upward, you’ll notice a pointed red cylinder resembling the tip of a lipstick tube protruding from the ground. The figure is “Lipstick (Ascending) on Caterpillar Tracks, ” a piece by the Swedish sculptor Claes Oldenburg ’50. Created in 1969 amid anti-war student protests, the sculpture was originally installed on Beinecke Plaza, where it served as a podium for speeches during the May Day demonstrations and symbolized a pointed critique of consumer culture and institutional authority. All of which raises a question: Why is it now tucked behind the walls of the Yale University Art Gallery? The preservation of art has long been debated by art critics, historians and institutions that steward cultural memory. The sculpture’s relocation to the gallery’s sculpture garden in March stirred sadness among students and protest from alumni, particularly those affiliated with Morse College, where it had stood since 1979. The head of Morse College wrote to students in December that the Lipstick would be removed from the college’s courtyard for “conservation and care” after etchings were found in its underside. In an email to alumni who objected to the sculpture’s relocation, University President Maurie McInnis cited “significant sun damage.” But what does restoration really entail? While the surface of a work can be returned to pristine condition, free of wear and damage, such treatment addresses only its material form. What about the restoration of the artist’s original intention and the work’s original context and purpose? Minimalist artists such as Donald Judd have argued that the space surrounding a work of art is an extension of it — functioning as a material in the same way a canvas, or any other medium, would. Perhaps the most daring expressions of this idea are site-specific works like Robert Smithson’s “ Spiral Jetty ,” where the environment itself becomes the medium. To displace such a piece is, in effect, to destroy it. By that measure, restoration remains incomplete unless the sculpture returns to its original site on Beinecke Plaza. Oldenburg conceived “Lipstick (Ascending) on Caterpillar Tracks” as a work of public art. Its removal from Beinecke 10 months after its installation and subsequent move to Morse College in 1974 already marked a fundamental rupture from that original vision. Detached from the site that gave it meaning, the sculpture inherently loses a part of its conceptual force. All this leads to a broader, longstanding question: What role should art play in our society? For an institution like Yale, perhaps the goal of installing art is to educate the community and make art more accessible to the broader public. That mission is an admirable one, and in that sense, the Yale University Art Gallery by and large serves as a more inclusive setting for the sculpture than the Morse College courtyard, which is restricted to those carrying a university ID. But there’s also the argument that art is simply a portal for us to experience an artist’s unique vision for the world, in their own terms. Seen that way, relocating “Lipstick (Ascending) on Caterpillar Tracks” into a museum setting, when it was meant to stand in a democratic space outside of traditional institutions, feels like neutralizing the very critique the work embodied, softening the urgency and diminishing the essence of the piece. I am for the ephemeral experience of encountering art and noticing the physical and emotional sensations that arise when we meet a work through the condition and lens its creator intended. The clarity of these sensations is inevitably dulled by the sculpture’s move to the art gallery, because Oldenburg did not envision it as a museum work. While its placement in the art gallery serves an educational purpose, it is impossible to view it in that manner and attempt to understand and feel what the artist sought to express in the moment. If we are to truly experience the sculpture as an art and appreciate it in its most complete and vital form, it must be reconnected to the space it originally occupied — and that’s Beinecke Plaza.

title: Yale Rep to close out season with drama school alum’s original play
subtitle: “Furlough’s Paradise,” which was written by a David Geffen School of Drama alum, explores themes of grief and prison abolition and is set to officially open next Thursday at the Yale Repertory Theatre to close out the 2025-26 season.
author: Brett Penk, Kiva Bank
text: “Furlough’s Paradise” is set to open next Thursday at the Yale Repertory Theatre. The show will close out the theater’s 2025-26 season and mark James Bundy’s DRA ’95 last season as the artistic director of the Rep. Bundy has served as dean of the David Geffen School of Drama since 2002 and is set to retire at the end of June. Written by a.k. payne DRA ’23, the two-woman show follows Sade, who is on a three-day furlough from prison to grieve the death of her mother and uncle with her cousin Mina. “I feel really grateful to be able to bring this play to New Haven in two different iterations and to be able to really deeply connect with both the Yale University community and the community outside of the University,” payne said in a voice memo emailed to the News by a Rep spokesperson. The show, which won the Susan Smith Blackburn Prize in 2025 , was originally written by payne when they were a playwriting student at the School of Drama, and it later premiered in 2023 at the Carlotta Festival, which showcases the work of the school’s graduating playwrights. payne said that in addition to incorporating themes of grief and prison abolition, the work also “really dances with the blues” since the music genre is significant to how they write plays and conceptualize Black theater. “The blues, to me, is about paradox, about the capacity to hold multiple things that are seemingly nonsensical at the same time,” they said. “As the show explores grief and prison abolition alongside moments of joy and levity and laughter, to me, it’s very integral to what it means to be Black in this world, the kind of dancing with both the kind of space that we carve out to breathe in the midst of some of the most impossible situations.” The two women seemingly follow different paths in life. Mina went to an Ivy League school and has a corporate job, and Sade is in prison. But they both feel imprisoned in their lives, Tiffany McLarty, who plays Mina, said in a phone interview. Lauren Walker DRA ’25 is reprising her role as Sade. She previously played the character as a first-year acting student at the School of Drama when the work premiered at the Iseman Theater for the Carlotta Festival. “Sade is one of my favorite characters I’ve ever worked on, primarily because she is somebody who is honest, almost to a fault,” Walker said in a phone interview. “She’s very direct, and I think she’s taught me a lot about the importance of being direct and of being honest and of not being afraid of your feelings, no matter how big they are.” According to McLarty, there is something in Mina relatable for most people who watch the performance. “I think I can see myself in both characters, and I’m sure many people will be able to see themselves in each character,” McLarty said. “Ultimately, the play is about grief and the different stages of it and how people deal with it individually, specifically Black women.” Walker echoed similar sentiments, saying the grief in the play is similar to what she experienced after graduating from the School of Drama last year and returning as an alum to perform at the Rep. “I can definitely relate as far as coming back to a place that used to feel like home every day, and kind of feeling like you're a stranger passing through,” Walker said. “But I would say my position as an actor, as a person, is way more privileged than Sade is.” When the Rep announced its 2025-26 season last year, Walker said she knew she wanted to play Sade. This January, she booked the role of Sade at the Rep and the Geva Theatre, which is presenting “Furlough’s Paradise” from April 14 to May 10, but she ultimately chose to return to the Rep. “I was like, ‘Oh, I have to go home.’ To go home and do it like that was a dream of mine, even a year ago,” she said. “I’m really so happy to be here and to be doing this show.” Walker added that last August she lost her grandmother, who raised her, which added a deeper layer to her character. “Returning to this play with that new understanding of grief and a new connection to Sade in that way, I think it’s completely enriched my relationship to the text and to the character,” she said. payne said they were “grateful” to be closing out Bundy’s last season at the Rep as artistic director. “I am very in awe of the breadth of shows that James Bundy has been able to usher into being as the artistic director of the institution and really honored to be the final show of that tenure,” they said. Walker said that it was “special” to be a part of the last production during Bundy’s leadership, and that closing out the season for him is a “gift.” “He really has such a great legacy here at the school, and he’s done so much for not only the Rep, but for the student body at the School of Drama, and so I’m just endlessly inspired by him and grateful for the grace with which he leads,” she said. “I’m excited to be able to close this out for him and to kind of send him off.” Walker said “Furlough’s Paradise” is aligned with political commentary of the other productions of the season, which have included Eugène Ionesco’s “ Rhinoceros ” and Zora Neale Hurston’s “ Spunk .” “Seeing two full-bodied, healthy, dark-skinned Black women on the stage in a story that completely requires an audience to see them in their full humanity, I think that is quite profound,” she said. The Yale Repertory Theatre is located on 1120 Chapel St.

title: Seniors look back on four years in Yale’s undergraduate theater scene
subtitle: Three seniors — Emiliano Cáceres Manzano, Hannah Kurczeski and Zaida Rio Polanco — reflected on how making theater on campus has influenced their academic, creative and social lives.
author: Sofia Gaviria Partow
text: As the class of 2026 approaches graduation, seniors involved in Yale’s theater scene are reflecting on the communities they have built with fellow performers, professors and audiences during college. In interviews with the News, three seniors highlighted a profound sense of intimacy developed through working with the same group of actors and directors for various productions, as well as their relationships with a tuned-in audience and the influence of coursework across departments. “I think that the theater world can be super insular here, which is one of its biggest strengths,” Emiliano Cáceres Manzano ’26, who is known for his credits directing “ Spring Awakening ,” “ The 25th Annual Putnam County Spelling Bee ,” and other musicals and plays, said. Cáceres Manzano, who is double majoring in Film and Media Studies and English, described the long-term collaborative relationships he has built within the theater community as some of the most meaningful parts of his time at Yale. Cáceres Manzano said he first became interested in theater through a series of poetry recitation contests he participated in as an elementary school student living in Mexico. That led him to acting, which in turn opened his eyes to directing as a means by which to shape the environment in which theater is created, he said. “One of my favorite quotes about acting is that ‘acting is being private in public,’” Cáceres Manzano said. “I recently just acted a little bit in a friend’s web series, and it reminded me of how vulnerable it can be. I think it’s important as a director to understand what you’re asking your actors to do.” He also shared how his academic studies, particularly in the English major, have grounded his work as a director, a discipline that he described as ultimately founded in text analysis. He added that academic exploration beyond theater has allowed him to bring new perspectives and creativity to his shows. For Hannah Kurczeski ’26 — who also spends a lot of her time outside of class working on student productions, including playing Sally Bowles in “ Cabaret ” — the decision to major in Theater, Dance and Performance Studies was an easy one. “This is what I love to do and is what I ideally would love to spend all of my time doing, always,” Kurczeski said. “There’s no place I’d rather be than rehearsal.” Kurczeski mentioned Annette Jolles, Daniel Egan and Sohina Sidhu as three transformative professors she’s had during her time at Yale. The three professors are all in the Theater, Dance and Performance Studies department. Kurczeski reflected on a piece of advice she had received from Jolles, who is also her thesis advisor. They had been speaking about the tendency to overintellectualize what’s happening on stage, which can result from studying theater in an academic setting. “You always need to be serving the story and serving the work,” Kurczeski said, referencing Jolles’ advice. “At the end of the day, you have to trust yourself — that all the invisible prep work that you did, trust that it is there for you and it has got your back, and just be in the moment.” Zaida Rio Polanco ’26, who is majoring in Environmental Studies but has also taken several theater courses at Yale, recalled another class — “Acting Shakespeare,” taught by Daniela Varon — that she said fundamentally changed her approach to acting. The class introduced Polanco to a concept of three macrocosms into which the body is separated: the head as the heavenly space, the gut as what grounds an actor and, finally, the bowel region. Varon explained to the class that acting in Shakespearean plays requires one to locate different lines in the various regions of the body, according to Polanco. “It’s those distinctions within any piece of work that I’ve worked on since then that have made a difference in how I approach new dialogue,” Polanco said. Polanco highlighted that the distinctions have been particularly useful to her in conceptualizing darker roles she has played in theater at Yale, including the character Anita in a production of “ West Side Story ,” which was directed by Cáceres Manzano two years ago. Both Polanco and Cáceres Manzano described “West Side Story” as one of the most meaningful projects they’ve worked on at Yale. For Polanco, it allowed her to combine the three things she loves most — singing, dancing and acting — and fall back in love with theater. For Cáceres Manzano, the show taught him how to create theater both logistically and artistically. “The high stakes of, ‘Oh my God, I don’t know if we’re gonna be able to pull this off,’ I think created an experience that was unlike any other,” Cáceres Manzano said. “Everybody was really, really invested in it. Even the audiences were invested.” Cáceres Manzano also underscored the power of audiences, adding that one of the most special parts of doing theater at Yale is having access to audiences who are deeply interested in performance and art. Kurczeski also said that the University’s support of artistic projects through Creative and Performing Arts grants and other programs means that the creative possibilities at Yale are endless for actors and directors alike. “It grants students so much agency, especially at Yale, that I think frankly would blow the minds of people who study theater at more traditional BFA schools,” Kurczeski said. “If you can dream it, you can do it. You can truly make anything happen, and you don’t need to wait for someone else to make it happen for you.” Kurczeski, Cáceres Manzano and Polanco all highlighted the collaborative nature of theater at Yale and its ability to build networks of trust and support among participants. “Theater is such a powerful community builder, both the process of making it, but also watching it. It’s a presence art form, it demands so much engagement and real-life connection,” Cáceres Manzano said. “I think spaces like that are really missing from our lives.” Yale’s 325th commencement will take place on May 18.

title: New Schwarzman Center art exhibits highlight student experiences
subtitle: The Yale Schwarzman Center this month unveiled five exhibitions, showcasing the work of 53 student artists from across Yale and New Haven. Visitors can view some exhibitions in person and some online.
author: Angelina Kovalchuk
text: Five exhibitions that opened in the Yale Schwarzman Center on April 7 feature creative work from 53 young artists, including New Haven high school students, Yale undergraduates and Yale graduate students. “Each exhibition explores how the students see and interact with the world; they are all responding to different themes and questions,” Carlynne Robinson, the center’s associate director of venues and exhibitions, wrote in an email to the News. “They explore identity, unity and memory, and nature and emotion.” The works span a range of mediums, including visual art, photography, installation, digital work and multimedia. All exhibitions are free and open to the public. “Whether someone is coming to the Yale Schwarzman Center to have lunch or see a performance in The Dome, I hope that people visit the exhibitions multiple times and experience something new every visit,” Robinson said. “Call-to-Connect,” located in the building’s rotunda, was designed by Soleil Piverger ’27. The exhibition consists of two payphones positioned on opposite sides of the space, inviting visitors to connect and converse from afar. Each payphone is accompanied by a phonebook, which contains extension numbers corresponding to previously-recorded messages. “Even if you’re not creating one in the moment, you can look through and listen to all these other people’s memories and moments of connection,” Robinson said of the installation. “The View From Here: Accessing Art Through Photography” is on the second floor of the center. In collaboration with the Yale Center for British Art, the annual exhibition features photography by New Haven high school students. According to Robinson, the photos were taken only on smartphone cameras. “All you need is a cell phone, because these days, every smartphone has a camera. So everybody is a photographer in some sort of way,” she said. The theme of this year’s “The View from Here” exhibition centers around “pathways” through the students’ lives and communities, according to the Yale Center for British Art’s website . On the Schwarzman Center’s lower level, “Rooted in Heritage: Art Across Yale’s Cultural Centers” showcases visual art and photography from Yale undergraduate and graduate students. Each of Yale’s five cultural centers are represented in the exhibition. “I just asked people to kind of reflect on the multicultural communities that they’re a part of at Yale, and how they feel represented here within their community and back home,” Robinson, who curated the exhibition, recalled. Stella Duran Gonzalez ’29, one of the artists spotlighted in the installation, said her identity and experiences as a Puerto Rican greatly inspire her work. In her featured pieces “Escúchame” and “Isla del Encanto,” Duran Gonzalez highlights classic figures and iconography from Puerto Rican culture, including the coquí frogs native to the island. Duran Gonzalez wants her work to inspire viewers to reflect on their own cultures and backgrounds, she said. “This dual reflection — artist reflecting while working, viewer reflecting while observing — is what I believe makes art so powerful,” she said. Olivia Jiang SOM ’26 contributed the pieces “Ablaze” and “Waiting,” which feature urban scenes from ancient and modern China, to the “Rooted in Heritage” exhibit. “The specific settings are deeply Chinese, but the feelings of longing, community, vitality and loss belong to everyone,” Jiang said of her work. The corridor of the Schwarzman Center’s lower level displays “West of the River,” which features rural landscape photography by Christian Badach ART ’26. “His work is focusing on rural landscapes and how they function in the dichotomy of emotions,” Robinson said. “So thinking about community and isolation, bliss and madness, these different kinds of ways that you can feel emotions.” Another exhibition, “Storyboard: Poetry and the Everyday,” is showcased virtually on the center’s website. The digital gallery showcases a mix of visual art, audio, video and written work submitted by Yale students. Students were asked to convey in their submissions the ways in which they interact with poetry “on a daily basis, if at all,” according to the website . Elisabeth Nwasokwa ’26 submitted a poem inspired by the autumn views on Hillhouse Avenue. “While my poems are often about the beauty within the simplicity of nature,” she said, “the desire to reflect that beauty aligns with the long and continued history of storytelling of both my Nigerian and Haitian cultural heritage.” “Poetry and the Everyday” was curated by Babette Thomas GRD ’26.

title: ‘MUZIKO’ concert combines jazz and traditional Indian music
subtitle: In their performance on Sunday, Yale Dhvani, the Yale Undergraduate Jazz Collective and the Yale Undergraduate Chamber Orchestra merged ensembles in a concert centered around traditional Indian raag.
author: Simran Randhawa
text: “MUZIKO: Cycles of Sound & Season” brought together and celebrated cross-cultural musical traditions on Sunday afternoon in a performance centering South Asian voices at Yale. The striking sounds of violin, veena and saxophone billowed through the auditorium of Sheffield-Sterling-Strathcona Hall, coming together to create a unique melody that incorporated familiar sounds. MUZIKO blended together traditional Indian classical music, orchestration and Western jazz. “Music is a language,” Anjal Jain ’26, an event organizer and the lead singer for Yale Dhvani, said in a phone interview. “Everyone came from such different musical backgrounds and has been able to come together.” Reflecting on one of her last performances at Yale, Jain said that the experience reinforced above all else that music is capable of bridging even the most distinct artistic traditions. The production featured members of Yale Dhvani, the Yale Undergraduate Jazz Collective and the Yale Undergraduate Chamber Orchestra. Each group brought original compositions that centered on raag-based melodic structures, Western harmonic framing and jazz-like improvisational techniques. According to Jain, the performance was centered around the concept of a multi-genre jugalbandhi, meaning “call-and-response.” The music created the sense of a conversation, emphasizing dialogue extending beyond the performers into the audience. For Jain, the collaborative process was as significant as the final performance. “Something that was so special was that it felt like we were all feeding off of each other,” she said, describing a rehearsal environment that emphasized experimentation and shared learning. Unlike traditional orchestral settings, in which compositions are strictly notated, MUZIKO relied on a more fluid, workshopping approach. The performers of the orchestra, accustomed to structured compositions, adapted their style to the more experimental structure of jazz and raag. Jazz musicians involved in the project similarly described the process as both challenging and illuminating. “Limiting ourselves to the available notes in the raag was definitely a unique challenge,” Arnav Travers ’29, a member of the jazz ensemble, said. “We kept the melodic content while mixing jazz into the harmony and rhythm.” For drummer Jonny Duncan ’29, the collaboration required a different kind of attentiveness. He noted that working alongside Indian percussionists required him “to listen a lot more,” especially given the contrast between jazz drumming and hand-based rhythmic traditions. Rather than fully adapting one style into another, Duncan described the process as “coming in and out” of jazz, which allowed the two traditions to coexist without one overpowering the other. The collaborative nature of the project extended beyond composition into the performance itself. Musicians described the rehearsals as dynamic, responsive environments in which improvisation and adaptation were central. Tabla player Ayush Iyer ’26 noted how the final piece mirrored a traditional jazz structure while remaining grounded in raag form. There was “a lot of improvisation, a lot of learning from each other.” For many performers, the experience reinforced a broader takeaway. Travers described it as an experience that instilled humility, saying that it taught him the limits in his knowledge of music. “This whole process has really shown me that I have a lot to learn,” Travers said. Sheffield-Sterling-Strathcona Hall is located on 1 Prospect St. Correction, April 21: An earlier version of this article misstated the name of a stringed instrument played in the concert. It was the veena, not the sitar.

title: Former Yale golfer James Nicholas ’19 returns to the U.S. Open
subtitle: James Nicholas ’19 fired –2 over 36 holes at final qualifying to punch his ticket to the 2026 U.S. Open at Shinnecock Hills.
author: Brody Gilkison
text: PURCHASE, New York — Before last year, no Yale golfer had played in the U.S. Open since Bob Heintz ’92 in 1999. However, James Nicholas ’19 secured a spot in the 125th iteration of the tournament in 2025 — and this Monday, he qualified yet again for the 126th in Shinnecock Hills. Nicholas earned his spot during “Golf’s Longest Day,” the day when the United States Golf Association hosts 10 of its 13 U.S. Open final qualifying events. For those not already exempt into the U.S. Open, qualifying typically comes in the form of an 18-hole local qualifier in early May and a 36-hole final qualifying site a week before the championship begins. “It feels great after such a long day,” Nicholas said in an interview with the News on Monday. He added that he has experienced “a lot of ups and downs this season,” but hopes that having qualified for the tournament will help him “finish the season strong.” Nicholas, a one-time winner on the Korn Ferry Tour — the PGA’s developmental tour — sits at 15th place on the tour’s point list and inside the Top 20 mark needed to earn his PGA Tour card for next year. Just days after playing in the BMW Charity Pro-Am in South Carolina, Nicholas flew to New York to play 36 holes in the hopes of earning his way into his second consecutive U.S. Open. The New York site, which was the only site hosted at two separate clubs, was played at Century Country Club and the Golf Club of Purchase, both in Purchase, New York. Nicholas teed up first at the Golf Club of Purchase and took advantage of scorable conditions with a cool –4 round of 68 to put himself in second place after 18 holes. While he was in a good position halfway through the event, Nicholas needed to remain in the top four of a strong field in order to qualify. Among others competing at his site were four-time All-American Ben James of Virginia, as well as seasoned PGA Tour veterans Max Greyserman and Kevin Roy. The second round proved to be much more difficult, as the rock-hard greens and thick rough required accuracy and a strong mental game. Through 27 holes, Nicholas was still two shots clear of the cut line but had to finish strong in order to punch his ticket to the national championship. Standing on the 17th tee, Nicholas was a shot clear of the cutline at –3, leading amateur Christian Cavaliere by one stroke and two strokes ahead of Matt Jones, a two-time PGA Tour winner himself. However, Nicholas misjudged a greenside shot on the 17th, causing him to drop a shot before paring the final hole to post rounds of 68–72 and sit in a tie for the final spot at two-under. Luckily for Nicholas, Cavaliere made a costly mistake on his final hole of the day, a short, scorable par-4 hole. After Cavaliere double-bogeyed the hole, Nicholas qualified for the U.S. Open without needing to play any extra holes. Nicholas will be playing in this week’s Korn Ferry Tour event in Amarillo, Texas, before heading back to his home state to play at Shinnecock Hills next week.

title: Bulldogs return to NCAA tournament after second-place Ivy finish
subtitle: Following a strong performance at the Ivy League Championship last weekend, the Elis earned an at-large bid at Tuesday’s selection show to compete for the National Championship for the fifth consecutive year.
author: Eli Ratner
text: Yale women’s crew will return to the national stage after showing strength at last weekend’s Ivy League championship and earning an at-large bid at Tuesday’s NCAA selection show. No. 7 nationally-ranked Yale will make its fifth straight appearance at the tournament with its fifth-seeded first varsity eight, sixth-seeded second varsity eight and seventh-seeded first varsity four crews. With a national title on the line in Gainesville, Georgia, 22 teams will take the water. The Bulldogs’ first varsity eight won the national title last year, and the team narrowly finished second to Stanford. This year, Yale’s first varsity eight boat, the defending national champion, will compete in Heat 4, facing off against the University of Massachusetts, Rutgers University, Princeton, the University of Miami and the University of Rhode Island. The second varsity eight boat will take on Boston University, the University of Miami, the University of Virginia, Oregon State University and Jacksonville University in Heat 3, and in the first varsity four, Yale will compete in Heat 2 against the University of Texas, the University of California, Berkeley, Boston University and the University Michigan. Tuesday’s at-large bid follows the Bulldogs’ strong showing at the Ivy League Championship last weekend. All Yale crews medaled, with the first varsity four and second varsity four taking home gold, the first varsity eight, second varsity eight and third varsity eight winning silver and the third varsity four making a bronze finish. With 82 points, Yale won the team points title for the second consecutive year, narrowly surpassing Princeton by two points. The Tigers, who won the conference championship, earned the Ivy League’s automatic bid. In addition to Yale, two other Ivies, Brown and Columbia, also earned at-large berths. Now, the Bulldog crews look to avenge the narrow loss to the Tigers at the conference championship and return to New Haven with new hardware, cementing their position as one of the nation’s top programs. The Elis will hit the water on Friday, May 29.

title: Yale heads to regionals after beating Brown, capturing Ivy Pennant
subtitle: Although Yale had a losing record to Brown and Columbia throughout the regular season, the Bulldogs swept the two teams at the home Ivy tournament this weekend, advancing to the NCAA Regional Championships for the first time since 2017.
author: Justin Leahy
text: The Yale Bulldogs advanced to the NCAA regionals after defeating the Brown Bears 7-5 on Sunday afternoon in the final round of the Ivy League Tournament. With the win, Yale concluded a 3-0 sweep of the double-elimination tournament at George H.W. Bush ’48 Field. This year’s rise to regionals marks the Bulldogs’ first since 2017 and seventh in program history. The Bulldogs entered the tournament having won a dramatic second consecutive Ivy regular season title. Entering their last regular season series of the year against Dartmouth earlier this month, they needed a three-game sweep to take the title. They did just that, winning the regular-season Ivy title and earning the right to host the Ivy League Tournament.
