To reach the tournament championship game on their home turf, Yale knocked off last year’s champions, Columbia, 12-3 on Friday and this year’s runner-up, Brown, 5-2 on Saturday, before defeating Brown again on Sunday afternoon. “All the things this team has accomplished and worked through this year have all led to this,” captain Davis Hanson ’26 wrote in a text message to the News. “It’s even more special that we all get to go compete together for a championship.” Hanson emphasized the Bulldogs’ strong mindset heading into the tournament after Yale had dropped two of three to Brown and lost or tied every game versus Columbia in the regular season. “With Brown and Columbia, we really adapted the mentality that we owe those teams our best performance,” Hanson wrote in a text message to the News. “Those are two really good teams this year, and they got the better of us in the regular season, but everyone on our team wanted to play them again in the playoffs. We wanted to show them what we truly are.” The lean, light-throwing righty Daniel Cohen ’26 started for the Bulldogs on Sunday. Although Cohen had been dominant all season, compiling a sub-5 ERA, he struggled in his first two innings, giving up three runs and failing to record a sixth out. Though Yale was down 0-3 heading into the bottom of the second, Yale pieced together a rally. Garrett Larsen ’27, Kaiden Dossa ’27 and Chris DiPrima ’27 provided clutch back-to-back-to-back hits, eventually leveling the score at 3-3. After Cohen was pulled in the second inning, Teo Spadaccini ’27 came into the game. Spadaccini served up nearly six innings of one-run control, setting a near-impeccable tone for the afternoon. The Bulldogs went on to score four more unanswered runs, backed up by clutch doubles from Hanson, Dossa and Jack Dauer ’28. When Spadaccini faced a little trouble at the end of his performance, Ben Winslow ’27, a reliever who has been dominant all year, came in to mop up, instead walking two straight batters. Jack Ohman ’28, last season’s Ivy League Pitcher of the Year who had started on Friday, then came on in relief. He quickly forced three outs, walking only one batter, limiting the bases-loaded, no out damage to just two runs and helping Yale keep their lead. The next inning, Ohman closed out the game in dominant fashion, securing the Bulldogs the game and the Ivy League title. Winning the Ivy League meant especially more for Hanson, who tore his ACL in practice before the start of last season. “To me, personally, this game means everything,” Hanson wrote in a text message to the News before the championship contest. In Friday’s first game of the tournament, Yale faced No. 4 ranked Columbia. During the regular season, Yale struggled versus Columbia, going 0-2-1 in New York. However, Yale stood firm. Ohman tossed five innings of two-run ball on the mound. Ohman also stranded six runners and struck out five, often hitting speeds of 96 and 97 miles per hour, with many MLB scouts watching in the stands. After Ohman’s two runs, the Bulldog offense put up a concerted effort to back up their ace. With two outs, DiPrima hit a bases-clearing double, pushing the Bulldogs ahead 6-2. Spadaccini stood sharp on the mound in relief, retiring all nine batters he faced to carry Yale through the eighth inning. Yale then added four more runs in the bottom of the eighth to close out the 12-3 win. Dossa, an ALl-Ivy honoree, reached base four times, stole two bags and extended his on-base streak to 35 games. Dossa kept his perfect 28-for-28 stolen base record for the year. Another All-Ivy honoree, Larsen, who led the Ivy League in batting average this year (.389) added an RBI double in the eighth and also scored three times. In game two on Saturday, Tate Evans ’26 — who was named as the new Ivy League Pitcher of the Year on Thursday, succeeding Ohman — threw seven innings of one-run ball against the third-ranked Brown Bears. Evans tossed a career-high 123 pitches and struck out five Bears. During the regular season, Yale had lost two of three against Brown. But again, Yale was able to overcome the odds. The game was tied through the first five innings. However, the offense proved strong yet again. In the sixth inning, Larsen legged out an infield single, swiped second, then moved to third on a sacrifice bunt by DiPrima. Dauer then lined a double down the line, driving in Larsen and giving Yale the lead. To end the game, the workhorse reliever Winslow closed out the final two innings, securing the 5-2 win. The victory marked Evans’ seventh win of the season, placing him on top of the conference. Pablo Rodriguez ’27 summed up the Bulldogs’ no-quit mentality in a text message to the News. “Every pitch, the ball might not always fall our way, but when we stick with it and grind out every at-bat, eventually we’re going to get the results we want,” Rodriguez wrote. Yale will learn its NCAA Tournament regional assignment on May 25 after the bracket is revealed on ESPN2.

title: Seniors led first playoff run in history, four victories over Harvard
subtitle: This season, the class of 2026 led the Bulldogs in claiming the Ivy title, which, under a new Ivy League rule, allowed them to reach the Football Championship Subdivision playoffs for the first time in history. The seniors also leave behind an undefeated record against Harvard.
author: Brody Gilkison
text: The bar for making a splash in Yale’s football history is high. The Bulldogs have 18 college football national championships, the most in the history of the sport. Walter Camp, class of 1880, known colloquially as the father of football , headlines a deep list of accomplished Yale alumni in the sport’s chronology. But the seniors still managed to leave their mark on the program throughout a career that ended with Yale’s first playoff run in history. After joining a team that had gone 5–5 in its previous season and ended its year with a heartbreaking loss to Harvard, the now-seniors saw a stark contrast during their rookie season. Although Team 149 dropped its first game of the season to Holy Cross, it went on to win eight of its final nine games, including a 19-14 victory over Harvard, and became the Ivy League champions. Among the only members of the class of 2026 to get meaningful playing time on Team 149 were Josh Pitsenberger ’26 and Inumidun Ayo-Durojaiye ’26, both of whom would become foundational pieces on future Yale teams. In the now-seniors’ sophomore season, Yale’s 150th football team took the field looking to defend their conference title. The Bulldogs got off to a slow start, losing their first two games to Holy Cross and Cornell. But from there, the Bulldogs won six of their next seven games, entering the week of The Game with a 6–3 record. At The Game, Yale took down the rival Harvard Crimson to win the Ivy League Championship for the second year in a row. With the win, the class of 2026 were now two for two in conference championships — and against the team from the North. The 2024 season brought adversity, with two early conference losses handicapping Team 151’s chances of winning its third straight Ivy League championship. However, the Bulldogs persevered, fighting all sixty minutes of every game that season. This time around, Team 151 entered the week of The Game with one goal and one goal only. Yale, with a record of 6–3 overall and 3–3 in conference play, was mathematically eliminated from winning the conference title. The only thing left to do in the 2024 season was to take down the Crimson for the third year in a row. In an absolute barn burner that Yale led the entire game, the Bulldogs upset the Football Championship Subdivision No. 17 Harvard, winning 34-29. Among the top moments of the game was Pitsenberger’s unforgettable 39-yard touchdown catch and score , where he seemingly juked out half of Harvard’s defense en route to the end zone. It was the class of 2026’s third straight victory over the Crimson. The following offseason was unlike anything Ivy League football had ever seen before. In a shocking announcement , the Ivy League adopted a rule change allowing its teams to play in the postseason. This meant that, for the first time ever, the conference champion would receive an automatic berth in the Football Championships Subdivision playoffs following the end of the regular season. Yale, a program that has long held the record for most national championships, was given a chance to compete for their first title since 1944, when the Ivy Group Agreement was created. With the stakes now as high as they had ever been before, Team 152 entered the 2025 season led by team captain Josh Pitsenberger. The Bulldogs started strong, with two straight wins to kick off the season. Then, Yale fell 31-13 to Lehigh University, led by then-coach Kevin Cahill. The following week, Yale’s defense surrendered a last-second game-winning field goal to Dartmouth to fall to 2–2 for the season. The Bulldogs bounced back a week later with a 40-point win over Stonehill College to right the ship. From there on, it was smooth sailing for Team 152 as they rattled off four consecutive victories over Ivy League opponents to set the stage for the 141st playing of The Game. No. 8 Harvard entered the final week of the regular season with a perfect 9–0 record, the heavy favorite to be the inaugural Ivy League automatic qualifier. But Yale, with just one conference loss up to this point in the season, still had good reason to keep its Ivy title hopes alive. A win over Harvard would have put both teams at 6–1 in conference play, with the head-to-head tiebreaker then favoring Yale. Yale stripped Harvard on the first play from scrimmage, deep in Crimson territory, and never looked back. A quick score and another field goal put the Bulldogs up 10-0 nine minutes into the first quarter. A few drives later, Pitsenberger punched the ball in for the first of his three touchdowns on the day to put the Elis up 17-0. While Harvard finally managed to put points on the board, in the following drive, wideout Nico Brown ’26 hauled in a 64-yard touchdown pass, sending the Bowl into pandemonium. Yale maintained its lead throughout the game, and the Bulldogs secured their third Ivy title in four years. In addition to their fourth-straight win over the Crimson, Yale had also punched their ticket to their first-ever Football Championship Subdivision playoff appearance. The first-round playoff matchup saw Team 152 traveling to take on Youngstown State University. Down 42-14 halfway through the third quarter, the Bulldogs pulled off the unthinkable. Overcoming a 0.2 percent win probability, Yale came all the way back to knock off the Penguins 43-42 . In an instant classic, the Bulldogs won their first playoff game in school history. The following week, Yale’s Cinderella story came to a close with a close road loss to the eventual champions, Montana State University. Many of the Team 152 seniors have since found new homes. Pitsenberger recently signed as a priority undrafted free agent with the Houston Texans, while Ayo-Durojaiye signed with the Hamilton Tiger-Cats of the Canadian Football League. With eligibility still remaining, many other seniors found new homes with high-major FBS programs as well. Michael Bennett ’26 recently departed to join the Oregon Ducks, while Brown, Ezekiel Larry ’27 and Wilhelm Daal ’26 all left to join ACC programs in Stanford University, the University of Virginia and Duke University, respectively. Center Leo Bluhm ’26 is headed to Rice University, defensive lineman Ejiroghene Egodogbare ’26 committed to the University of Houston and safety Josh Tarver ’26 transferred to Tarleton State University to round out the Texas crew.

title: Seniors leave behind four years defined by resilience
subtitle: After a first-year season that marked a striking comeback, the five graduating seniors of the women’s soccer team led the team through an era of ups and downs.
author: Walter Royal
text: The five members of the class of 2026 made an immediate impact for Yale women’s soccer. In the now-seniors’ first season, the team jumped five spots in the standings to finish third in the Ivy League. The rejuvenated team boasted a 7–0–1 home record in 2022, with a season-opening 0-0 draw with Hofstra University being the only blemish to their undefeated defense of Reese Stadium. 2023 represented a step back. While their nonconference season showed promise, with the team’s offense displaying further improvement, the hot boots went cold in conference play, finishing the Ivy slate 0–5–2. However, the Bulldogs bounced back in the class’s junior year, posting a winning overall record and picking up a pair of conference victories. This season started out bright, with a 4-0 win over Hofstra University in the home opener. Things went south from there, but the Yale squad showed promise, rallying for a stunning 1-0 midseason upset over the eventual Ivy-champion Princeton Tigers on the road. Though the team missed the postseason once more, the Princeton win provided a crowning achievement for the team’s senior class that graduates after persevering through adversity.

title: As seniors cross finish line, a ‘proud’ record to look back on
subtitle: In their final season, Yale cross country’s class of 2026 distinguished themselves on the regional stage. The team’s captain said he is proud of his team’s strong performance and team spirit.
author: Walter Royal
text: The class of 2026’s final season was one to remember for Yale’s cross country team. Last fall, the team finished fifth place in regionals, the highest in recent memory, for the second straight year. Owen Karas ’26 and Kenan Pala ’26 also qualified for NCAAs, becoming the first Bulldog men to do so since 2023. Captain Braden King ’26 recalled the team’s collective excitement after the regionals race in an interview with the News. “It’s such a weird mix of feelings and emotions when you just finished the hardest race ever in the 10K,” King said. “You find out your two best friends qualified for nationals, and it’s just super overwhelming. A lot of people hugging each other, a lot of tears.” Entering New Haven as a particularly highly recruited class, expectations for the class of 2026 were elevated. King said he is proud of what the class has achieved together. “When I look back at our time and how we’ve all developed, I’m super proud of what we built here and what we’ve continued,” he said. “We’ve really helped push this program further, and I couldn't have asked for a better group of fellow seniors to do that with.”

title: Seniors saw team through years of steady improvement
subtitle: The class of 2026 will graduate after a strong four years of performance, which culminated in the program’s first-ever NCAA tournament appearance.
author: Eli Ratner
text: The four seniors of Yale field hockey — Captain Poppy Beales ’26, Lauren Venter ’26, CC Wolf ’26 and Maddy Zavalick ’26 — brought the program to new heights. The class of 2026 arrived in 2022 alongside a new head coach, Melissa Gonzalez, and played its way into a new era for Yale field hockey. Since their freshman season, the Elis have steadily progressed, moving up from a 7–9 record in 2023 to 10–6 in 2024, before their most successful 12–6 campaign in 2025. This fall, the Bulldogs advanced to the NCAA tournament for the first time ever. The Elis went 12–4 in the regular season, earning a 4–3 record in a competitive Ivy League before falling short against Princeton in the Ivy League tournament. The Bulldogs finished the season 12th in the NCAA Division I rankings. The excellence of the class of 2026 began before their senior season. Early in her career, Beales emerged as one of the team’s most accomplished players, starting every game she has played since her sophomore year and earning All-Mideast Region and All-Ivy First Team honors in each of the past three years. Following her successful senior campaign, she was dubbed a second-team All-American. Zavalick also played a key role on the team throughout her career, starting 12 of 15 games her sophomore year, before returning to start all games as a senior.

title: Seniors leave behind a legacy of excellence on and off the course
subtitle: The class of 2026 of the men’s and women’s golf teams closed their careers with personal bests and academic honors. Underclassmen highlighted the seniors’ contributions to a culture of “family.”
author: Liberty Adams
text: The seniors of the men’s and women’s golf teams leave behind a strong impact both on and off the course. On the men’s side, the seniors broke personal bests in their final season. Captain Will Lodge ’26 shot six under at the Schenkel Invitational, his lowest career round to par, while Sam Davis ’26 recorded his single round career low at the Rutherford Intercollegiate, his final regular season tournament. Lodge earned All-Ivy First Team honors, marking his second year-end honor. For the women, Mia Sessa ’26 opened the fall season shooting five under par to claim first place at the Nittany Lion Invitational and earn the Ivy League Golfer of the Month honor. In the spring, Sessa recorded a 54-hole career low before finishing second at the Valspar Augusta Invitational in her hometown. The season concluded with her third consecutive All-Ivy First Team honor. Captain Ashley Yen ’26 also won the Molly Meyer Humanitarian Award, which honors seniors who have demonstrated extraordinary devotion to their team and the greater Yale community. Yen and Davis additionally received the Academic All-Ivy honor for their commitment to excellence both in competition and in the classroom. The teams’ underclassmen also emphasized the seniors’ contributions to team spirit. “We are all cheering for each other more than anyone,” Preston Howe ’29 wrote in a text message to the News, adding that Lodge has been a “phenomenal leader.” Matthew Lin ’28 stressed the team’s “amazing” culture. “Whether it be hyping each other up, laughing during dinners, blasting music in the car, strategizing for the upcoming tournament, or anything in between, I am grateful to be a part of this family,” Lin wrote in a text message to the News.

title: Seniors leave a legacy of leadership
subtitle: After a sophomore season that included winning the inaugural Ivy League tournament and making an NCAA run, the class of 2026 has remained a reliable presence in the men’s soccer program.
author: Liza Kaufman
text: The 10 graduating seniors on the Yale men’s soccer team “left the jersey in a better place than they found it,” according to Andrew Myerson ’27, a junior on the team. “Their leadership and professionalism consistently raised the standard for everyone around them,” Myerson wrote in a text to the News. During the 2022 season, Alex Umana ’26 scored his first two collegiate goals in back-to-back games and Aydin Jay ’26 played in 13 matches. That year, Yale finished 7–4–5 overall. The defining season of Yale soccer’s last four years came in 2023, when Yale improved to 11–6–3 overall and captured the inaugural Ivy League title the first year the tournament was held. The win followed a 4-0 clean sheet victory over Harvard in the semifinals, in which the Bulldogs avenged an earlier regular season loss, before the team topped Brown 2-1 in the finals to secure the conference’s automatic NCAA bid. On the national stage, Yale earned a 1-0 shutout victory over Bryant University before falling to Hofstra University in the second round. Although the team struggled to replicate that success in the following two seasons, the seniors were a consistently strong presence. Jay scored his first collegiate goal against Sacred Heart University in 2024, and in the class’s senior season, captain Diego Zaffanella ’26 delivered a game-winning penalty kick goal against Brown — scoring his first collegiate goal in his final game. Goalkeeper Conrad Lee ’26 closed out his career with two shutouts this season and finished first in the Ivy League in saves per game. “Zaffanella especially set the tone as captain through his empowering servant leadership, always putting the team first,” Myerson wrote. “That group will definitely be missed.”

title: Seniors leave Yale after a dominant Ivy League run
subtitle: The five seniors on the Yale women’s volleyball team leave behind a legacy of success, having won all but one home game in their final season.
author: Rachel Mak
text: The five-member Yale volleyball senior class leaves the program after a historic run. Gigi Barr ’26, Chidera Chukwumerije ’26, Lauren Kellen ’26, Isabella Mendoza ’26 and captain Halle Sherlock ’26 witnessed their team capture three straight Ivy League regular season titles in 2022, 2023 and 2024. In the class of 2026’s senior campaign, the team finished second in the Ivy League with a 10–4 conference record. A trip out west early in the season tested the Bulldogs with some tough competition, but the Bulldogs triumphed, coming away with two wins and only falling to the University of San Diego, a team ranked first in its conference. Over the course of the season, Yale built upon this momentum, especially at home. The Bulldogs won all of their home games this year except one, avenging their road losses to Ivy League opponents. In the tournament semifinals, the team won a back-and-forth battle against Cornell before falling to Princeton in the title match. The five seniors leave New Haven having accumulated a 49–7 Ivy League record, three consecutive Ivy League regular season and Ivy League tournament titles and three straight NCAA tournament appearances.

title: Following after big stars, seniors leave court as most decorated class
subtitle: This year’s seniors bonded amidst player turnover and found unprecedented success, setting the men’s basketball program record for most wins as a class.
author: Walter Royal
text: The Yale men’s basketball team has won a record 85 games over the past four years. This spring, the three seniors graduate following a pair of NCAA tournament appearances and a thrilling sophomore season win over No. 4 seed Auburn . The members of the class of 2026 stepped onto the collegiate court with team expectations already sky-high. The men’s basketball program was coming off its second NCAA Tournament appearance in three years and teemed with talent. Though pressure abounded, the friendships forged among the now-senior class helped ease the players’ transitions as first years. “It was just a shell shock for me just to be at Yale, coming from California and moving out here,” Devon Arlington ’26 said in a phone interview. “But Nick and Danny, those are my boys from day one,” he added, referring to Nick Townsend ’26 and Danny Wolf. The 2022–23 Ivy Conference season placed the first-year crew in the midst of a title race, in which both Yale and Princeton finished 10–4 in league play. Yale handled the Tigers in both of their regular season matchups, winning both by double-digit margins. The Ivy League Tournament final featured a third edition of the rivalry that season. A heroic effort on the glass, coupled with a few costly Yale turnovers, however, lost Yale the opportunity to go on to the NCAA Tournament as the Ivy representative. The Bulldogs lost to Vanderbilt in the first round of the National Invitation Tournament to end their season. Following the end-of-year heartbreak, Casey Simmons ’26 transferred to the team from Northwestern. The team immediately embraced Simmons, according to Townsend, who is the outgoing captain and reigning Ivy League Player of the Year. “I feel really grateful for the people that I’ve played with, no matter the year, but especially my class. I think Casey really made our group whole when he came in,” Townsend said in a phone interview. While the entire class of 2026 saw improvement as sophomores, it was Danny Wolf that took the spotlight for the Elis during the 2023–24 season. Wolf, who is currently a power forward for the NBA’s Brooklyn Nets, was a member of the class of 2026 before he transferred to the University of Michigan and was subsequently selected for the NBA draft. As a sophomore, Wolf led Yale in scoring en route to a chaotic 2024 Ivy League tournament. Princeton, the 12–2 Ivy No. 1 seed and presumptive favorite following a Sweet Sixteen run the prior year, dropped its first-round match in a shocking upset by Brown, the No. 4 seed who went 8–6 in conference play. With the door wide open, the Bulldogs stepped up to fill the void. Yale, led by Wolf as well as the NBA-bound duo of John Poulakidas ’25 and Bez Mbeng ’25, snagged the Ancient Eight’s automatic bid to the big dance, where a juggernaut Auburn squad awaited them. The Tigers jumped out to a 41-34 halftime lead before the Elis came roaring back, outscoring Auburn 44-35 in the second half. As the buzzer sounded, the team swarmed the court, Townsend said, adding that his role model and close friend Jack Molloy ’25 hoisted Townsend in the air. “I don’t think I’ve been picked up very many times in my life,” Townsend said. He characterized the moment as one of “pure elation.” An NCAA second-round loss did little to dampen the team’s mood, as the Bulldogs entered the 2024–25 season primed for their best season yet. Though Wolf had transferred to Michigan, both Townsend and Simmons stepped up to the plate. Townsend led the Ivy League in rebounds and averaged 15.4 points per game, while Simmons’ 103.6 defensive rating placed him in the top 10 players in the conference. All of this culminated in a 13–1 conference record, and following wins over Princeton and Cornell, the Bulldogs were back in March Madness for the second year in a row. A loss to Texas A&M University in the first round did not dilute the impact of the historic 2024–25 season. Yale had never before made back-to-back NCAA tournament appearances, and Mbeng became the first player in conference history to be designated both Player of the Year and Defensive Player of the Year. The class of 2026, rather than rest on their laurels, took another leap forward in their senior seasons. Arlington emerged as a critical piece, becoming the team’s second point guard and steering the second unit. Simmons took home the Ivy League Defensive Player of the Year award, continuing to show his prowess on both sides of the ball. Townsend, the captain, was a unanimous first-team All-Ivy selection and the Ivy League Player of the Year. The 2025–26 season got off to a hot start, with the Elis sneaking into the top 50 in the Kenpom rankings. The season ended earlier than many hoped, however, with a shocking University of Pennsylvania Ivy League tournament upset dampening the thrill of a second consecutive Ivy regular season title. Still, the accomplishments of the class of 2026 are unprecedented in the history of Yale men’s basketball. Arlington, when asked the secret to the senior’s success, gave a simple answer. “We’ve always just stuck together. We try to lead by example every year and learn from our upperclassmen and pass the torch, keep the culture going for our team. I think that’s why our team has had so much success in the past years, and I think that’s why it’ll continue,” Arlington said. Townsend and Simmons both plan to play professionally this upcoming season, while Arlington will join Stonehill College as a grad transfer.

title: Class of 2026 guides Bulldogs through years of growth
subtitle: Over four seasons, Yale fencing’s senior class contributed to sustained success with accomplishments both on and off the piste.
author: Liza Kaufman
text: Throughout four seasons of steady growth, Yale fencing’s class of 2026 has built a nationally competitive record in both the women’s and men’s programs. The women, led by Amanda O’Donnell ’26 and Olivia Ren ’26, improved from an 18-13 finish in the class’s first season to a 23-6 record last winter. The Bulldogs opened the 2025–26 season with a 6-1 performance at the Vassar Open Invitational before sweeping the Tri-State Shark Showcase, Sacred Heart Tradition Meet and Yale Invitational en route to an eighth place finish at the NCAA Championships, the program’s strongest result during the seniors’ four years. O’Donnell’s impact extended beyond the piste. The captain earned the Amanda D. Walton Award after persevering through injuries following a car accident three days before the Ivy League Championships in her first year. She also received the Delaney Kiphuth Student-Athlete Distinction Award, which honors student-athletes with the highest cumulative GPA during their four years at Yale. Ren made an early impact as a first year, placing fifth at the Garret Penn State Open, and she also earned the Kiphuth distinction this year. On the men’s side, Jack Pan ’26 established himself as one of the nation’s top sabre fencers, capturing bronze at the NCAA Championships and earning First Team All-American honors his first-year season. Meanwhile, Tony Whelan ’26 finished 16th at the NCAA Championships his junior year, while Vinni Yu ’26 closed his collegiate career with an 18th-place finish in foil this season.

title: Yalies for mental health
author: Paul Johansen
text: Congratulations to the class of 2026! It has been a long and difficult road to graduation, and it’s a uniquely challenging moment in history to begin post-college life. How do I know it’s been a long and difficult road? In 2021, a group of students, alumni and family members impacted by Yale’s mental health policies organized Elis for Rachael. I serve as president of this group, and over the past four years, your fellow Yalies have communicated their trials and tribulations with us regarding mental health services at Yale. We are grateful to those of you who shared painful stories of struggling through the labyrinth of mental health services offered at Yale. We used your feedback to create a 10-point petition, which some of you may recall signing at Yale-Harvard games. And we used this petition as the basis of a landmark class-action lawsuit, which we settled with Yale in August 2023, just as you began your sophomore years. In the settlement, Yale agreed to many of the conditions that student groups had requested of the administration over the previous decade. Those student groups included the Yale College Council twice, Mental Health Justice at Yale, Students Unite Now and the Yale Law School Mental Health Alliance. Yale had previously made no credible changes in response to the requests of these student groups. In response to the lawsuit, however, Yale agreed to the majority of your 10 petition requests: Yale stopped terminating health insurance for students taking a medical leave; Yale eliminated costly, non-medical roadblocks to reinstatement following a medical withdrawal; Yale restructured the refund schedule for tuition, room and board fees; Yale allowed students reasonable access to campus while on medical withdrawal, no longer requiring written permission to set foot on campus; Yale agreed to reduce the minimum duration for medical withdrawals; Yale designated a part-time, non-evaluative administrator to advocate for students considering, currently on or returning from a medical leave of absence; and Yale thus far has ended their practice of rejecting reinstatement applications without any actionable explanations. In addition, Yale now allows part-time study as a reasonable accommodation in “rare” instances. Yale also agreed to establish a process for appealing accommodation denials from Student Accessibility Services. We are not convinced this process is independent from SAS, however. On the other hand, many students still wait unacceptably long to see a therapist. For instance, upon returning from summer vacation, students do not automatically continue seeing their therapist from the previous school year. Instead, they must undergo the placement process all over again, unnecessarily lengthening the time it takes to be matched with a therapist. Yale has not met student requests for a more diverse range of therapists. Yale still does not offer an affordable Preferred Provider Organization option for health insurance. And Yale did not agree to implement annual mental health first-aid training for students, faculty, staff and administrators. Furthermore, none of the settlement’s changes apply to graduate students. Under our August 2023 settlement with Yale, the University agreed to a three-year monitoring period during which alleged violations of the new policies could be brought quickly before a judge. That period ends Aug. 28, just two months from now. Although we have generally been encouraged by Yale’s implementation of their new policies, any future erosion will become significantly harder to challenge once judicial oversight expires. We note that the class of 2026 is the last set of students who can remember suffering through Yale previous punitive policies. One of the largest unresolved issues remains federal law. Under current regulations, universities that provide medical care may classify student medical records — ordinarily protected under HIPAA — as “educational records” governed instead by FERPA, which provides substantially weaker privacy protections. We have spoken with Massachusetts’ Senator Ed Markey, a longtime mental health advocate and one of HIPAA’s original authors, about closing that loophole. We were very encouraged to see the Princeton Review include three Ivy League schools — Columbia, Dartmouth and Princeton — on its “2026 Mental Health Services Honor Roll.” Yale was notably absent. The schools recognized this year have “shown a strong commitment to their students’ mental health and wellbeing.” We look forward to the day when Yale earns similar recognition through sustained, rigorous and genuinely student-centered mental health support for both undergraduate and graduate students. Only then will Yale’s policies truly help future classes get to their own finish lines. PAUL JOHANSEN is president of Elis for Rachael and an adjunct professor of mathematics at Berkshire Community College in Pittsfield, Massachusetts. Paul graduated from Yale in 1988 as a member of Ezra Stiles College. He can be reached at findus@elisforrachael.org .

title: On rights and responsibilities
author: Michael Ndubisi
text: Today, on Old Campus, the University’s president will stand before the graduating class for the 325th time and admit them, by tradition, to “all the rights and responsibilities of their degrees.” Some version of those words will be spoken at thousands of commencements across the country, but Yale’s carries particular weight. Where 133 miles north, Harvard will welcome its graduates into “the fellowship of educated men and women,” Yale’s conferral reflects a distinction once made to elevate the obligations of learning and has since diffused into the shared ceremonial language of American commencements. It is a small but telling example of how Yale has long set the terms for American higher education. This April, in another attempt to shape the landscape of higher learning, Yale released a 58-page report on why public trust in universities has collapsed. It identifies real failures in costs that have made elite education a luxury and admissions processes that perform meritocracy while, in reality, rewarding wealth . To its credit, the committee that wrote the report confronts the crisis of higher education with unusual candor. But what it cannot bring itself to examine is the role Yale played in spreading those failures everywhere else. Nor can it reckon with what has become of the graduates it sends into the world. Yale's career infrastructure delivers 46% of its work-bound graduates into finance, consulting and technology. That pipeline concentrates them in five cities across four coastal states. They work in industries that have done more than almost any other to narrow the geography of American opportunity, to hollow out the places they came from and to make the kind of upward mobility Yale claims to represent harder for everyone else. For the rural or first-generation student whose family sacrificed for the degree, that pipeline is not a promise kept. For the country that subsidizes Yale's tax exemption, it is a negative return on its investment. For the faculty who came to contribute to something larger than credential production, it is a quiet embarrassment. And the trust report does not mention any of this. Yale’s report was still circulating when emails obtained from San Francisco’s mayor’s office revealed that the University’s engineering dean had been developing a business plan for a satellite campus in the Bay Area. Yale’s spokesperson says there are no current plans. The emails suggest the conversation is further along than that. The move would follow Vanderbilt’s recent acquisition of the financially insolvent California College of the Arts, in what one Atlantic writer called the national-chain model of undergraduate education. The schools with the resources to expand are doing so toward the cities where talent and capital have already concentrated. While Yale measures its national impact on a state-by-state map , counting clinical trials and patents, its expansion strategy points in the same direction as its graduates . Just days after the trust report’s release, Yale quietly revised its mission statement, excising its commitment to improving the world and educating aspiring leaders. What remains is the preservation and dissemination of “knowledge,” in a narrowed ambition that happens to position the University perfectly to become a national leader in artificial intelligence. Researchers writing in this year’s Business and Society journal warned that generative AI threatens to degrade our collective knowledge ecosystem through what they call “ epistemic pollution ” or the dissemination of low-quality, misleading and fabricated information at scale. And the University that just committed itself to preserving knowledge is racing toward the industry most likely to corrupt it. The report identifies Yale’s predicament as a trust problem, but it is more precisely a resentment problem. Americans increasingly see elite universities and their graduates as participants in a system designed to concentrate wealth and opportunity for a small class of people who already possess most of both at their expense . A university that has revised its mission to withdraw its vision from the world beyond its walls, tracks its impact in patents while half its graduates funnel into extractive industries and is considering expanding toward the same coastal cities those graduates already cluster in is not answering that resentment; it is confirming it. Most of us will leave Old Campus for the last time after commencement with some sadness. There will be tears for the end of our shortest, gladdest years of life. But the conferral of our rights and responsibilities is not a farewell. It is a beginning. Gone are the days of the University for God and for country. But a University that exists only for Yale is not much use to the country that sustains it. So if the institution no longer takes the obligations embedded in the words of our commencement ceremony seriously, then we must. We know the rights: Yale clubs, library access, reunions, The Game and others. The responsibilities are real, too, and they are ours now as well. MICHAEL NDUBISI is a former editor of the Yale Daily News’ Opinion desk and writer of the fortnightly column “A More Perfect Union.” He is a graduating senior in Saybrook College majoring in History and Political Science. He can be reached at michael.ndubisi@yale.edu .

title: Consider our legacy
author: Emily Hettinger
text: We made it. After four years of essays, problem sets and a million choices to make about extracurriculars and internships and social groups, all that’s left is to design quirky hats and enjoy the momentous occasion of our commencement with our friends and family. But in the midst of all this joy, I think we should make one final commitment. We need to grapple with the fact that we are the beneficiaries of a deeply broken and unequal system. And as the class of 2026, we must use the privileges of our education to do something about it. It needs to be said: Yale has a wealth problem, uncomfortable as it may be to acknowledge. According to the New York Times , Yale accepts more students from the top one percent of the United States’ income distribution than the entire bottom sixty percent combined. Daniel Markovits ’91 LAW ’00 says it well in his 2019 book, The Meritocracy Trap: The wealth-based admissions system we have concentrates wealth and power in the hands of a small few, at the expense of political unity, upward social mobility for most Americans and the life satisfaction of even the most successful among us. But, to me, the deeper risk is that the disproportionate wealth at Yale denigrates the very idea of the liberal arts education. There are many reasons to choose a liberal arts education, but probably the main value of the liberal arts, especially in this moment, is that they can, allegedly, prepare citizens to be critical thinkers. In her 1963 book, Eichmann in Jerusalem , philosopher Hannah Arendt describes evil as banal, that most people who do evil things are not evil people but just people following orders. Critical thinkers, in my view, are the ones who say no to banal orders to do evil things. Or at the very least, stand up for what is right, even when it is easy to stay silent. For a school that claims a devotion to the liberal arts, I am concerned by our acceptance of the banal orders we are confronted with. Yale sends a disproportionate number of its students into fields that bring wealth and prestige to their workers, often at the expense of the common good. Places where it is someone’s job to cut costs in United States detention centers, turbocharge opioid sales and invest in fossil fuels. Is this how we want to be remembered? I think things would be different if we reimagined who a Yale education was for. For middle and working class students, a college education, much less an elite college education, is not inevitable. Paying for a school like Yale as a middle class family usually means pinching every penny for years, often to end up in serious debt . And coming to Yale from an underfunded public school often means being the only person from your high school at Yale, which can be an incredibly lonely and daunting experience. Taken together, the opportunity cost to attend Yale for non-wealthy students is quite high. As such, the very choice to attend a fancy school where life is put on hold for four years, just to think and grow, is grounded in the critical thought that the liberal arts are meant to cultivate. For these students, a Yale education is a service to something much larger than themselves, an undertaking on behalf of the community that sacrificed much to make the dream possible. If we had more students for whom this education carried a strong sense of community responsibility, maybe more people would dedicate themselves to the many problems in our world that need solving. We, as a generation of Yalies, have the power to change the definition of a Yale student, to utterly transform our admissions system into one that prioritizes the public good over wealth. But in order to do that, we need to acknowledge that regardless of how we all arrived at Yale, we leave with far more to personally gain from the status quo than we ever will from a reformed system. All of us will have access to opportunities that very few are afforded, simply because of our Yale diploma. All of us who choose to have children will be the parents of Yale legacies who will have an explicit advantage in the Yale admissions process. And even if legacy preference goes away, our kids will have disproportionate access to fancy private schools, music and sports lessons, test prep and all sorts of other things that will look good on college applications. If we change the system, we directly jeopardize our own children’s chances of following in our footsteps at Yale. So, to my peers in the class of 2026, what do we want our legacy to be? Though it will involve personal sacrifice, I think we ought to lead a transformation of Yale’s culture to prioritize the public over the self. We can do better than agnostically educating a disproportionately wealthy student body to develop skills used for personal benefit instead of the public good. In a country with increasing distrust in elites , it is our duty to do better. As my parting contribution to the News, I ask the class of 2026 to do one thing: Pull the ladder up behind you. Reject the structures that we will soon benefit from and call for something better, even though it may cost your future children the opportunity to study at Yale. Commit to organizing against legacy admissions at our university, reinvest the thousands of dollars you might have spent on private school tuition into your local community, surround yourself with people who did not receive an Ivy League education and demand that Yale serve the public good over its own interests. If we succeed, we will be remembered for something much more than clever hats on Class Day. EMILY HETTINGER is a senior in Pierson College studying Psychology and Education and a staff columnist for the News. Contact her at emily.hettinger@yale.edu .

title: The death of college exploration
author: Ryan Wang
text: If you read my application to Yale, you would probably guess that I spent my bright college years flitting between economic research seminars, physics labs and a political campaign office. Instead, I spent my first week of classes grinding through applications for the Yale Student Investment Group or the Yale Undergraduate Consulting Group. By the end of sophomore year, I had climbed the ranks of the consulting group and was running its client acquisition team. Academically, I dropped my physics major and rarely strayed outside of economics and mathematics classes. Sometimes, I mourn for the kid who eagerly submitted his Yale application. I suspect I am not alone. In his 1852 treatise “ The Idea of a University ,” the English theologian John Henry Newman argued that a college education should cultivate the intellect broadly, not provide vocational training. Newman was not alone in his position. Yale’s own 1828 report on academic instruction declared: “Our object is not to teach that which is peculiar to any one of the professions; but to lay the foundation which is common to them all.” Yet for many of us Yalies, this vision has not materialized. Somewhere between 1828 and today, the dream of spending our college years exploring our passions and career interests remains only that — a dream. Exploration is dead. The question is: who killed it? My first suspect is one familiar to most Yalies: our future employers and graduate schools. In the past two decades, the college internship has devolved from an act of exploration into a prerequisite for entry. During my time at Yale, top consultancies have steadily moved recruiting timelines earlier. The proliferation of sophomore summer internships and pre-professional clubs pressures new students to dedicate their underclassmen years to breaking into the industry. My friends in other fields tell the same story. The aspiring investment banker needs a boutique stint before junior year, the software engineer needs a project portfolio to capture the attention of tech companies, the mechanical engineer needs lab experience before finishing their core requirements and the future policymaker needs a D.C. internship before their first political theory seminar. Yalies interested in further academic study face the same curse. Matriculant GPAs at top medical and law schools have crept up consistently, while aspiring doctors now spend thousands of undergraduate hours on clinical experience and research to remain competitive. Students drawn to doctoral studies face National Science Foundation and National Institutes of Health fellowship applications demanding research experience dating to freshman year. Career preparation has superseded exploration across campus. Yet, can our future employers and schools be fully to blame? I have a second suspect for the murder of exploration: us. When younger Yalies seek career advice over dining hall meals, I first ask if they have found their guiding purpose. The conversation usually plays out like this: “What is your north star?” “Right now, I’m locked in on securing that McKinsey internship.” “Yes, but what’s your end goal? Your own guiding mission?” “...I’m not sure yet. Maybe law school?” The ambition that carried us to New Haven is easily hijacked by arbitrary measures of success established by the student community. Within weeks of arriving, first-years are exposed to what “success” looks like in each realm of campus: “You should apply to YSIG if you’re interested in finance. They have a ton of alumni at Goldman Sachs.” “If you want a shot at a T14 school, you should have a 4.0 and a law journal publication.” Rather than charting their own paths, Yalies often co-opt a communal definition of success and work tirelessly to achieve it. First-semester me — the kid that raved about particle physics and economic policy in his Yale application — sleepwalked my way up the ranks of a consulting club without ever asking if that was what I had really wanted. So, who killed college exploration? Both suspects are culpable. Employers and schools impose aggressive recruitment timelines and stringent requirements, and ambitious students eagerly rise to the challenge. This hyper-competitive applicant pool encourages firms to raise their standards again, fueling an arms race. Consequently, higher education produces a generation of over-prepared, under-explored graduates. Students determined to succeed within a single field never discover what they truly want, arriving at 22 in a job they chose at 19 for reasons that were never really their own. Is there a way to break the cycle? Employers and graduate schools should ask which of their requirements still measure genuine ability, and reckon with what they lose by screening out candidates who spent college actually exploring. Absent institutional change, I find it naive to tell Yalies they should simply ignore post-graduate pressures. Instead, I recommend “strategic exploration:” invest seriously in at least two fields you actually care about, fulfill your distributional requirements early to explore the whole range of classes Yale has to offer, and ask whether your ambitions are genuinely yours or inherited from the students around you. I broke out of autopilot at the end of junior year. I rediscovered my love for economics, reneging on a full-time consulting offer to pursue academic research. The world today rewards the straightest path, yet there is a quiet tragedy in arriving at a destination you never chose for yourself. The kid you described in your Yale application is still in there — go find them. RYAN WANG is a senior in Jonathan Edwards College studying Economics and Mathematics. After Yale, Ryan will be working at the MIT Economics Department as a research fellow. Contact him at ryan.wang@yale.edu .

title: What is Yale for?
author: Matias Guevara Ruales
text: Why does one come to Yale? One reasonable answer is money. Just four years out of college, Yale alums earn almost double what the typical college graduate does, and the median lifetime return of coming here was recently estimated at 3.4 million dollars. Still, one could be forgiven for thinking that a Yale education ought to offer more than the material benefits that might accrue from the degree. You may have heard Dean Lewis make some such distinction during a public appearance at some point, and I echo it here because my concern is precisely one of Yale’s main selling points to prospective students: the idea that when you decide to enroll here, you’re signing up to get a “liberal education.” The phrase is familiar to all of us at American higher-learning institutions, but what has it come to mean, amid the onerous demands of our results-oriented culture? Can we still recognize in it the suggestion that there are at least two parts to the value of a college education that matter, only one of which produces “results” in the traditional sense? The first of those is plainly the accreditation of technical skills embodied by the diploma, which paves the way for the superior earnings elite schools are famous for. The second part, often called the liberal arts, is harder to discern. It has to do with the development of students as people — their personal flourishing, we might have said once, though that word now carries a sentimental ring that may itself be a symptom of the diminishing status of the liberal arts in our culture. But the liberal arts have a use, too. They are, in the words of the recent report by the Committee on Trust in Higher Education, “the foundational wisdom that will serve [students] throughout their lives.” No virtue has ever advertised a payoff in strict cost-benefit terms, yet “wisdom” does properly characterize the part of our education that makes us critical thinkers, generous friends and conscientious citizens. These attributes are crucial to any individual life, and to any free society, but in recent years we have begun to surrender them. Ultra-competitive job markets have meant that a growing number of students are allocating their time so as to maximize early-career outcomes at the expense of pursuits once considered worthwhile, like taking challenging courses outside of their majors or joining non-professional clubs. Even within the areas directly related to their post-graduate goals, students must often turn to artificial intelligence, dig up class materials from previous semesters or employ other workarounds that free up time for professional development but defeat the most basic point of academic work: to learn. Underlying this trend is the belief that wasting time on pursuits not easily translatable into attractive resume entries can be fatal for long-term success. But recent research says otherwise. A literature review about the highest levels of human achievement, published last year, analyzed data from top performers across different fields — from Nobel laureates to Olympic champions to the the world’s best chess players — and found that what distinguished them from their sub-elite peers was that the former showed a more gradual progression toward excellence and a larger amount of multidisciplinary practice early on. That is, they took the time to explore many different interests before they specialized and became best-in-class. The world is not organized to facilitate this kind of exploration. Universities emphasize early displays of merit over lifelong achievement, while corporations use their deep pockets to lure young graduates into highly specialized jobs. And on a cultural level, too, we tend to think of doing things simply because we’re passionate about them as somehow amateur in the pejorative sense. But doing things for their own sake should not demote our pursuits to the status of hobbies. On the contrary, when we treat a hobby with enough seriousness we elevate it to the category of a passion, and the power to do this lies, in no small part, with each of us. What would happen if we approached our education as an experience in itself valuable? For one, our efforts might begin to seem worthwhile even without anybody’s stamp of approval, and we, in turn, might become more resistant to failure. When we looked in the mirror and saw that we had played our cards wrong, squandered our time, disappointed the expectations of parents and friends, we would not give up, because our work would not be just a means, but an end in itself. Why does one come to Yale? Yes, to be successful. Yes, to have a good income. But we also come here to be students, become better persons, and find the thing, or things, worthy of our lives’ work. We come here, in short, to flourish. The role of administrators in protecting that sacred soil of our personal flourishing, the university, is paramount. Yale could do more to fight the utilitarian trends that endanger it presently. But we, too, have a part to play in reclaiming the privilege of a truly liberal education — a phrase whose roots lie in the idea that to be educated is to become more free. What could be more profitable than that? MATIAS GUEVARA RUALES is a senior in Jonathan Edwards College studying Humanities and Economics. He can be reached at matias.guevararuales@yale.edu .

title: On being wrong about myself
author: Zachary Jacobus
text: My older cousin mentioned to me in high school that he believed deciding where to attend college is a problem of insufficient information. The ability to know what you are looking for in a college, and to choose accordingly, is something that you only learn in college. At the time, this did not make much sense to me. College was not going to teach me what mattered in a college. Rather, knowing my interests was how I was meant to decide which college to choose. Chemistry caught my attention in high school. I was good at it and thought it was interesting, so this is what I planned to study in college. I spent countless hours trying to gauge which universities would be “best” for chemistry. The impossibility of this task reveals one way my cousin was correct. With my high school chemistry education, I could not make an intelligent decision on what subfields or researchers would interest me and therefore could not choose a college accordingly. I have learned that the ability to make such a decision comes through attending college: sitting in classes, talking to professors about their research and realizing I did not like certain types of lab work. But I believe his observation about the limits and evolving nature of self-understanding is true more broadly. Looking back, I was remarkably unaware of what could, and would, come to interest me. My point here is not a critique of my formerly closeminded self. I had some inkling, nudged on by my parents and a guidance counselor, that I could discover new interests. This inkling was a reason I chose Yale. But I needed experiences to draw these interests out of me. I arrived in New Haven wanting to explore broadly, and I assumed I would find something else if chemistry did not work out, but I had no idea what that something else would be. During my first semester, I joined the brief-writing team of Yale Undergraduate Moot Court, just for fun and because two suitemates were trying it. I loved it. I loved to read judges break down a situation that involved real people into chunks that the law could cover. I also loved the idea that I could write my own analysis, drawing from one Supreme Court justice here but contesting another there. I had always loved to read, write and argue, so it should not have been surprising that I would become interested in the law. But I could only find this part of myself in a new environment. Coming into college, I imagined myself as someone lacking visual artistic talent. Some people had it, and others, like me, didn’t. This didn’t seem like a problem, and I had no desire to learn an artistic craft. But after sophomore year I found myself increasingly interested in how to take a nice photograph. I decided to try a digital photography class junior spring. I am graduating having taken three photo classes, one each semester since the digital class. I am a film photography enthusiast and have learned to use a camera that “looks like it’s from the Three Stooges,” in the words of a passerby who watched me set up for a photo. I hope to take photos as long as I live and build my own darkroom one day. In hindsight, maybe this interest should not have been so unexpected either, given that several people in my family are visual artists. I now feel a connection to this legacy and think of it as something inside me. But I had never imagined this interest in photography until it emerged under experimentation and practice. Returning to my cousin’s point, it is not merely that my interests have changed in college. Instead, I feel that I have discovered parts of myself that existed but were waiting to be found, parts I could only find through the time and exploration that college provided. Law and photography are prime examples, but others include running, which I previously hated, and cinema. College is well-known as a potent time for self-discovery, but I aim to continue this process of growth and transformation through my life. Drawing from Yale and the tradition of the liberal arts education, I hope to always explore new things, be open-minded and remain open to wisdom from others, accepting the limits of my own self-knowledge. ZACHARY JACOBUS is a senior in Davenport College studying chemistry. He can be reached at zachary.jacobus@yale.edu .

title: So long, and thank you for reading
author: Milan Singh
text: When I came to Yale, I had no idea what I would do after graduation. I thought I might go into management consulting. Four years later, I’m taking a fellowship as a journalist; we’ll see how long that lasts. In a way, this is a bit of a full-circle moment for me: the first extracurricular I joined in college was the Opinion section of the Yale Daily News. I suppose now, as a graduating senior, is my time to dispense a little bit of advice to the class of 2030. I’ll try to keep things brief. Take your coursework seriously. There is an instrumental reason to do so: A high GPA is often a necessary but not sufficient condition for future employment or educational opportunities. But no less importantly, there is an intrinsic reason. College is one of the few times you will have in your life to focus solely on absorbing knowledge and learning things, particularly difficult things. Self-driven study and independent reading are valuable, but nothing will motivate you to drill a fact, equation or theory into your head like studying for an exam. You’re surrounded by top-notch teachers and brilliant classmates; you ought to try to soak up as much as you can from them. Really engage intellectually with your peers. Not just in the seminar or in the dining hall, but in public — in reading groups, or debating societies, or in one of the many campus publications, though preferably in the pages of the News. When I’ve asked classmates whether they’d be interested in writing an op-ed, the most common response I get is some version of “I want to, but I’m worried about potential backlash.” I think this particular fear is overblown, and it deprives the university of fruitful conversations. Take, for example, the last piece I wrote for the News. In response to a classmate’s article, I contended that the relative lack of conservative students and faculty at elite universities could, at least in part, be chalked up to conservatives having slightly lower IQs than liberals on average. That piece was probably my most provocative column for the News, and it was intentionally somewhat snarky. I received some emails from readers who did not agree with my argument, and a couple of my right-leaning classmates wrote op-eds responding to me. But that was pretty much it. I wrote down an opinion. Some people disagreed with it. I enjoyed reading their responses, and after a few weeks, everybody moved on. One thing I learned when I edited this section was that Opinion pieces often receive more views than anything else that the News publishes. Publishing an op-ed lets you, in whatever small way, influence discussions over how this University is run. The recent report of the Committee on Trust in Higher Education includes citations to eleven articles published in the Opinion section of the News, including one of my own. After exams finished, I rewatched Sir David Attenborough’s masterpiece documentary series “The Life of Birds” in honor of his 100th birthday. “Now, we have taken over the Earth, the sea and the sky. But with skill and care and knowledge, we can ensure that there is still a place on Earth for birds, in all their beauty and variety. If we want to — and surely, we should,” Sir David says at the end of the final episode. I first watched the series when I was in kindergarten. It has probably influenced my life more than any other piece of literature or film. I wrote my college essay on the question of what makes a species “invasive” or not. And while these days I mostly write about politics, my interest in the subject came about as a result of reading National Audubon Society emails comparing Mitt Romney and Barack Obama’s environmental platforms in the lead-up to the third-grade straw poll. There is a moral undertone in Attenborough’s closing monologue: With great power comes great responsibility. As Yalies, we have a similar obligation. Fairly or not, attending this small college endows us with outsized economic, political and cultural clout. Contrary to the University’s newly pared-down mission statement, I believe that “improving the world” is and should be a part of our core mission. We are extraordinarily lucky people. On average, Yale graduates tend to hail from wealthy families . Most of us have the enormous privilege of having grown up in the richest nation in the history of our species. We get to learn from the best scholars in the world for four years. And then we enter the labor market, and we face a choice. Take it from an economics major: Capital allocation is an important and socially valuable undertaking. It is also a very lucrative line of work. But the marginal Yale graduate who goes to work in investment banking or management consulting or at a pod shop isn’t making capital allocation that much more efficient. Our educations and upbringings have given us the skills and the knowledge to improve our country and our world, provided that we care enough to want to. And surely, we should. I will close out this last column with some thank yous. Thank you to James Prud’homme ’26 and Luca Girodon ’26, my two closest friends from my graduating class. Thank you to Josh Kalla ’14 GRD ’14 and Akhil Amar ’80 LAW ’84 for being such excellent teachers. Finally, thank you for reading. I hope I had something interesting to say. MILAN SINGH is a senior in Pierson College studying Economics and a former Opinion editor for the News. Contact him at milan.singh@yale.edu .

title: Getting it right
author: Tristan Hernandez
text: The first day I was the editor in chief of the Yale Daily News, I felt completely unprepared. All I knew was my own experience from my sophomore year: interviews with Dean Lewis, copy editing shifts every Tuesday, reports on sorority disaffiliation and Coke-Pepsi wars in the dining hall. Suddenly, I was sitting in the Berkeley College common room with the managing editors while protesters set up an encampment on Cross Campus. My new reality was answering calls and fact-checking updates, all while I still had to learn how to simply upload onto our website. I was out of my league, but people depended on me to do my job. Even though I was not sure if I was making the right calls, decisions had to be made, and the News still had to publish. In time, I learned what the job would require of me. I would give up my junior year to dedicate myself to the paper, writing class assignments ten minutes before the deadline and completing problem sets at 4:00 a.m. after the News’ print production finished. I knew more about McInnis’ administration and Yale’s racial demographic data than I did about my own classes. It was the year of a presidential election, federal attacks on university funding and international students, new policies on institutional neutrality and the aftermath of mass student protests. It was the closest I ever got to understanding this place. But while I experienced Yale from 202 York St., life went on without me. In retirement from the News this year, I nod along as my Pierson College friends recount stories from junior year and I pretend to understand. I lived off campus with a nocturnal schedule while they threw suite parties I could never make it to; my job asked me to be present for everyone else’s experience while I was absent from my own. I also reflect on the News itself in my tenure’s post-mortem — whether I did enough or if I made the correct choices. I find myself wondering what it meant to be a Latino editor in chief at a paper where you could count the non-white editors on one hand, what it meant to be a queer person given massive responsibility to shape narratives around our community. I don’t know if I did it right, and I doubt I ever will. As I reintegrated into campus life, the News kept going. I was happy to retire, leaving the work to a set of incoming editors who had new ideas and energy. I came into my senior year hoping to cram every Yale experience into the 40 weeks I had left in New Haven. I attended Feb Club every night, went back to my favorite carrel in the stacks, and spent more time in Pierson’s Lower Court than my own suite. For the first time, I felt like I was experiencing Yale, the one advertised to me in admissions brochures, even as I missed the newspaper work that had defined my first three years. Leaving Yale now, I don’t know what comes next. For four years, I went through college with a set of benchmarks I needed to meet — the News, FroCo, society. I knew what classes I wanted to take since I got admitted. But the best parts of Yale were the unplanned moments: New York trips made two hours before we left, impromptu hour-long conversations in the newsroom, showing up in the Pierson dining hall and finding someone to eat with. At the end of this year, I was talking to some of the first years I counseled as a FroCo. They’re younger and use slang I have never heard of and freak out when there’s a mouse in Vanderbilt Hall. The news I covered as a reporter happened when they were in high school. They make changes to their Yale plans constantly. On some level, I feel unqualified to give them advice because I had all these plans yet never knew what was the right decision. Now that I’m done, I find that I no longer worry about the right choice. My path was made, and maybe that’s enough. Yale is 325 years old, and we’re only here for four of them. People will fill the gaps when we leave, and this place will keep going when we’re gone. Someone will be sitting in the News building covering what comes next. They will feel unprepared. They won’t know if they’re getting it right either. The News will still have to publish. I spent four years planning Yale. When I leave, I’ll only remember the parts I didn’t. TRISTAN HERNANDEZ was the 147th editor in chief and president of the News. He is a graduating senior in Pierson College studying political science. He can be reached at tristan.hernandez@yale.edu .

title: Home address: 68 High Street
author: Yurii Stasiuk
text: I received my Yale acceptance letter weeks after Russia started bombing Ukraine. I had just turned 19 when the war started — one year past the threshold that determined, at the time, which Ukrainian men could leave the country during wartime. I was already studying abroad at the time. And so, for four years, I kept choosing not to go home — with a brief exception this year, when a change in law temporarily allowed me to visit Ukraine twice without being unable to leave — so I could continue attending Yale, so I could later use this privilege to help my country. So I told myself. For four years, Yale has been my literal home, the only constant I have had. I wrote 68 High St. on every form asking for my home address. Every winter break, when Yale closed its dorms, I found another friend to crash with, usually in a basement room. Since I couldn’t go home, every summer I got to live in another country — Poland, Georgia, Belgium and yes, one New York summer — and always worried I’d hit a visa-free stay limit, or that a temporary rental would end before I found another one. I always looked forward to returning home to New Haven. Yale gave me a bed to cry on at 5 a.m. when I learned someone I knew had been killed or when my city appeared once more in war headlines. Yale kept my water and electricity running when another Russian air strike left my family without them. Sometimes I had to pretend to be fine; it felt obscene to grieve on Yale’s campus while people at home were actually dying. I was not the victim, I told myself, and wouldn’t behave like one, at least not in public. Over time, my expressions of joy became increasingly sincere. For better or worse, Yale and its people had also provided me the luxury to forget about war, if only for brief moments. I started writing in a language I barely knew just a few years ago, and soon found reporting on New Haven’s budget and student protests more meaningful than writing papers on Said or Spinoza. 202 York St., the building of the very paper you are reading, became my second home. Junior year, I worked Goldman Sachs hours for free, editing stories through the year when Yale and the United States both got new presidents. I have met wonderful people who challenged and supported me through war news and 20-page papers, partied and pulled all-nighters with me. I got high on High Street; stayed past midnight to talk about religion, ice cream flavors, Iran, love; grabbed coffee at Koffee; swore a blood oath to my secret society. Not all late nights were spent crying. My four years in the safety of New Haven — yes, war at home really puts its purported danger into perspective — had helped me become who I needed to be. I never stopped questioning whether I should be here at all, staring at the leave-of-absence form every semester. But I stayed, and whatever I owe to that choice I intend to pay back. Soon, I will no longer be able to write 68 High St. as my address on official paperwork. My country is still at war. I don’t yet know what my next permanent address will be or how much constancy my future holds. I choose to believe that Yale will still remain a constant to a degree — I will call my college friends, maybe watch the Bulldogs crush The Game, or once again go to March Madness. Every once in a while, I will open the News to read about another student service getting cut. And I will cherish the four years when my Jonathan Edwards dorm felt like home, even as my rooms kept changing. YURII STASIUK is a senior in Jonathan Edwards College and a former print managing editor of the News. He can be reached at yurii.v.stasiuk@gmail.com .

title: The view from Yale isn’t the view from America
author: Manu Anpalagan
text: When I arrived at Yale, something was missing. There was no Republican group at one of the most prestigious and influential universities in the country. At an institution that trains future leaders of government, business and culture, there was no organized home for students who believed in the principles of the America First movement. So I started one . Serving as the founding president of Yale College Republicans for the last two-and-a-half years has been one of the most meaningful experiences of my time here. But our group’s very existence raises an uncomfortable question: How did Yale go so long without a Republican organization in the first place? The answer points to a deeper concern. Too many students and faculty at Yale live in a bubble — one that is intellectually insulated and socially detached from the realities facing the 340 million Americans living outside of our campus boundaries. Conversations here often assume that the dominant ideological viewpoints at Yale are representative of the country at large. They are not. And when our nation’s future leaders forget that, the consequences are real. For decades, elite institutions like Yale helped produce the architects of policies that devastated American communities. The bipartisan consensus around free trade hollowed out American manufacturing and shipped jobs overseas. The architects of the Iraq and Afghanistan wars, many of them attendees of elite universities, committed our nation to two decades of costly conflict with nothing to show for it. These decisions were shaped by a worldview that prioritized abstract global theories over the lived realities of working Americans. When I looked around Yale, I saw no home for a different kind of conservatism — one that put the American people first. Instead, what existed were organizations that echoed the same outdated, failed neoconservative ideas that had already led the country astray. They championed globalism, catered to elite interests and aligned themselves with Wall Street over Main Street. That is why I founded Yale College Republicans. I wanted to build a space for students who believe that conservatism should serve the American people — not multinational corporations, not foreign policy abstractions and not elite consensus. A space for what is known as America First conservatism. The future of the Republican Party will not be defined by figures like Nikki Haley or Mitch McConnell, who represent an out-of-touch establishment. It will be shaped by visionary leaders like Vice President JD Vance LAW ’13 and Senator Josh Hawley LAW ’06, who are willing to challenge both parties’ orthodoxies and fight for American workers , families and communities . At Yale, this divide is especially clear. There exists not just liberal elitism , but also a conservative elitism — an unholy union of a bipartisan establishment that advances policies disconnected from the needs of ordinary Americans. We must reject that. As students at Yale, we have a responsibility that goes beyond our own success. We are being prepared to lead institutions that will shape the future of this country. That means we must think beyond the narrow confines of campus life and consider the well being of all Americans, not just those who share our classrooms or our zip codes . Yale College Republicans, at its core, is an attempt to bridge that gap. It is a community rooted in the belief that conservatism should reflect the views, concerns and aspirations of everyday Americans. Lastly, to my fellow students, regardless of political affiliation: Step outside the Yale bubble. Engage with the country you are preparing to lead. Listen to people whose lives look nothing like yours. Because the strength of our nation depends on our ability to see, and serve, all of it. MANU ANPALAGAN served as the founding president of the Yale College Republicans. He is a senior in Branford College studying Economics and Political Science. He can be reached at manuneethy.anpalagan@yale.edu .

title: On conversation
author: Pericles Lewis
text: Graduating seniors: four years ago — before move-in, before shopping period, before the first blue book or lab report — I welcomed many of you during Bulldog Days and framed this place as more than somewhere you would attend. I asked you to think of Yale as a community you would have to practice: a community based on conversation. I invited you to learn with humility, to assume good intentions and to value complexity and nuance over self-assurance and stereotype. I opened those remarks with a question from Confucius: “Is it not a pleasure to learn...? Is it not a joy to have friends coming from afar?” Four years later, you can answer for yourselves. I hope that learning has been a pleasure — sometimes thrilling, sometimes difficult, maybe sometimes both — and that friendship has arrived from farther away than geography can measure. As you leave Yale College, I hope you take with you not only what you studied but also how you learned: in dialogue, across difference, with humility.
