I also hope that you do the same in the conversations ahead, wherever you land next. Commencement speeches usually urge you to go out and change the world. Maybe you will. But here is a more tangible and, in many ways, more urgent charge: to use conversation as a practice, and as a public good. The habits I named four years ago — openness to challenge, resistance to stereotype, generosity in interpretation — are not just campus niceties; they are civic equipment. In workplaces, communities, families and public life, you will enter conversations already underway, many of them fragile. The question is whether your contribution will narrow or widen them, whether you will flatten someone into a category or make room for the full complexity of their story. That charge matters because it is the antidote to what Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie calls “the danger of a single story.” In those Bulldog Days remarks, I shared her reminder that stereotypes are not always false; they are usually just incomplete. And if that is true of places and people, it is also true of a graduating class. The class of 2026 will be remembered, inevitably, through a few stories we repeat: the breakthroughs we celebrate, the conflicts we debate, the moments that made news. But you also carry thousands of narratives that rarely make headlines: the late shifts at a campus job followed by rehearsal, the times you cared for a friend in crisis, the decision to change majors or plans, the discipline of showing up week after week for people who depended on you. I also borrowed an image from Tennyson’s “Ulysses,” where “all experience is an arch wherethro’ / Gleams that untravell’d world” — an image for how education expands the horizon. Many of you have felt that expansion in obvious ways: new fields of study, research methods, languages, archives, data sets, artistic forms. But maybe you have also felt it in quieter, more human ways: the first time you ate with someone whose life story upended your assumptions, the first time you realized you had been casually repeating a stereotype, the first time you learned to ask a better question rather than deliver a faster answer. The horizon does not expand because Yale is magical; it expands because you do the work of moving toward the unfamiliar. You have also lived through four years of polarized, often bleak public dialogue. You have watched debates harden into slogans, and you have seen how quickly misunderstanding can become moral certainty. Yale is not immune to dogma — no university is — but I still believe we can aspire to a higher kind of conversation: one where arguments are tested rather than traded, where listening is not a concession but a skill and where the goal is not to win but to learn. That work is hard. It asks for patience when impatience feels righteous, for generosity when cynicism feels safer and for the courage to say, sometimes publicly and sometimes only to yourself, “I might be wrong.” So as you prepare for graduation, I come back to that original invitation — not to grade yourselves against it, but to use it as a bookend. Commencement tempts us to summarize four years into a single story: the headline accomplishment, the defining crisis, the proudest milestone, the hardest setback. But if Yale has taught you anything, I hope it is that your lives — and this class — are bigger than their easiest summaries. As you leave, keep practicing conversation, especially when it is hard. Assume good intentions. Value complexity and nuance over stereotype and certainty. Keep moving your horizons outward. And help one another, wherever you go, to build communities worthy of the people in them. Congratulations, class of 2026. PERICLES LEWIS is the Dean of Yale College. He is the Douglas Tracy Smith Professor of Comparative Literature and a professor of English, and formerly served as vice president for global strategy and vice provost for academic initiatives. Contact him at pericles.lewis@yale.edu .

title: Green light
author: Truth Templeton
text: Being at Yale has often produced in me a feeling difficult to describe, arising less from any one thing than from an accumulation of barely perceptible encounters. There are visible features of the institution — inherited traditions, improbable wealth, alumni who drift from seminar rooms into warmongering — but these have always seemed easier to account for than the opaque systems accompanying one through the day. One encounters them only in fragments: the green light beside a doorway, the soft click after an ID swipe, the verification of a NetID login, the absentminded gesture of holding one’s card above the dining hall reader while exchanging an “it’s nice to see you” with the wonderful people behind it. The Yale ID card gradually began to feel less like a mundane object than evidence of a structure whose scale I could sense more easily than understand. The first time I took my Yale ID photo, it was a banal event. My hair possessed a dry frizziness, my eyes carried an exhausted heaviness I perhaps mistook for some bureaucratic chic and I wore a gray V-neck sweater over a white T-shirt too large for me and sweater, the fabric gathered in folds at the neck like a vaguely Elizabethan ruff. My mom had reminded me repeatedly to take the photograph, and so I carried out the task as quickly and disgruntledly and ineffectively as possible beneath the kitchen light of our apartment, scarcely looking at myself before submitting the image through a Yale ID Center website that now returns only the message “redirect_error.” I still dislike the photograph. But what troubles me about it cannot be reduced entirely to vanity. It is the sense that this forgettable image no longer belongs entirely to me. Movement through Yale once depended upon looser, analog infrastructure. Most students carried three keys at all times: one for their residential college gate, one for the entryway and one for their suite, and even then often found themselves waiting outside other colleges until sympathetic students let them in. The transition to electronic ID systems in 1994 reorganized this relationship. Swipe cards were introduced as a safer, streamlined alternative to keys, particularly amid growing anxieties surrounding security and Yale’s relationship to New Haven. Yet over time, the Yale ID evolved far beyond a mechanism for unlocking doors. What once functioned primarily as a tool of physical access became an infrastructural node linking dormitory entry, dining halls, payroll systems, course registration, Wi-Fi authentication, library privileges, email accounts and countless other administrative systems. Yale’s identity architecture now operates through centralized databases, multifactor authentication systems, cloud storage environments and commercial identity-management platforms capable of coordinating enormous populations across physical and digital space. What appears as convenience conceals a sprawling infrastructure increasingly responsible for organizing institutional movement, access and belonging. Earlier this spring, I visited the Yale ID Center, a small office staffed, at least that afternoon, by no more than two or three employees. I asked about card production and vendor relationships. Before I had finished speaking, the staff member shook his head. Printing and issuing, he explained, happened “in-house,” but vendor information could not be disclosed. The restriction was framed primarily in terms of security, particularly following recent incidents involving counterfeit IDs. Yet turn over a Yale ID card, or look closely at any of the readers mounted beside entrances, and one encounters three small letters repeated across the system: HID. HID Global is one of the world’s largest manufacturers of access-control technologies, used across universities, hospitals, airports, corporations and government facilities. But the company’s origins lie within a far older communications and defense lineage. HID emerged in the mid-1990s out of a Hughes Aircraft spin-off alongside Palomar Products, whose engineers developed secure airborne communications and avionics systems used in military aircraft and surveillance platforms. The point here is not that Yale students are secretly participating in some concealed military apparatus, nor even that these overlaps are unusual. Much of modern technological life operates through these kinds of dual-use infrastructures, where systems developed for defense, surveillance, logistics or security gradually become absorbed into the ordinary organization of civilian life. What interests me is how thoroughly these histories disappear once the technologies become mundane. I still dislike the photograph on my Yale ID — tired expression, badly layered sweater and all — but perhaps part of what unsettles me is the knowledge that this image, once private and forgettable, now circulates through systems I scarcely understood. The difficulty is not simply that these infrastructures are large or technically complex, but that participation in them so rarely registers as participation at all. And maybe that is the feeling I have been trying to describe, this sense that we are surrounded by vast arrangements we encounter only in flashes and fragments, little clicks and green lights and passwords and scans, while the true scale of what we participate in remains mostly hidden from us. Which means that questions about responsibility, complicity, dual-use technologies, about what is good and what is dangerous, cannot even really begin until we learn to see where we already are and have been. TRUTH TEMPLETON is a senior in Benjamin Franklin College studying Ethics, Politics, & Economics. He can be reached at truth.templeton@yale.edu .

title: Times are changing, so we need each other
author: Estelle Balsirow
text: In 2022, I walked into Woolsey Hall for the first time. Masks lined the rows of the stage. Both my University and my country had a different president in those days. The hot, stuffy air doused my excitement in a layer of drowsiness. I didn’t know that linguistics, the major I’m graduating with, existed. It seemed that every single club and class at Yale was important, that every interaction I made could take me on a course for prestige, that if I made one wrong decision I could jeopardize generations of my family for years to come. I wondered if I could survive. Maybe I was hyperbolizing. Actually, I was definitely hyperbolizing. My sister says I have a knack for it. First-year Estelle didn’t know that ChatGPT would be released in November of that year. She didn’t know she would have to compete with robots to find a job, nor that there would be sweeping policy changes on mission statements and endowments reshaping campus and the United States. In December 2021, I cried in front of a computer as I opened my Yale decision. I couldn’t bring myself to press the view button. I was terrified that this decision would define my next four years. My sister, a state away, soothed me as I first laid eyes on my admissions letter. During the spring semester of junior year, I was a mess. I was dealing with the gradual decline of my grandmother, and every time I received a new rejection, the image of my grandmother, alone in her living room, whirred into focus in my head. I was overworking myself, trying to prove to a spectre in my mind that I was some exceptional Yalie that could do everything and anything. Instead, I was falling spectacularly behind. After my grandmother’s funeral service, my family held a memorial, shared pictures and memories, and laughed and cried at the times we couldn’t return to. In May 2026, during dead week, I ate ice cream with a friend and reminisced about our time together at Yale. We don’t have the cushion of the university to fall back on; we have to find housing, new friends, new people, new everything, and it feels like we’re learning how to be adults all over again. It seems that the worrying continues, like an annoying itch on the palm of your hand or a clump in your throat that can’t escape your esophagus. It makes you wonder if you can survive. In 1943, thousands of Kalmyks were deported to Siberia in the dead of winter. My family had around a hundred men fight in the Battle of Stalingrad, only for less than a fourth to come back, wounded mentally and physically. My maternal grandfather had to bury his mother in the snow and slept with horses in a flea-ridden barn for warmth, taken in by a man I don’t know the name of, who saved my grandfather’s life that winter. We learned about the proto-Indo-European language this year in my historical linguistics class. Thousands of years of language, of billions of humans who survived and thrived and lived to pass down their thoughts and stories to their next generation, created the languages we speak today. The way they did that? Through speaking with one another; through passing down stories with other humans. My family, the proto-Indo-Europeans, Americans from centuries ago: it’s not useful to say that they had it tougher than us, that they experienced actual hardship, or whatever complaints you hear your distant relative yell about to no one in particular. But maybe it gives a little comfort to say they had people in their lives to help them fight. That every feeling of emptiness, pain, and despair cannot erase the fact that they survived, huddled together with their friends, their allies through it all. It gives me comfort and a bit of drive to be that person in someone else’s story, to give a helping hand, a small hope that we can survive for one more day and keep going. It isn’t over. Times are new and unprecedented and scary. But we can reach out to others and put trust in our fellow humans to bring us comfort in uncertain times. Eat some ice cream with your friends. Check in with someone you haven’t talked to in a minute. Times are changing. We will need each other. ESTELLE BALSIROW is a senior in Jonathan Edwards College studying Linguistics with a certificate in Russian. Contact her at estelle.balsirow@yale.edu

title: Revisiting the race issue in the Engineering department
author: Deja Dunlap
text: Two years ago, I wrote an article about the race issue in the School of Engineering and Applied Sciences, or SEAS. Much has happened since then: the first freshman class at Yale following the end of affirmative action, Yale’s creation of an institutional voice policy and the Trump administration’s attacks against DEI, to name a few. On the precipice of my graduation, I wanted to revisit the issue that I spent so much of my Yale tenure considering. I want to start this article with a few facts that I recently learned. In 2015, former Yale President Peter Salovey GRD ’83 ’84 ’86 committed $50 million to increasing faculty diversity across all of Yale. In 2020, the commitment was upped by an additional $85 million and the launch of the five-year “Belonging at Yale” initiative. Now that we are one year out from this effort, I think it’s important to ask: Did Yale increase diversity in its faculty population? On the surface, the answer is yes. The publicly available database Yale shares about its faculty demographics shows, from 2013 to 2025, the number of Black, Hispanic, Indigenous and Pacific Islander tenured or tenured-track professors nearly tripled across departments. But how much of this increase was concentrated in the SEAS department? Four. Not 4 percent, four people. SEAS went from five underrepresented minority — or URM — faculty members in 2013 to nine in 2024. While the overall faculty in the SEAS department increased 44 percent, only four of them were from URM backgrounds. With this foregrounding, I still hold many of the opinions I expressed in my original article. The SEAS department has continued to not seriously consider the racial disparities in the same manner that they have other disparities like gender. Engineering continues to lack representational diversity in the workforce, a fact that has been exacerbated by the Trump administration’s attacks on DEI. And, for the rest of my time at Yale, I continued to be one of the only Black students in my math and engineering courses. Where I differ is in my relationship to this disparity. More specifically, I no longer feel an inclination to prove my worth. Perhaps this is a natural symptom of maturing, but I’ve come to believe that my talents speak for themselves. What I’ve come to realize is that, no matter if I were to graduate with a perfect GPA and the most prestigious post-graduate job, it wouldn’t be enough to change the culture. Whether my professors remember me by my name or as “the Black girl in the class” — personally offensive as it is — it won’t fix the systemic injustices minorities face in the field. One person’s exceptionalism doesn’t change a culture built on centuries of systematic exclusion and erasure. What, however, does change it is to address that such a culture even exists. While SEAS and Yale more broadly have made significant progress in terms of diversity, it is not enough. In an environment where all things DEI are heavily scrutinized, it may seem reckless to openly address something like this. But, as the recently published report from the Committee on Trust in Higher Education states, an emphasis on intellectual diversity is critical to nurturing an academically productive environment. While the report focused on political diversity, I would argue for the extension to all axes, including race. Besides the multiple documented benefits of diversity, it’s just the right thing to do. As Yale is one of the most televised universities in the world, what Yale chooses to do in this critical moment in history could have widespread effects on Black engineers everywhere. I’ll end the article urging for the same things I did before. Yale should publish racial student demographic information by major. SEAS should invest more in SEAS-affiliated affinity-oriented student organizations. SEAS should build and expand partnerships with Historically Black Colleges and Universities and Hispanic Serving Institutions to build diverse graduate student and faculty populations. And SEAS should invest in programs that improve minorities’ retention. By proactively addressing the race issue, hopefully Black engineering students in the class of 2036 will look back at this article in disbelief at the covert and overt instances of racism described, shocked that this is what SEAS was like in 2026. But if we do nothing, I promise future students will read this article and feel as though they could have written it themselves, how eerily similar their own experiences will have been. It’s time Yale addresses the race issue in the engineering department. DEJA DUNLAP is a senior Applied Mathematics major in Pauli Murray College and served in the National Society of Black Engineers, or NSBE, at Yale for two years and the Regional Executive Board of NSBE for one year. Contact her at deja@berkeley.edu .

title: Candy Crush hopes
author: William Ning
text: On the first Tuesday of this semester, I found myself having a drunken conversation in GHeav with a first year who mentioned a fear of settling down and committing to a single path. They had arrived at Yale with hopes for who they might become. But how could they decide on a specific future if they might change their minds? I sympathized with the concern: I asked myself the same question when I walked into Durfee Hall. I have never been good at deciding what I should do: Walking through one door has always felt like closing ten others. Yale was handing me a blank canvas, a sea of possibility; with the world at my fingertips, I wasn’t sure how I could do all I hoped to do. But sitting there in GHeav at 2 a.m., with friendships I once could never have imagined having, I realized I could not have guessed who would become my closest friends, or which pursuits would feel most meaningful to me. I had no clue what Yale would bring me. I didn’t know I would walk in circles around Old Campus for three hours playing Candy Crush, killing time with a friend while watching another friend go through a breakup. I had no idea I would walk to East Rock with a classmate just to watch the sunrise after five straight all-nighters finishing a random project. But now, looking back, there was no other way the past four years could have unfolded. In hindsight, I see several clean strands braiding my life together in a way that feels almost inevitable. Every spontaneous side quest has woven itself into what now feels like a coherent mosaic of these bright college years. And now we are at the end of it all. Having painted our Yale canvas, what once felt like pure possibility might begin to feel like lost opportunity: I will never be able to rush an a capella group or work at the Yale Farm or play intramural broomball again. It is so easy to let our memories shape the anxieties of the future — to believe that because something mattered deeply here, it cannot be replaced elsewhere. To look at the many directions our lives are now pulling us in and conclude that we are leaving something irreplaceable behind. What I should have told the first-year student in GHeav is that I had not left their question behind. They were standing at the beginning of their four years, afraid of what choosing would cost them; I am sitting at the end of four years of choosing, afraid now of who I have become. What we are both afraid of isn’t the possibility of making the wrong choice, but the realization that our choices slowly and steadily accumulate into a life. The discomfort of graduation, I think, is the unfamiliar feeling of having become someone specific. The canvas that once felt blank is now marked by inside jokes, routines, relationships and accidents of timing that have hardened into a self. And the strangeness of leaving Yale is the strangeness of recognizing that there is, finally, a “you.” But this is, I think, exactly what these four years were for. Being someone is not a burden; it is a beginning. The end of college is not really a closing of doors so much as it is the recognition that we were never meant to remain suspended in possibility forever. We have so much to hope for. We have so much time to turn our hopes into the rest of our lives. I hope all of the silly little decisions we make continue to make sense in hindsight — that they braid themselves into something that, from the other end, looks like it could only have gone this way. I hope we get long walks around unfamiliar cities, late-night laughs after dinner parties we won't remember, and friends we couldn't have predicted at twenty-two. I hope for the Candy Crush hours: for the time spent on things that don’t matter, in the company of people who do. I hope for myself. I hope for you. I hope for us. Class of 2026, let’s keep hoping. WILLIAM NING is a senior in Morse College studying Mathematics and Education Studies. Contact him at william.ning@yale.edu .

title: Thanks and farewell, Directed Studies
author: James Prud'homme
text: One of my closest friends has repeatedly urged me to write a piece for the Yale Daily News. I have always replied that I have no topic fitting for publication. As I near the end of my undergraduate years at Yale, however, I have realized there is something I can write about. I applied to Yale with the express intention of taking Directed Studies, or DS. The program, structured as an interdisciplinary interlacing of literary, philosophical, historical and political thought, seemed perfect for someone with my interests. DS has been valuable to me in ways that I could not have anticipated, and I thought, as a gesture of farewell and thanks, that I would try to express some of what those values are. The intellectual values are prominent and profound but not exactly what one might expect. Five values come immediately to mind. We became acquainted with the births and lives of ideas that continue to shape and challenge our thought. We learned to detect and track the intellectual conversations and arguments between thinkers as a mode and history of exchange, refinement and sometimes progress. We developed a habit of thinking against the grain of our preferences: discovering the unconventional, innovative and regularly radical origins and uses of ideas that we often assume as the conventionality of our own tacit agreements. We used rhetorical instruction as both rational and moral inquiry: learning from succeeding thinkers how to interpret their predecessors — and by extension any intellectual expositor — as participants in an ongoing exchange over truth and value, right and good. We followed reason to and beyond the limits of what it has sometimes seemed reasonable to say: The curriculum is full of examples of exorbitant modes of agreement and disagreement, including disagreements about the validity and function of such traditions of exchange themselves. But DS promotes other kinds of values, too. I met my closest friends in DS. The overlap between personal affection and shared intellectual memory has been probably my greatest pleasure at Yale. The professors teaching the courses are often prominent in their departments. The chance to learn from them is wonderful; the chance to learn from them when new to Yale is even better. I’ve had the pleasure of coming round in future years to other courses with the same teachers; these have served as continuations of relationships and also as spurs to notice how my thoughts have changed. DS is interlaced within itself; it also sets you up to interlace the years of your life in college with one another — and your own life with the lives of the people you meet within its precincts. Looking back, I see and feel these values patently — they have, as Wordsworth says of his memories of his younger self, “such self-presence in my heart.” In that spirit, I offer a miscellany of observations and advice for thriving in DS. Each semester, students in DS write nine short papers — three on literature, three on philosophy and three on historical and political thought — and take one final examination for each of the three tracks. Many are daunted by the workload. My experience has shown this to be, on the whole, unwarranted, or at least, largely self-correcting. Though the assignments are consistent — you will have a paper due most weeks — they are not overwhelming. With good scheduling — a skill we quickly developed — it is rare to face more than a single essay due each week. The rapid-fire paper-writing instructs itself through iteration; the ease and quality of writing naturally improves over the course of the semester. For the examinations, it is best to keep things simple. Review the most important sections of the texts for a few hours every day during reading period. Think of conversations between authors as conducted in the list of central passages. Use this image also for the timed essays: make a list of themes, assemble the dramatis personae of main authors and texts in conversation, assign their dialogue in the form of principal concepts and passages. Consider compiling brief outlines of trial essays. Use the peer tutors! Feedback from people in the same position, but with an objectivity you can’t give yourself, is invaluable. I was one of the first peer tutors, and last year, I served as head tutor. This year I oversee the tutors as writing supervisor. In his last letter to me, my friend Gordon Rogoff ’52 told me to “keep well, and keep deriving fun from great readings.” For those looking to collide with some of these “great readings,” in forms whose interest often lies in their not being what we expect from “greatness,” and who wish to interweave this learning with their lives, Directed Studies awaits. JAMES PRUD’HOMME is a senior living in Grace Hopper College who studies Humanities and Philosophy. Contact him at james.prudhomme@yale.edu .

title: Jesus and Grand Strategy
author: William Barbee
text: Few subjects may appear more disparate than the resurrection of Jesus Christ and the study of grand strategy. Yet, while in church for this year’s Easter services, I considered a connection between the two which enhanced my perspective on religion, on strategy and on Yale. Grand strategy, or “GS,” is a concept loose in its meaning. While often associated with the ways in which nations wage wars or governments enact policies, the term can refer to any action which requires a strategy to be achieved: athletic endeavors, job opportunities, romantic prospects. The best definition I have devised is to equate it to observing a round hole and attempting to find the square peg that best fits it. The principles one applies when conjoining limited means with limitless aspirations will never be perfect, but some may be more useful than others. And strategically, it is often wiser to make something less bad than to strive — and fail — to make something more perfect. Too often, however, discussions of grand strategy fall short of their grand implications and deal exclusively with matters of minute importance. Nowhere is this more true than at Yale, where the pursuit of perfection — rather than improvement — defines so much of what constitutes strategic success. Many of us have fallen prey to the belief that excellence might be achieved through the acquisition of wealth, influence, honor and acclaim. We see it in pre-professional clubs, where lucrative careers prove more enticing than personal fulfillment; in social settings, where peers may judge each other based on employment status rather than content of character; even in classrooms, where the rigid hierarchy of intelligence often prevails over the beautiful struggle of education . During my time in Yale’s Brady-Johnson Program in Grand Strategy, such academic sentiments were on full display. I learned much from my professors and colleagues, acquiring countless square pegs I will use later in life to fill countless round holes. But our class sought only to uncover whether a strategy had succeeded or failed in achieving its defined, material ends. Questions of moral strategy — how one might formulate ethical goals and the means to attain them — remained underexamined. It may be appropriate to exclude moral discussions from class if the goal is strictly to establish causal relationships between actions and events. But what happens when a strategy demands moral compromise? What happens when the desire for good ends attempts to justify the use of nefarious means? What happens when one believes that power is the only thing necessary to achieve change? Jesus was, by all accounts, a grand strategist. He recognized both his limitless aspirations — man’s salvation — and his limited means — man’s proclivity to sin — by which he might attain them. Yet, in doing so, he did not commit to obey the laws of power. He did not need to read the Melian Dialogue and Machiavelli to implement his vision. His was the strategy of success through failure: a necessary death for a perpetual resurrection. His was the strategy of complete commitment to leading a moral existence. His was the strategy not of this life, but of the next. Every student of grand strategy struggles to comprehend strategic lessons from the figure of Jesus. This is likely due to the fact that, despite its implicit “grandness,” grand strategy is actually quite limited in its scope of application. So many strategists seek to meet temporal means with temporal ends — victory in an election, preservation of an empire — that, when confronted with a truly grand strategy like that of Jesus, they suddenly recognize the smallness of their aims. Many graduates may experience a similar belittling at commencement. Students work tirelessly for years at their grand strategies of success, only to discover themselves as one drop in a sea of hats and gowns. Things previously considered important appear inconsequential when observed at a distance. Success amounts to no more than a handshake, a sheet of paper and a photo-op. Thus the importance of reminding ourselves that the grandest strategy of all is not how to succeed in life, but how to succeed in death. Whatever our temporal ends may be, our ultimate end remains the soft reprieve of the tomb; every strategy, therefore, must lead us to where we will be when we die, and whether or not our means of arriving there were just. Jesus’ story, religious professions aside, serves as perhaps the most useful square peg to be placed in the round hole of life. It is certainly the one which I consider to be remembered the least, and needed the most, at our university. As I close my Yale career, my final advice for all can be summarized no better than to follow the prescription of W. H. Auden: “Read The New Yorker, trust in God; and take short views.” WILLIAM BARBEE is a senior in Silliman College studying History and a staff columnist for the News. Contact him at william.barbee@yale.edu .

title: Leaving our legacy
author: Andrew Lipka
text: Last year, President McInnis chartered the Committee on Trust in Higher Education. The committee’s capstone report has arrived, with accompanying headlines . Thirty-two pages with statements of principles, diagnoses and 20 recommendations contain much that needed to be said. One can hope that the best ideas will receive the attention they deserve and find their way into implementation. But here’s the thing: Media headlines about the report have avoided its best parts, and many instead screamed a consistent meme: Legacy admissions must go. Bloomberg led with, “Yale Report Backs Curbing Legacy Admissions,” The Washington Post blared, “Yale considers changes to admissions” and Fortune echoed, “Admissions Process Questioned.” To be sure, other topics came up — notably including tuition cost — but legacy admissions was at the front, again and again. This is doubly problematic. The committee’s worthy primary theme, the primacy of academic excellence, got buried. Meanwhile, the report perpetrates an unjustified, unreasoned and frankly contradictory attack on legacy preference. In fact, the report mentions legacy only twice, principally through its recommendation that “Yale reduce preferences for special classes of applicants.” Why? It “distorts the admissions process.” In an 18-page bibliography, only one source is cited for this proposition. I applaud the committee’s choice to champion academic excellence. They even brought this same ethic to admissions when they asserted, “At Yale, the primacy of academic criteria should be non-negotiable.” They continued: “If the university claims that academic excellence is its mission, then its admissions process must visibly reflect that claim.” But much of what the public reads is instead the committee’s call — made essentially in passing — to eliminate legacy preference. This is damaging. And it is wrong. The committee was careless in throwing legacy preference to the dogs. By drowning out its real message, it was self-defeating . It should have known that the media would latch onto this. Legacy preference has long been a sexy topic. The public grossly exaggerates its extent and its degree. It is an easy target; it smacks of self-dealing, corruption and unfairness. But the committee offered no evidence to make that case. Its members should have known that this would drown out the good work they were trying to do. The public misunderstanding is in part due to the frequent mischaracterization of legacy preference at Yale. In truth, it is infrequent, mild and decreasing. Only 11 percent of the class of 2029 were alumni children; over the past 20 years, this number has decreased consistently. It basically takes the form of a tiebreaker when applicants are otherwise equal. The substance of the recommendation to eliminate legacy preference flies in the face of the committee’s own standards — this is the heart of the matter. The committee stresses that Yale is first and foremost a school, so admissions processes should prioritize academics. Legacy preferences do just this; the mere presence of a higher acceptance rate among legacies is not proof of “distortion.” Consider these crucial facts, cited by a Yale luminary: “The pool of legacy applicants is substantially stronger [academically] than ... the rest of the pool ... [and admitted legacies’] grades and test scores are higher than the average of the rest of the admitted class.” Given this, it makes sense that a higher admit rate prevails among legacy applicants. Furthermore, it belies the committee’s conclusory statement that legacy preference “distorts the admissions process.” And crucially, “The legacies that matriculate achieve higher grades at Yale than non-legacy students with the same high school grades and test scores.” This remarkable fact affirms the utter consistency of legacy preferences with the committee’s academic emphasis. A legacy preference, used as a tiebreaker, results in better Yale academic performance. To not employ this preference would be to vote for poorer academic performance — the opposite of the committee’s priority. The source for these facts is impeccable: a 2004 interview with then-Yale President Richard Levin GRD ’72 ’74. While these are somewhat old statistics, Dean of Undergraduate Admissions Jeremiah Quinlan ’03 has in recent years reiterated most of these claims and contradicted none. The committee recognized that it can be difficult to assess academic promise. Still, it criticized Yale’s admissions process, specifically the 1967 letter from then-President Kingman Brewster Jr. ’41, which set forth admission policies, as imprecise. But there is nothing unclear about the legacy preference process the letter prescribed: “Tradition, loyalty, familiarity deserve to be given weight if — but only if — the candidate has survived initial comparison and appears to be as worthy of the opportunity as any of the others being finally considered.” Just right. And what of “tradition, loyalty, and familiarity?” Do these have no value? In ignoring the virtues of legacy preference, the committee diminishes the vital assets the preference — and alumni in general — bring to Yale: institutional character, continuity and community. I love Yale; I am a Yale patriot. I love Yale traditions, in part for their own sake, but largely because they remind me that I am part of an intergenerational project that is passed on through ritual, through archive, through practice — and through Yale families. If my Yale education is valuable beyond merely earning power, if I am strengthened in character and commitment, it will imbue crucial areas of my life, which surely include parenting. If my children merit admission and attend Yale, they will bring this Yale ethos to campus, enhancing the continuity of the deep, ineffable goodness of the place, through the intimate corners of those Yale years. I am privileged to lead a community of lifelong learners, now known as EverScholar, but previously termed “Yale for Life.” EverScholar programs reconvene the Yale academic experience for alumni through the seminar classroom and campus-like milieu. One EverScholar alumnus is John Boardman ’64, a ninth-generation Yale student. John’s life is one of service and memory. To hear him is to learn of the greatness — and the flaws — of Yale’s past from one raised on them, who lived by them. This is priceless. John is — for us — an “alumni child,” and we are better for John. How could one replace this? The committee’s report scarcely recognizes Yale alumni’s existence, much less their role in the place’s fabric. Little wonder, then, that it so easily discards legacy preference. But this breaks trust with alumni, not a trust that we are “owed” something but a promise that we will be visible, valued and considered fairly, not dismissively. We trust in what Yale stands for. Its motto says it stands for truth. Its mission says it stands for knowledge. The committee says it stands for academic excellence. These are all the same promise. The committee did many things to honor that promise, and in that sense, it has acted to preserve that trust. But this piece has shown that legacy preference promotes the very values the committee champions. More than this, however, the preference crucially recognizes — and facilitates — the integral nature of alumni in the fabric and continuity of the institution. It makes Yale better and stronger. In its modest but vital current form, it is ethical and effective. To wrongfully diminish it would be not only an act of pandering; it would deny the value of alumni and of Yale’s past. This would violate, rather than confirm, trust. Earning trust can take the form of admitting mistakes when they are made. I call upon the committee to correct this error and to thus be worthy of the trust they seek. No doubt many may disagree, including many of my alumni brethren. However, if I may speak of Yale for the 170,000 Yale alumni for a moment, in the words of Daniel Webster in the case of Dartmouth College: “It is, Sir, as I have said, a small college. And yet, there are those who love it.” Do not diminish it in this way. ANDREW LIPKA is the President of the nonprofit EverScholar. He co-hosts the weekly podcast “Amarica’s Constitution” with Akhil Amar ‘80 LAW ‘84 and is Director of the Yale Alumni Schools Committee in central New Jersey. He graduated from Yale College in 1978 and from the Albert Einstein College of Medicine in 1982. He is a fellow of Jonathan Edwards College. He can be reached at andrew.lipka@gmail.com .

title: Noble ends in need of proper means
author: Ari Shtein
text: A couple of weeks ago, I wrote that “ Yale is finally headed in the right direction .” I felt that our administration had truly — finally — recommitted itself to the politically neutral pursuit of truth. And I thought it was the best we could get, that I could be no happier. Then, just two days later, the Committee on Trust in Higher Education proved me wrong . Their report can be divided more or less neatly into three parts: diagnosis, aims and methods. I endorse wholeheartedly the first two of these. Yet I worry that the Trust Committee’s goals will be undermined by its too-gentle methods for achieving them. The report’s authors are right that Yale has become expensive, out-of-touch and — as one Trust Committee guest speaker , Musa al-Gharbi, put it — just plain weird. So what should we do? Refocus! Make Yale better at being Yale — a university that “creates, disseminates, and preserves knowledge through research and teaching,” not a taxpayer-funded nonprofit devoted to diversity, leadership and justice. Diversity, leadership and justice are good things, no doubt — but they’re not what tuition dollars, nor federal research dollars, nor property tax-exempt dollars are supposed to be paying for. To earn the public’s trust, the report’s authors correctly note that we must first have “clarity, not diffusion, of purpose.” They go on to list several ways that our clarified purpose can be better served, ranging from an admissions overhaul to a new civics requirement for first-year students. These are ambitious and concrete recommendations. If followed, they would make Yale a shining example of what the elite American university can be: a nimble fusion of the liberal arts and the sciences where students are prepared to produce cutting-edge research, enter top law schools, practice medicine, write books, run for office — all with an eye still to the traditions and literatures that form the foundation of our civilization. As far as intellectual diversity and academic freedom go, however, the authors’ recommendations are more reserved. They write that “each department and school should engage in a self-study examining the breadth of its intellectual and methodological commitments; the range of scholarly approaches represented on its faculty; the diversity of perspectives in its curriculum; and the openness of its hiring and admissions practices to dissenting or underrepresented traditions.” A mere self-study! Is that really all that’s warranted? The authors throw in some other soft suggestions. Perhaps professors could teach alongside visiting scholars with opposing views. Maybe the Yale Center for Civic Thought could take on a broader mandate, more like that of Princeton’s James Madison Program . But all this fails to take seriously the urgency, scale and persistence of the problem. For nearly a decade now, ideologically and racially motivated hiring practices have been the norm, both at Yale and throughout elite higher education. A December report in Compact magazine made clear that departmental hiring committees have explicitly biased themselves against white men and against academics who study anything “that’s white and European and male and dead.” At the University of California, Berkeley, a 55-page report was prepared instructing search committees on how they could legally subvert the meritocracy in pursuit of left-wing political priorities. And between 2015 and 2023, the share of white men hired to tenure-track jobs in the humanities at Berkeley fell from 53 percent to 22 percent. At Yale, since 2018, tenure-track humanities hires have been just 8 percent white and male. I am not advocating the establishment of quotas for white male representation. That would be bad. What I am saying instead is that we have strong evidence showing that faculty hiring committees make biased and politically motivated decisions constantly, decisions which result in the overwhelming ideological orthodoxy we see on campus. Further, given the structure of academic leadership — the administrators of today were professors 10, 20, 30 years ago — if these practices do not change immediately, all the work done to improve Yale by administrators now will be undone by administrators 10, 20, 30 years hence. The situation calls out not for “self-study,” but for external scrutiny or even formal investigation. If the aims of the Trust Committee’s report are to be achieved and maintained long-term, it will be necessary to increase departmental oversight in the short term. The Yale administration should critically review the hiring practices of each department and ensure they are unflinchingly meritocratic. Departments and roles that should not exist, that produce research without social value, that function primarily as A-farms for students and soapboxes for ideologues with doctorates, should be identified and cut. Just as an open society must refuse to tolerate intolerance or risk its eventual dissolution, a new academically free Yale cannot tolerate ideologically conformist departments. Biased hiring practices are a ticking time bomb which threatens to undo all that might be accomplished today. That bomb must be defused, or the Trust Committee will ultimately prove a failure. ARI SHTEIN is a first year in Saybrook College. His column mostly consists of the airing of grievances. He can be reached at ari.shtein@yale.edu .

title: Is teaching Yale’s top priority?
author: Daniel Martinez HoSang, Alessandro Gomez
text: The recent report by Yale’s Committee on Trust in Higher Education calls on the University to “re-center the classroom,” reminding us that colleges and universities may be among the last places where it is possible to slow down, think carefully, and cultivate sustained intellectual engagement. That is precisely why the wave of instructional faculty firings across the Faculty of Arts and Sciences is so troubling. Universities cannot credibly speak of rebuilding public trust while eroding trust within the classrooms that shape students’ educational experience. As recent reporting in the Yale Daily News has shown, several instructors have been informed that their appointments will not be renewed. Many of these faculty members teach well-enrolled courses that are central to the undergraduate curriculum. Yet they have been let go late in the year and with little transparency. Instructional faculty are, in the words of the 2023 report of the FAS Instructional Faculty Working Group, “indispensable to the achievement of Yale’s educational mission.” They lead small seminars, guide intensive writing projects and provide the detailed feedback and mentorship that are central to a Yale undergraduate education. This fall, incoming first-year students will have 18 fewer sections of Yale’s introductory writing seminars. Programs such as Film and Media Studies and performance studies are facing significant cuts. These are not marginal offerings. They are core components of how Yale students learn to think, write, and engage the world. The consequences of these decisions will be felt immediately by students in reduced course offerings, fewer mentors and increased class sizes. But the cuts may also impact the quality of teaching. In recent years, Yale has invested in high quality teaching through improving the job security of its instructional faculty by offering more stability, resources and flexible appointment structures. These cuts undermine this progress. Recent surveys by both the FAS-SEAS Senate and Yale’s American Association of University Professors chapter found that many instructional and research faculty feel unable to speak openly about these changes, fearing professional repercussions. This climate of uncertainty and silence is corrosive. When instructors are anxious about their livelihoods and excluded from decision-making, they have few incentives to encourage difficult conversations in the classroom. The quality of teaching and mentorship inevitably suffers. Our teaching conditions are our students’ learning conditions. University administrators have pointed to financial pressures, including the impending federal endowment tax increase, as justification for these measures. These are real challenges. Yale has already taken steps to address them, including a hiring pause, retirement incentives, and delays in capital projects. But what has been notably absent is any consideration of shared sacrifice from the top tiers of administration. Today, as universities across the country are facing fiscal challenges from attacks on higher education, leaders at Harvard , Brown and Northwestern have embraced the principle of shared sacrifice. These universities and Princeton have implemented senior faculty and administrator salary freezes alongside other budget cutting measures. These measures signal a collective commitment to protecting core academic functions and maintaining trust across the institution. Yale, by contrast, has not announced any measures to cut or freeze the salaries of upper administrators in the spirit of shared sacrifice. Thus, the burden of budget cuts appears to be falling disproportionately on instructional faculty, many of whom are among the lowest-paid members of the academic workforce. This approach is difficult to justify in light of the university’s self-reported financial position. Yale ended the last fiscal year with a $347 million operating surplus. Its endowment grew by nearly $3 billion, generating an estimated $150 million in additional annual payout capacity. It just concluded a successful $7 billion fundraising campaign “For Humanity.” The university also maintains billions in liquid assets and continues to invest heavily in new initiatives, including a $150 million multi-year commitment to artificial intelligence. What are Yale’s institutional priorities? It is hard to reconcile the call to “re-center the classroom” with decisions that reduce the number of instructors teaching the courses that anchor undergraduate education. The lack of clarity surrounding these non-renewals only deepens the concern, undermining the trust university leaders say they want to build. Administrators have suggested that the cuts respond to “ urgent teaching needs ,” yet have also indicated that course enrollment and student demand were not decisive factors. Vague and shifting criteria in contract decisions undermine confidence in the process and suggest a troubling indifference to both faculty expertise and student experience. The Committee on Trust warns that universities are often perceived as “self-regarding, insular, and unreceptive to legitimate criticism.” This moment of financial challenge offers an opportunity to prove otherwise. Yale’s leadership can still revisit these decisions. It can provide targeted resources to departments to sustain critical courses and the instructors who teach them. It can pursue strategies of shared sacrifice that align financial stewardship with institutional values. Most importantly, it can affirm that the classroom is not simply a rhetorical priority but a real one. If Yale is serious about rebuilding trust, it must value the people and practices that sustain the undergraduate education by which it distinguishes itself. DANIEL MARTINEZ HOSANG is professor of American studies and political science. He sits on the executive committee for Yale’s chapter of the American Association of University Professors. He can be reached at daniel.hosang@yale.edu . ALESSANDRO GOMEZ is a professor of engineering in the School of Engineering and Applied Science. He sits on the executive committee for Yale’s chapter of the American Association of University Professors. He can be reached at alessandro.gomez@yale.edu .

title: The classroom is not the problem
author: Brunella Tipismana
text: Last Wednesday — an hour before the Yale Committee on Trust in Higher Education released its 58-page report calling for sweeping reforms — my history class discussed an essay written in Berlin back in 1807, during an earlier crisis in higher education. At the time, the university seemed like a pitiful relic, threatened by political crisis and the perceived decadence of national elites. In response, the Prussian government asked public servants and intellectuals to come up with solutions to academia’s existential crisis. One response came from philosopher Johann Gottlieb Fichte, who submitted a proposal for a new university in Berlin — and, it turns out, a foundational manifesto for what became the first modern research university. This school would shed the academy’s medieval habits. It would raise scholars committed to the public sphere. It would also be a refuge. Insulated from an increasingly commercial world and uncompromised by crude professionalism, young people would cultivate a new art of learning. This could only happen under specific material conditions, Fichte wrote: The university needed “to cover [students’] current expenses and provide a guarantee of appropriate support in the future.” To do otherwise was to subordinate the pursuit of knowledge to the desire for profit — and, in the process, to ensure that students learned nothing. As we discussed these passages in class, we all burst out laughing. Yes, we acknowledged one after another: Preprofessional culture — pointless coffee chats, club meetings , evenings spent tinkering with Workday applications — cannibalizes the university’s academic core. But it also seems impossible to graduate into a semi-secure future without giving in to it. Indeed, I can only sit down to write this piece because I am no longer sinking 30 hours a week applying to internships and other resume-stuffers. To be clear, I didn’t spend my sophomore and junior years like this for fun. I would have much preferred to spend that time poring over essays or doing the optional readings. No: I did it because I am a low-income student, and I knew I’d have to care for myself and my family soon after graduation. And while this preprofessional emphasis did get me a job, it also seems obvious to me that it was the single most destructive force in my undergraduate education. So, as I read the committee’s report after class, I was surprised to see only a passing mention of this side of student life. It calls on Yale to focus on its austerely rewritten goal, “to preserve, create, and share knowledge,” through measures like a no-device policy — a good idea — and grade deflation — a more controversial one. But in its attempt to “re-center the classroom,” the committee misses what pushed it aside in the first place: not the students, but the conditions they were embedded in and the choices their university made in response. So I believe, as Fichte did, that nothing would bring classrooms back to the fore more effectively than a reimagining of what Yale owes its students. For current undergraduates, the Buckley Institute arranges internships at the National Review and the Manhattan Institute, and the Block Family Foundation funds highly competitive fellowships in local journalism. Yale should radically expand post-graduate support for similar public service initiatives. Freed from spending the years before graduation chasing the spring program that leads to the internship that leads to the job offer, students could “re-center the classroom” secure in the knowledge that a cushion of time explicitly devoted to kickstarting their careers awaits them after graduation. As the report rightly acknowledges, the administration must also steer students’ considerable privileges — inherited from their families or acquired here upon admission — toward braver pursuits than editing PowerPoints for health insurers. Consulting and finance have excelled at selling themselves as the natural next step for any competitive undergraduate; Yale must use its resources just as directly to raise the status of public service careers, at least within its own walls. My proposals might seem obvious, yet the University’s approach to its current budget shortfall suggests otherwise. In addition to shrinking summer activity funds and low-income student stipends , the administration has cut the contracts of contingent faculty, the instructors most directly tasked with supporting our education. For Yale to implement this report as it erodes the tangible foundations of intellectual life is like giving a blood transfusion to a patient whose wounds haven’t been stitched shut: You’ll end up with a corpse anyway. To be fair, the committee’s report does nod at expanding public service fellowships. And because responsibility for recent cuts lies with the Office of the Provost and the Board of Trustees, it also rightly calls for a stronger faculty voice in university governance. It must ask for more. To focus on academic excellence means putting pressure not just on students’ grades, but on an unaccountable administration often more concerned with cultivating donors than knowledge. It might also mean contending with the failure of the university when run as a corporation. To restore public trust, it isn’t enough to simply strike out the word “diversity” from our mission statement and make vague allusions to intellectual renewal. Let us remember that the choice between those faddish changes and the real material conditions needed for the cultivation of thought is not new. In fact, it’s the same choice Fichte wrote about in 1807: Today, the university “must justify itself, or perish.” BRUNELLA TIPISMANA is a senior in Saybrook College studying History. She can be reached at karinabrunella.tipismanaurbano@yale.edu

title: Why so scared to talk Black?
author: Jean-Claude Pierre III
text: It is frustrating that three years since the end of race-conscious admissions, one year after the first-ever election of an all-Black Yale College Council ticket and in the midst of an intense anti-DEI campaign against universities across the nation, there is little to no journalism that explicitly and substantively regards race on this campus. This is troublesome because the stakes of comprehensiveness in our journalism are high, especially in a democratic society. The dissemination of information vitalizes our perception of the world, in turn informing our actions within it. Race-blind journalism builds limited perceptions, which — often, unknowingly — motivate the construction of flawed realities. This is problematic in the context of any race, but I’d like to focus on the need for change specifically in relation to blackness at Yale. First and foremost, we need better representation of Black journalists within our publications. While it may seem at a glance that the Yale Daily News’ racial demographics are generally reflective of that of broader Yale, representation dwindles in more influential positions. In the past 20 years, the News has had 65 managing editors and editors in chief, including in the incoming board. Four have been Black; none of the four have been editor in chief. I wouldn’t be surprised if this trend is reflected in most of Yale journalism. Throughout American history, Black journalists have played an important role in intervening on prevailing narratives within American discourse. Ida B. Wells pioneered data-driven investigative journalism to disprove misleading portrayals of lynching. It was the Black-owned Jet magazine that spread the tragic photo of Emmett Till and lit an urgent fire within the Civil Rights Movement. Ta-Nehisi Coates has repeatedly countered comfortable distortions of America’s racial reality that white liberal journalists so easily fall into. The reason for this is simple: The uniqueness of the Black experience leverages Black journalists to have different perspectives on key issues. This is most true while directly reporting on race — white journalists have often been allowed to take for granted what have been matters of life and death for Black journalists, instantiating a strong bias — but it remains true for all topics of discussion, primarily because race is so fundamental to our society that it affects every realm of our existence. This is not to say that a Black food journalist needs to infuse their racial experience into their reportage on, say, the taste of a meal, but rather that their lived experience has been significantly affected by their race in one way or another, and that lived experience often will affect how they taste and interact with said meal. Where it often fails to be monolithic, the Black experience on Yale campus remains undeniably unique. Whether it be from The House’s Black oasis or knee-deep in the whiteness of Sigma Nu-turned-Knox Club, we as Black Yalies see this campus differently, and that matters — it is extremely consequential to the type of journalism we can and want to produce. But even more importantly, there is a need for what I call Black journalism. By this, I mean journalism — by journalists of all races — that concerns itself with the intricacies of Black life on this campus. This can be applied to Yale-wide topics: What were attitudes about race in regards to the YCC election of Andrew Boanoh ’27 and Jalen Bradley ’27? We love talking about Yale dating, so how is it affected by race? Or this can be focused within Yale’s Black community: Did the affirmative action ruling have an effect on the Black community that was not captured by the demographic percentage? How do Black people feel about the Black community here at Yale? While some pieces have discussed race , Yale publications have not approached these questions with the nuance and depth they deserve — to ask beyond the “what” of the matter, and inquire at length about “why” and “how” things are the way they are. These types of questions are not just a means to an end of more comprehensive journalism. Journalism that asks these questions has a pragmatic benefit to campus life. We are in desperate need of a more robust archive of Black life on this campus. There are repeated conversations of issues of all types we wish we could improve upon, but once we learn enough about Yale life to have any sort of solution, we are on our way out and too old, jaded, busy and tired to do much about it. Black journalism highlights and archives meaningful discussions about problems and solutions relevant to the Black experience here at Yale. Data collection, wide-ranging interviews and well thought-out reportage and analysis are far more helpful to present and future Yalies than the insular dialogue we currently rely on. Thus, Black journalists and Black journalism are both critically important to our Yale community. They contribute to our journalism by building new knowledge and new pathways to action. With better information, it becomes easier to rally, easier to make change — the type of change that makes Yale a better place. This isn’t new. It’s what my ancestors have been doing in America, in Haiti and around the world for centuries. And they’ve been able to do it because they and the world were aware of their race. JEAN-CLAUDE PIERRE III is a sophomore in Berkeley College studying American Studies. He can be reached at jean-claude.pierre@yale.edu .

title: Yale still doesn't get it
author: Jack Clawson
text: The Committee on Trust in High Education’s 58-page report is nothing if not thorough. It makes 20 recommendations and cites hundreds of sources. But the committee missed an obvious problem. Yale became a prominent university because of the success and strength of its alumni. Our public trust and legitimacy are directly correlated with what those alumni do with their Yale education. So I was astounded to find that in 58 pages on trust in higher education, alumni were only mentioned about a dozen times. Furthermore, alumni were almost always mentioned as donors or trustees, never as the actual output of the institution and therefore a critical source of legitimacy. While I respect the committee’s effort, academics often overcomplicate or outright ignore simple truths. Have we considered that the first step to rebuilding trust in Yale is making sure our alumni aren’t inflicting massive pain on our country? It is a fact that Yalies are fleecing America. The Wall Street Journal reported that nearly 30 percent of Yale graduates are funneled into finance or consulting. The report briefly acknowledges this, writing that selective universities reinforce this “culture by allowing recruitment from finance, law, and consulting firms as early as a student’s first year, before a single round of classes has been completed.” The conversation ends there. This brief remark is treated as a byproduct of declining classroom rigor rather than a central fact of our campus culture and a crisis of legitimacy. Americans have watched, decade after decade, elite schools like this one shepherd our nation’s best and brightest into industries that actively extract value from the public. Alumni of this school were architects of the Iraq War, the Vietnam War and the 2008 financial crisis . The American people are not confused when they distrust Yale. They are simply beginning to see Yale for what it is. Obviously, the University can’t police the careers and private lives of its alumni. So there is certainly no way this problem can be addressed, right? Wrong. Start with admissions. Each time a student admitted based on an interest in humanitarian work becomes a McKinsey consultant junior year, the admissions office has failed. To avoid this common pitfall, the admissions office could connect admitted students before they reach campus with clubs and communities on campus that match the moral aspirations they wrote about in their essays. We need to give students the resources to actually accomplish their lofty moral ambitions. Thirty percent of the class of 2030 probably did not answer “Why Yale?” by stating their desire to work 80-hour weeks in high finance. Next, examine our career infrastructure. The Office of Career Strategy should devote substantial time and energy into recruiting for careers like public service, teaching or journalism. Seemingly, first-year students are more likely to have opportunities to interview with consulting firms than local nonprofits. This ought to change. Finally, look at who Yale celebrates. When people think of Yalies, they think of a Silicon Valley tech bro creating AI solutions to nonexistent problems. They envision a private equity executive who bought his hometown’s only grocery store and overleveraged it within a year. They picture an establishment politician who has spent years dodging accountability, likely for his time spent on a certain island. Instead, let’s highlight the alumni that are doing meaningful work and leading change. Yale’s communications apparatus could prioritize the graduates doing work aligned with the mission of “improving the world today and for future generations,” rather than the ones who are simply rich, powerful and prestigious. None of this violates institutional neutrality. Yale does not have to denounce Vice President JD Vance LAW ’13. It does not have to denounce any particular alumnus or career. It only has to stop crafting and celebrating a pipeline that contradicts our mission to serve the public. Shockingly, the first step toward rebuilding trust may be actually doing good for society. The report writes that “trust is earned by doing what you say you’re going to do—and, ideally, doing it well.” Yale says it is educating future leaders who will serve the public. The public has looked at said leaders and, rightfully, drawn conclusions about the trust they place in our institution and higher education. Now we have to respond. JACK CLAWSON is a first year in Grace Hopper College studying Ethics, Politics and Economics. He can be reached at jack.clawson@yale.edu .

title: Letter: Women’s Center puts politics above women
author: Letters To The Editor
text: Re: “ Standing our ground at the Women’s Center ” A step inside the Women’s Center reveals it is only a safe space for some women. Surrounding the comfy couches and table with arts supplies are a plethora of posters that would seem more at home at a political protest. With slogans such as “Free Palestine” and “Books Not Bombs,” their relevance to women is dubious. Moreover, their role in an organization that historically receives both space and funding from Yale University should be scrutinized. I entered the Women’s Center for the first time this week. As I looked around the room, I considered the paradox quite literally written on the walls. Here was an organization created to protect all women from discrimination, with political rhetoric that blatantly excluded many of them. Yet, according to its Instagram link, the Women’s Center purports to be “working across identities towards collective liberation.” Which identities? And whose liberation? If any campus organization should be capable of recognizing that women are not a homogenous group, it is the Women’s Center. Yet, with its overt political leanings, the Women’s Center pushes away Yale students who otherwise may have sought its support. Recently, the Women’s Center faced the choice of whether to be absorbed into the Dean’s Office as a politically neutral cultural center or to become a student organization free to engage in political activities. The center chose to abandon the idea of neutrality — and any intentions to accommodate all women – reflecting its true nature as a political group. This is not to say that the Women’s Center should forgo discussions regarding women’s issues and politics — far from it. However, rather than tacitly agreeing on a singular correct narrative for today’s most controversial issues, the Women’s Center leadership should invite students with a range of beliefs on a subject, for instance abortion, to gather and debate it. There are ways to encourage political discussion without adopting a political stance. The center was created as an affinity space and to criticize traditional patterns of thought. Yet, in an ironic twist, the Women’s Center has made itself into an exclusive space for a select group of students who choose not to question the tenets of progressivism. In its efforts to challenge the status quo, the center has neglected to consider that the ideals it promotes as obvious can — and should — also be challenged. Hannah Rubin Morse College ‘27

title: In uncertain job market, Yale students turn to new career pipelines
subtitle: Organizations such as the Buckley Institute, the David Network and Dwight Hall are providing new professional opportunities as undergraduates confront a tough post-graduation landscape and Yale pulls back some resources.
author: Michael Ndubisi, Jane Park, Olivia Woo
text: When Will Barbee ’26 arrived at Yale, he didn't come for politics. He said he came for theater and music. His freshman fall, he rushed five a cappella groups, a dramatic society and some of the campus comedy troupes, navigating the high-stakes audition culture that serves as a brutal rite of passage for Yale first years. Like most students, he met rejection. Within two weeks, he filled out an application to become a fellow of the William F. Buckley Jr. Program, a campus organization founded to promote conservative thought and open debate, and was accepted. The application, he said, took about 15 minutes. “I was immediately accepted into the community,” Barbee said. What followed surprised him. There were speaker events open to anyone, followed by smaller, invitation-only dinners at New Haven restaurants. He said it was a level of intellectual access he found that Yale’s formal classrooms rarely matched, so he kept going back. By his junior year, he was president of the program. By his senior year, a Buckley mentor was writing his recommendations for journalism fellowships. Barbee’s path is not unique. As the class of 2026 navigates a job market that has rarely looked more uncertain, job postings have proliferated while actual offers have declined, producing a market that appears abundant from the outside but functions like a brick wall for most applicants. The Trump administration’s cuts to the federal workforce and military education have closed off categories of public interest employment that schools like Yale have long relied on as graduate destinations. And finance and consulting, long the reliable fallback for elite graduates, are now slowing hiring and announcing layoffs of their own. Over several months, the News spoke with more than a dozen students, alumni and program directors across the Buckley Program, the David Network and Dwight Hall. At Yale, perhaps the most durable professional pipelines now belong not to the University but to organized programs that have moved into the space it left open, and the students who are finding their footing there. Mixed signals Johns Hopkins sociologist Amy Binder finds that at elite universities, the path from credential to career is largely unstructured. At large public universities, career prep programs and employer pipelines are often built into the curriculum. At highly selective institutions like Yale, students navigate that path themselves. In a 2025 white paper, Binder described the process as less a formal pipeline than a gravitational pull from peer culture, on-campus recruitment and institutional signals that steer students, often without their full awareness, toward a narrow set of outcomes she calls “ career funneling .” Across Ivy-Plus schools — the Ivy League and several other top universities — roughly half of all work-bound graduates end up in finance, consulting or tech, according to a 2016 study published in the journal Sociology of Education. At Harvard, more than half of the class of 2025 ended up in those same three fields according to The Harvard Crimson’s annual senior survey. At Yale, finance and consulting alone accounted for more than a third of the class of 2025’s first destinations according to a University report on graduates’ first jobs. But a growing number of students in the class of 2026 are realizing there may be no light at the end of the funnel. The Office of Career Strategy has moved to meet record-breaking demand. According to its annual report for 2024–25, the office conducted 7,391 total advising interactions, a 9 percent increase over the previous year. The office’s deputy director, Robyn Acampora, wrote in an email to the News that 87 percent of the class of 2025 reported using the office’s resources. Even as students increasingly turn to those official channels for guidance, Yale administrators under budget pressure have narrowed some financial resources available to them. In the fall, Yale College Dean Pericles Lewis announced that the Summer Experience Award and the International Study Award — two non-competitive funding opportunities for financial aid recipients — would be subsumed into a single Summer Experience Grant beginning in 2027 , citing the upcoming increase in the federal tax on university endowment returns, which raised Yale’s rate on investment gains from 1.4 to 8 percent. Allie Ruiz ’28, a Dwight Hall Student Executive Committee coordinator, said students “might have limited opportunities to engage in public service because of the cuts to the ISA, SEA.” For students already navigating what Binder describes as an unstructured path from credential to career, the loss of summer funding shifts an already outsized burden further. For years, students have had to construct, through an architecture of clubs and social maneuvering, the kind of career infrastructure that many state schools provide as standard programming. Several student organizations have spent years building exactly that, increasingly becoming the places students believe can actually turn a credential into a career. Each has a different theory of what a Yale career can look like and different tools for getting there. A think tank in New Haven? Founded in 2010 by a handful of undergraduates who felt the campus had no serious home for conservative thought, the William F. Buckley Jr. Program today has over 800 student fellows — more than one in 10 Yale undergraduates — and a multimillion-dollar budget. Its summer internship program places students in Washington, D.C., and New York on a $5,000 stipend that is sponsored by the program and administered directly through the Office of Career Strategy. When asked publicly why Yale has avoided the targeted funding cuts that have hit most of its Ivy League peers, University President Maurie McInnis said there was “no obvious answer,” but cited the Buckley Institute — the professional, off-campus parent of the student-led program — and a broader campus tradition of “open debate” as potential reasons. No matter Buckley’s significance for the institution, Isaac Oberman ’26, who comes from Wisconsin, came to the program the same way Barbee did — almost by accident. After hearing from a friend that becoming a fellow was the easiest way to get priority tickets to a speaker event, he filled out the form. Before Buckley, his only internship had been at a dairy company back home. When asked if he had joined looking for professional direction, Oberman didn’t hesitate: “Definitely not.” He kept showing up anyway. He attended winter-break seminars and met people, including someone who had interned at the Calvin Coolidge Presidential Foundation, a small conservative organization based in Washington and Vermont, through a Buckley-sponsored internship. Oberman applied, went through several rounds of interviews and got it. That summer, he was in Washington transcribing presidential speeches, traveling to Vermont to run debate programs for high school students and writing book reviews for the foundation’s journal. “It was an experiment, really,” he said. “By taking this internship in a field I wasn’t used to, I discovered skills in writing and people-interaction that I wouldn’t have known I had otherwise.” The Buckley stipend had bought him something harder to quantify than a summer salary. Trevor MacKay ’25, a former Buckley program president now working at McKinsey in Washington, was measured in his assessment of the program’s preprofessional element.
